beta-carotene from mixed carotenoids.notes: Natural plant-derived provitamin A from sources like algae (Dunaliella salina) and carrots. Your body converts it to active Vitamin A using the BCO1 enzyme, but conversion is inefficient (~12:1 ratio from food, ~2:1 from supplements). Best absorbed with dietary fat. Includes beneficial carotenoids like alpha-carotene and beta-cryptoxanthin.

retinyl palmitate.notes: The most common preformed Vitamin A in supplements. Already in active form — no conversion needed. Absorbs at 70–90%, making it highly effective. Shelf-stable and well-studied for decades.

retinyl acetate.notes: A preformed Vitamin A ester, similar to retinyl palmitate. Absorbs at 70–90% with no conversion needed. Slightly less common than palmitate but equally effective for correcting deficiency.

beta-carotene synthetic.notes: Lab-made beta-carotene with poor conversion to active Vitamin A (only 5–14% absorbed). The ATBC trial raised safety concerns about high-dose synthetic beta-carotene in smokers. Natural mixed carotenoid sources are generally preferred.

retinol.notes: The most bioavailable form of Vitamin A — the pure active alcohol form your body uses directly. Absorbs at 80–100%. Less shelf-stable than ester forms (palmitate/acetate) but requires zero conversion. Top-tier choice when properly stabilized in the formulation.

vitamin A from cod liver oil.notes: Traditional whole-food source providing preformed Vitamin A (as retinyl palmitate) alongside Vitamin D and Omega-3 fatty acids. Absorbs at 70–90%. The natural co-factors may offer synergistic benefits, though the Vitamin A form itself is identical to standard retinyl palmitate.

micellized vitamin A.notes: Vitamin A processed into water-soluble micelles for enhanced absorption. Especially valuable for people with fat malabsorption conditions (e.g., Crohn's, celiac, cystic fibrosis, gallbladder removal) who struggle to absorb fat-soluble vitamins.

absorption_structured.notes: Enhanced absorption via micellar solubilization; particularly beneficial in fat malabsorption syndromes.

liposomal vitamin A.notes: Vitamin A encapsulated in phospholipid liposomes to improve lymphatic uptake and cellular delivery. Bypasses some digestive barriers. A premium delivery format, though head-to-head clinical trials vs standard forms are still limited.

absorption_structured.notes: Liposomal encapsulation improves lymphatic uptake; limited comparative clinical data vs standard forms.

vitamin a (unspecified).notes: The supplement label does not specify which form of Vitamin A is used. Could be preformed (retinyl esters) or provitamin A (beta-carotene). Scored conservatively since the actual bioavailability cannot be determined.

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|Vitamin|Vitamin A | 201 (Number of products:4964)

thiamine disulfide.notes: An older synthetic thiamine form occasionally found in budget supplements. Less bioavailable than the standard HCl and mononitrate forms. Not commonly used in modern formulations.

benfotiamine.notes: A fat-soluble synthetic thiamine that crosses cell membranes far more effectively than water-soluble forms. Achieves ~5x higher blood thiamine levels than standard thiamine HCl. Especially studied for nerve health and blood sugar support.

absorption_structured.notes: ~5x higher plasma thiamine levels vs HCl in pharmacokinetic studies; crosses cell membranes via passive diffusion

thiamine mononitrate.notes: The standard thiamine form used in most multivitamins and fortified foods. Very shelf-stable. Well-absorbed and effective for preventing and correcting B1 deficiency.

thiamine hydrochloride.notes: A widely used water-soluble thiamine salt. Slightly less shelf-stable than mononitrate but equally bioavailable. One of the two most common B1 forms in supplements.

allithiamine.notes: A naturally occurring lipid-soluble thiamine found in garlic (Allium sativum). Can cross cell membranes, but human clinical absorption data is limited compared to benfotiamine and TTFD. Primarily supported by preclinical research.

thiamine pyrophosphate (TPP).notes: The biologically active coenzyme form of B1 (also called cocarboxylase). While it's the form your body actually uses, oral TPP is largely hydrolyzed in the GI tract before absorption, reducing its advantage over standard forms.

thiamine tetrahydrofurfuryl disulfide (T.notes: A highly bioavailable synthetic lipid-soluble thiamine derivative (brand name: Alinamin). Widely used in Japan for rapid thiamine repletion. Excellent membrane permeability and tissue delivery, with strong clinical evidence for neurological support.

thiamine from brewer's yeast.notes: Thiamine from a whole-food yeast matrix. Contains naturally co-occurring B vitamins and cofactors. However, no strong evidence that the food matrix significantly enhances B1 absorption over isolated forms.

thiamine oxide.notes: An oxidized, degraded form of thiamine with very poor bioactivity. If your supplement contains this form, the thiamine has likely been damaged by heat or oxygen exposure during manufacturing or storage.

thiamine analog.notes: A structurally similar but biologically inactive molecule that cannot function as vitamin B1 in the body. Provides no nutritional benefit. Found as a contaminant or degradation product, never intentionally used.

thiamine with thiaminase.notes: Thiamine that has been destroyed by thiaminase enzymes (found in raw fish, some plants, and certain bacteria). The resulting fragments cannot function as vitamin B1. Not a supplement form — represents degraded thiamine.

vitamin b1 (unspecified).notes: The supplement label does not specify which form of thiamine (B1) is used. Most commonly this means thiamine HCl or mononitrate, but the exact form and bioavailability cannot be confirmed.

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|TBD|Thiamin | 2241 (Number of products:66)

riboflavin-5-phosphate.notes: The active coenzyme form of B2 (also called FMN — flavin mononucleotide). Your body uses this form directly without conversion. Ideal for people with impaired riboflavin metabolism or who want the most bioavailable form.

riboflavin.notes: The standard form of vitamin B2 used in most supplements and fortified foods. Your body must convert it to the active forms FMN and FAD before use. Well-absorbed and effective for general B2 supplementation.

riboflavin from yeast.notes: Riboflavin from a whole-food yeast source, providing naturally co-occurring B vitamins. The food matrix adds nutritional context but has not been shown to significantly enhance B2 absorption over standard riboflavin.

flavin adenine dinucleotide (FAD).notes: An active coenzyme form of B2 involved in hundreds of metabolic reactions. However, oral FAD is broken down (dephosphorylated) in the gut before absorption and must be reconverted in your cells, reducing its theoretical advantage.

riboflavin analog.notes: A structurally similar but biologically inactive molecule. Cannot function as vitamin B2 in the body and provides no nutritional benefit.

light-degraded riboflavin.notes: Riboflavin that has been destroyed by UV light exposure. B2 is one of the most light-sensitive vitamins — degraded riboflavin has drastically reduced potency and effectiveness.

vitamin b2 (unspecified).notes: The supplement label does not specify which form of riboflavin (B2) is used. Most likely standard riboflavin, but the exact form cannot be confirmed.

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|Vitamin|Riboflavin (B2) | 203 (Number of products:536)

nicotinamide riboside.notes: An advanced NAD+ precursor (brand name: Niagen/TruNiagen). Efficiently raises cellular NAD+ levels without causing the niacin flush. Widely studied in longevity and metabolic health research.

nicotinamide mononucleotide.notes: A direct NAD+ precursor (NMN) popular in anti-aging research. Converts to NAD+ in one enzymatic step. Highly absorbable, with growing clinical evidence for energy metabolism and cellular repair.

inositol hexanicotinate.notes: A flush-free niacin form made of six niacin molecules bound to inositol. Releases niacin slowly, avoiding the uncomfortable vasodilation ('niacin flush'). Supports lipid metabolism without skin flushing.

niacinamide.notes: The non-flushing amide form of B3. Does not cause vasodilation. Preferred for skin health, cellular repair, and general B3 supplementation. Does NOT have the same lipid-lowering effects as nicotinic acid.

niacinamide ascorbate (b3).notes: Compound salt delivering niacinamide (B3 component) and ascorbate (C component). When the product context indicates B3, routes here. Niacinamide is well-absorbed from this compound.

nicotinic acid.notes: The original, traditional form of niacin. Highly effective for raising HDL cholesterol and lowering triglycerides, but causes the 'niacin flush' (skin redness, warmth, tingling) at higher doses due to prostaglandin release.

tryptophan_derived_niacin.notes: Niacin produced naturally in your body from the amino acid tryptophan. The conversion is very inefficient (~60 mg tryptophan → 1 mg niacin) and requires B6, iron, and riboflavin as cofactors.

niacin analog.notes: A structurally similar but biologically inactive molecule. Cannot function as vitamin B3 and provides no nutritional benefit.

heat-degraded niacin.notes: Niacin that has lost potency due to prolonged high-temperature processing. While niacin is relatively heat-stable, extreme or prolonged heat can cause significant degradation.

vitamin b3 (unspecified).notes: The supplement label does not specify which form of niacin (B3) is used. Could be nicotinic acid (may cause flushing) or niacinamide (flush-free). The exact form cannot be confirmed.

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|Vitamin|Niacin (Vitamin B3) | 204 (Number of products:661)

calcium pantothenate.notes: The most common B5 supplement form — calcium salt of pantothenic acid. Very shelf-stable, well-absorbed, and widely used in multivitamins and B-complex formulas. Provides ~92% pantothenic acid by weight.

pantethine.notes: The active, reduced form of pantothenic acid — a direct precursor to Coenzyme A (CoA). Superior to standard pantothenic acid for supporting lipid metabolism. Clinical studies show it can help maintain healthy cholesterol levels.

pantothenic acid.notes: The basic free-acid form of vitamin B5. Requires conversion to Coenzyme A (CoA) in the body for biological activity. Less shelf-stable than the calcium salt form but equally effective when fresh.

sodium pantothenate.notes: The sodium salt of pantothenic acid. Less commonly used than calcium pantothenate and somewhat less stable. Found in some liquid and injectable B5 formulations.

panthenol (pro-vitamin B5).notes: A pro-vitamin form of B5 (dexpanthenol) primarily used in topical skincare and haircare products. Not ideal for oral supplementation due to low systemic conversion. Best known for wound healing and skin hydration when applied topically.

pantothenic_analog.notes: A structurally similar but biologically inactive molecule. Cannot function as vitamin B5 and provides no nutritional benefit.

vitamin b5 (unspecified).notes: The supplement label does not specify which form of pantothenic acid (B5) is used. Most commonly calcium pantothenate in standard supplements.

d-pantothenic acid.notes: The natural D-enantiomer — the only biologically active form of pantothenic acid. Your body uses exclusively D-pantothenate for Coenzyme A synthesis. Superior to racemic (DL) mixtures, which contain 50% inactive L-form.

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|vitamin|Vitamin B5 (Pantothenic Acid) | 2547 (Number of products:2298)

pyridoxal-5-phosphate (P5P).notes: The biologically active coenzyme form of B6. Your body uses P5P directly in over 100 enzymatic reactions. Bypasses the liver conversion step required by other B6 forms — ideal for people with impaired conversion or liver issues.

pyridoxamine.notes: A natural form of B6 found in animal-derived foods. Must be converted to the active P5P form in the body. Less common in supplements than pyridoxine HCl but can support enzymatic reactions after conversion.

pyridoxine hydrochloride.notes: The most common synthetic B6 form in supplements and fortified foods. Requires liver conversion to active P5P, which can be inefficient in some individuals (especially those with liver conditions or certain genetic variants).

pyridoxine glutamate.notes: A rare B6 form combining pyridoxine with the amino acid glutamate. Less bioavailable than P5P and less commonly used than pyridoxine HCl. Generally not preferred for broad supplementation.

pyridoxine_oxide.notes: An oxidized, degraded form of vitamin B6 with very poor bioactivity. Indicates the B6 has been damaged by heat or oxygen exposure.

pyridoxine_analog.notes: A structurally similar but biologically inactive molecule. Cannot function as vitamin B6 and provides no nutritional benefit.

vitamin b6 (unspecified).notes: The supplement label does not specify which form of vitamin B6 is used. Most commonly pyridoxine HCl in standard supplements. The active P5P form would typically be explicitly stated on the label.

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|Vitamin|Pyridoxine (Vitamin B6) | 110 (Number of products:1286)

d-biotin.notes: The natural, biologically active form of biotin (the D-enantiomer). This is what your body actually uses for metabolizing fats, carbohydrates, and proteins. The vast majority of commercial biotin supplements contain d-biotin. Top-tier B7 form.

biotin from yeast.notes: Biotin from a whole-food yeast matrix with naturally co-occurring B vitamins and cofactors. Provides d-biotin in a food-based context, but no strong clinical evidence that the yeast matrix enhances biotin absorption.

protein_bound_biotin.notes: Biotin bound to proteins (as biocytin), the form naturally found in foods like eggs, liver, and nuts. Requires the enzyme biotinidase to release free biotin for absorption. People with biotinidase deficiency may poorly absorb this form.

biotin_analog.notes: A structurally similar but biologically inactive molecule. Cannot function as biotin in the body and provides no nutritional benefit.

biotin degraded by avidin.notes: Biotin irreversibly bound by avidin, a protein in raw egg whites. The avidin-biotin bond is one of the strongest in nature — once bound, the biotin cannot be absorbed. Cooking eggs denatures avidin and prevents this problem.

vitamin b7 (unspecified).notes: The supplement label does not specify which form of biotin (B7) is used. Most commercial biotin supplements use d-biotin, but without explicit labeling, the exact form cannot be confirmed.

dl-biotin (racemic).notes: A racemic (50/50) mixture of active d-biotin and inactive l-biotin. Only half the molecule is biologically useful. Rarely used in modern supplements — most have switched to pure d-biotin. Significantly less effective per milligram.

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|Vitamin|Biotin | 22 (Number of products:127)

5-methyltetrahydrofolate (5-MTHF).notes: The biologically active form of folate — the exact molecule your body uses for DNA synthesis, methylation, and neurotransmitter production. Bypasses the MTHFR enzyme entirely, making it essential for the ~40% of people with MTHFR gene variants who poorly convert folic acid.

calcium folinate.notes: Folinic acid (leucovorin) — an active folate that readily converts to 5-MTHF in the body. Used medically to rescue cells from methotrexate toxicity. A good alternative active folate for people who don't tolerate methylfolate.

quatrefolic.notes: A patented glucosamine salt of 5-MTHF with superior stability and shelf life. Structurally identical active folate molecule to generic 5-MTHF, but the glucosamine salt form provides better manufacturing stability and dissolution.

metafolin.notes: A patented calcium salt of L-5-MTHF developed by Merck. One of the first branded active folate supplements. Identical active molecule to generic 5-MTHF, with the calcium salt providing good stability.

folic acid.notes: The synthetic, inactive form of folate used in most cheap supplements and fortified foods. Must be converted through multiple enzymatic steps to become active 5-MTHF. Up to 40% of people carry MTHFR variants that impair this conversion, potentially leading to unmetabolized folic acid buildup.
aliases: folic acid from organic lemon peel

food folate (polyglutamate).notes: The natural form of folate found in leafy greens, legumes, and liver. Present as polyglutamates that must be enzymatically cleaved to monoglutamate for absorption. About 50% less bioavailable than folic acid due to incomplete deconjugation.

folate from yeast.notes: Folate from a whole-food yeast fermentation process. Provides folate in a food-matrix context with co-occurring B vitamins. Generally well-absorbed, though specific bioavailability data vs isolated forms is limited.
aliases: whole food folate from yeast supplement

folate_analog.notes: A structurally similar but biologically inactive molecule. Cannot function as folate and provides no nutritional benefit.

heat_degraded_folate.notes: Folate damaged by heat exposure. Folate is one of the most heat-sensitive B vitamins — cooking can destroy 50–90% of food folate content. Degraded folate has drastically reduced biological activity.

vitamin b9 (unspecified).notes: The supplement label does not specify which form of folate (B9) is used. Could be synthetic folic acid or active methylfolate — a critical distinction for people with MTHFR gene variants.

vitamin_b9_folate.cui_note: Folic acid is the closest ingredient-level UMLS concept for folate supplementation.

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|vitamin|Vitamin B9 (folic acid) | 3132 (Number of products:6858)

methylcobalamin sublingual.notes: The gold standard for B12 supplementation. Methylcobalamin is the active methyl form your body uses directly for nerve function and methylation. Sublingual delivery bypasses the GI tract and intrinsic factor requirement entirely — critical for people with absorption issues, pernicious anemia, or aging-related IF decline.

methylcobalamin.notes: The bioactive methyl form of B12 — used directly by your cells without conversion. Supports nerve health, brain function, and methylation pathways. Preferred over cyanocobalamin for people with MTHFR variants. Does not require intrinsic factor conversion, only GI absorption.

adenosylcobalamin.notes: The mitochondrial active form of B12 (also called dibencozide or coenzyme B12). Essential for energy production in the Krebs cycle. The form your mitochondria actually use. Less common in supplements than methylcobalamin but equally important biologically.

hydroxocobalamin.notes: A natural form of B12 with excellent tissue retention — stays in the body longer than cyanocobalamin. Used medically for cyanide poisoning treatment and B12 injections. Converts to both methylcobalamin and adenosylcobalamin as needed.

cyanocobalamin sublingual.notes: Synthetic B12 with sublingual delivery to improve absorption. While sublingual bypasses some GI barriers, cyanocobalamin still requires liver conversion to active forms and releases a tiny amount of cyanide during metabolism.

cyanocobalamin.notes: The most common and cheapest synthetic B12 form. Contains a cyanide group that must be removed during metabolism (the amount is toxicologically insignificant). Requires liver conversion to active methylcobalamin or adenosylcobalamin. The most clinically studied form.

b12 (unspecified).notes: The supplement label does not specify which form of B12 is used. Cheap supplements typically use cyanocobalamin; premium ones use methylcobalamin or adenosylcobalamin. The form matters significantly for people with MTHFR variants or absorption issues.

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|vitamin|Vitamin B12 (unspecified) | 2696 (Number of products:2012)

liposomal vitamin C.notes: The most bioavailable oral form of vitamin C. Phospholipid liposomes protect ascorbic acid through the GI tract and deliver it directly into cells. Clinical studies show ~1.5x higher plasma levels vs standard vitamin C. Premium delivery technology.

ester-C (calcium ascorbate).notes: A patented buffered vitamin C containing calcium ascorbate plus vitamin C metabolites (threonate, xylonate, lyxonate). Non-acidic and gentle on the stomach. Studies suggest faster absorption and longer cellular retention than plain ascorbic acid.

calcium ascorbate.notes: A buffered, non-acidic form of vitamin C combined with calcium. Gentle on the stomach — a good choice for people who experience GI upset from regular ascorbic acid. Provides a small amount of supplemental calcium.

sodium ascorbate.notes: A buffered, non-acidic form of vitamin C combined with sodium. Gentle on the stomach with the same bioavailability as standard ascorbic acid. Commonly used in IV vitamin C protocols. Note: contributes dietary sodium.

magnesium ascorbate.notes: A buffered form combining vitamin C with magnesium. Non-acidic and gentle on the stomach. Provides both vitamin C and a small amount of highly absorbable magnesium. Same vitamin C bioavailability as standard ascorbic acid. Also provides magnesium — see magnesium entry for that nutrient's scoring.

ascorbic acid.notes: The standard, most widely studied form of vitamin C. Highly bioavailable at normal doses (200 mg: ~90% absorbed). Acidic — may cause stomach upset in sensitive individuals at high doses. The benchmark against which all other forms are compared.

ascorbyl palmitate.notes: A fat-soluble form of vitamin C created by bonding ascorbic acid to palmitic acid. Primarily used as a preservative/antioxidant in skincare and food products. Less effective for raising blood vitamin C levels than water-soluble forms when taken orally.

vitamin C with bioflavonoids.notes: Vitamin C paired with natural plant bioflavonoids (hesperidin, rutin, quercetin). The bioflavonoids enhance vitamin C absorption and provide their own antioxidant benefits. A synergistic combination found naturally in citrus fruits.

camu camu extract.notes: A whole-food vitamin C source from the Amazonian camu camu berry — one of the most vitamin C-dense fruits on Earth. Contains natural bioflavonoids, carotenoids, and phytochemicals that may enhance absorption and provide antioxidant synergy.

acerola cherry extract.notes: A natural, whole-food vitamin C from acerola cherries. Contains naturally co-occurring bioflavonoids, carotenoids, and other phytonutrients. One of the richest natural sources of vitamin C with excellent synergistic cofactors.

potassium ascorbate.notes: A buffered form of vitamin C combined with potassium. Non-acidic and stomach-friendly. Less commonly used than sodium or calcium ascorbate. Provides supplemental potassium alongside vitamin C.

zinc ascorbate.notes: A combined form providing both vitamin C and zinc in a single molecule. Both nutrients support immune function synergistically. The zinc is well-absorbed in this chelated form.

slow-release vitamin C.notes: A timed-release formulation designed to deliver vitamin C gradually over several hours. May reduce peak plasma levels compared to immediate-release forms but provides sustained low-level absorption. Useful for maintaining steady blood levels.

dehydroascorbic acid.notes: The oxidized form of vitamin C. While your body can recycle small amounts back to active ascorbic acid, dehydroascorbic acid is unstable and degrades quickly. Indicates the vitamin C has been partially oxidized — reduced potency.

vitamin C analogs (non-functional).notes: Structurally similar molecules that cannot function as vitamin C in the body. Provide no antioxidant or nutritional benefit. May appear as degradation products in poorly stored supplements.

vitamin c (unspecified).notes: The supplement label does not specify which form of vitamin C is used. Most commonly ascorbic acid. Buffered forms (calcium/sodium ascorbate) and liposomal forms would typically be explicitly stated.

niacinamide ascorbate.notes: A combined salt of niacinamide (vitamin B3) and ascorbic acid (vitamin C). Provides both vitamins in one molecule with moderate bioavailability for each. The niacinamide component is flush-free.

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|vitamin|Vitamin C (mixed) | 3376 (Number of products:376)

cholecalciferol (D3).notes: The gold standard form of vitamin D — identical to what your skin produces from sunlight. D3 raises and maintains blood 25(OH)D levels significantly better than D2. The most studied and recommended form by clinical guidelines worldwide.

calcidiol (25-hydroxy D3).notes: The pre-activated form of vitamin D (25-hydroxyvitamin D3). Bypasses the liver conversion step, producing faster and more consistent blood level increases. Especially valuable for people with liver issues or poor CYP2R1 enzyme activity. The form measured in blood tests.

micellized D3.notes: D3 processed into water-soluble micelles for enhanced absorption without requiring dietary fat. Especially important for people with fat malabsorption (Crohn's, celiac, cystic fibrosis, post-bariatric surgery) who struggle to absorb standard fat-soluble D3.

liposomal D3.notes: D3 encapsulated in phospholipid liposomes for improved cellular uptake. Protects the vitamin through the GI tract and delivers it efficiently. A premium delivery format, though head-to-head data vs standard D3-in-oil is limited.

microencapsulated D3.notes: D3 coated in a protective shell (typically starch or gum) for improved shelf stability. Useful in powdered and tablet formulations where oil-based D3 isn't practical. Does not significantly enhance absorption beyond standard D3.

absorption_structured.notes: Microencapsulation protects D3 from degradation but does not significantly enhance absorption compared to standard D3 in oil.

vitamin D3 from lichen.notes: The only vegan source of true vitamin D3 (cholecalciferol). Extracted from lichen, a symbiotic algae-fungus organism. Molecularly identical and bioequivalent to animal-derived D3. The go-to choice for vegans and vegetarians who want D3 instead of D2.

ergocalciferol (D2).notes: The plant/fungal form of vitamin D. A 2024 meta-analysis of 20 studies confirms D3 raises blood levels 15.69 nmol/L higher than D2. D2 has a shorter half-life and lower potency per microgram. Only recommended when D3 is not an option (strict vegan preference).

vitamin D2 (unspecified source).notes: Vitamin D2 from an unspecified source — typically irradiated yeast or mushrooms. Less reliable than D3 at raising blood levels, with shorter duration of action. Scored conservatively due to D2's known inferiority and unspecified sourcing.

vitamin D2 from UV-treated mushrooms.notes: D2 produced by exposing mushrooms to ultraviolet light, mimicking natural sunlight conversion. Approved for food fortification in the EU. A natural vegan option, but still D2 — less potent than D3 at maintaining blood levels long-term.

vitamin D analogs (non-functional).notes: Structurally similar molecules that cannot function as vitamin D in the body. May interfere with vitamin D receptors without providing biological activity. Provides no nutritional benefit.

oxidized vitamin D.notes: Vitamin D that has been destroyed by light, heat, or oxygen exposure. Completely inactive — provides no vitamin D benefit. Indicates poor manufacturing, storage, or an expired product.

vitamin d (unspecified).notes: The supplement label does not specify D2 or D3. This matters significantly — D3 is far more effective at raising blood levels. Most quality supplements use D3; cheap or plant-based products may use the inferior D2 form.

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|vitamin|Vitamin D (unspecified) | 3120 (Number of products:1638)

mixed tocopherols.notes: The full-spectrum natural vitamin E containing all four tocopherols (alpha, beta, gamma, delta). Provides broader antioxidant protection than alpha-tocopherol alone. Gamma-tocopherol in particular offers unique anti-inflammatory benefits not found in alpha-only supplements.
aliases: alpha beta gamma delta tocopherols supplement

d-alpha-tocopherol.notes: The natural RRR form of alpha-tocopherol — your body's preferred vitamin E. The alpha-tocopherol transfer protein (α-TTP) in the liver selectively retains this natural form, giving it ~2x the biological activity of synthetic dl-alpha-tocopherol.

tocotrienols.notes: A distinct class of vitamin E found in palm oil, rice bran, and annatto. Emerging research shows tocotrienols (especially delta and gamma forms) have potent antioxidant, neuroprotective, and cholesterol-lowering properties that tocopherols lack. A cutting-edge vitamin E category.
aliases: alpha beta gamma delta tocotrienols supplement

d-alpha-tocopheryl acetate.notes: The natural alpha-tocopherol esterified with acetic acid for stability. Resists oxidation during storage and manufacturing. Your body cleaves the acetate group during digestion to release active d-alpha-tocopherol. The most common form in quality supplements.

d-alpha-tocopheryl succinate.notes: The natural alpha-tocopherol esterified with succinic acid. Water-dispersible, making it useful for dry formulations and for people with fat absorption issues. Also studied for unique anticancer properties in vitro.

dl-alpha-tocopherol.notes: The synthetic all-racemic form containing 8 stereoisomers — only 1 of the 8 (RRR) is the natural, preferred form. Your body retains it at roughly half the rate of natural d-alpha-tocopherol. The cheapest vitamin E, but significantly less effective per IU.

dl-alpha-tocopheryl succinate.notes: Synthetic alpha-tocopherol esterified with succinic acid. Water-dispersible but carries the same ~50% bioactivity limitation of all synthetic dl- vitamin E forms. Less effective than the natural d-alpha succinate.

dl-alpha-tocopheryl acetate.notes: The most common synthetic vitamin E form in fortified foods and budget supplements. Esterified for stability. Contains all 8 stereoisomers, with only ~12.5% being the natural RRR form. Roughly half the biological activity of the natural d-alpha acetate form.

vitamin E from wheat germ oil.notes: A whole-food vitamin E source from wheat germ, one of the richest natural sources of d-alpha-tocopherol. Contains co-occurring tocopherols, phytosterols, and essential fatty acids. A traditional, food-based vitamin E option. Note: not suitable for wheat/gluten-sensitive individuals.
aliases: natural e from wheat germ supplement

vitamin E analogs (non-functional).notes: Structurally similar molecules that cannot function as vitamin E. May compete with active tocopherols for absorption and binding sites without providing antioxidant benefit.

oxidized vitamin E.notes: Vitamin E that has been destroyed by light, heat, or oxidation. Ironically, vitamin E is an antioxidant that sacrifices itself to protect other molecules — once oxidized, it has lost its protective function entirely.

vitamin e (unspecified).notes: The supplement label does not specify which form of vitamin E is used. This matters greatly — natural d-alpha-tocopherol has ~2x the biological activity of synthetic dl-alpha-tocopherol. Check for 'd-' (natural) vs 'dl-' (synthetic) prefix.

vitamin_e.rxcui_note: No RxNorm concept found via GSRS lookup

menaquinone-7 (MK-7).notes: The premier form of vitamin K2 for supplementation. MK-7's long half-life (~72 hours vs ~1-2 hours for MK-4) means it stays active in your bloodstream for days, providing sustained activation of osteocalcin (bone health) and matrix GLA protein (cardiovascular health). Primarily sourced from natto fermentation.

menaquinone-7 all-trans.notes: The biologically active geometric isomer of MK-7. Only the all-trans configuration properly activates vitamin K-dependent proteins. Some cheap MK-7 supplements contain significant cis-isomer contamination, which is inactive. Look for 'all-trans' or '>99% trans' on the label.

menaquinone-4 (MK-4).notes: The short-chain form of vitamin K2, naturally produced in human tissues from K1. Rapidly absorbed but has a very short half-life (~1-2 hours), requiring multiple daily doses or high single doses (typically 15-45 mg in Japanese studies). Best studied for bone density.

vitamin K2 (cis form).notes: The inactive geometric isomer of vitamin K2. Cannot properly activate vitamin K-dependent proteins. Found as a contaminant in low-quality K2 supplements. If your supplement contains significant cis-K2, the effective dose is much lower than the label states.

phylloquinone (K1).notes: The plant form of vitamin K found abundantly in leafy greens (kale, spinach, broccoli). Primarily supports blood clotting (coagulation). Has limited conversion to K2 in the body (~5-25%), so it does not fully replace K2 for bone and cardiovascular benefits.

vitamin K1 synthetic.notes: Synthetic phytonadione (vitamin K1), commonly used in fortified foods and the injectable form given to newborns to prevent hemorrhagic disease. Functionally equivalent to natural K1 for blood clotting support.

vitamin K2 from natto.notes: MK-7 from traditional Japanese fermented soybeans (natto). The original and most studied food source of long-chain vitamin K2. Natto contains the highest natural concentration of MK-7 of any food. A whole-food form with excellent bioavailability.

vitamin K2 from cheese.notes: Vitamin K2 from fermented dairy, primarily containing longer-chain menaquinones (MK-8, MK-9, MK-10) produced by bacteria during cheese aging. Gouda and Brie are the richest sources. These longer forms have extended half-lives but less clinical data than MK-7.
aliases: vitamin K2 from fermented dairy supplement

vitamin K2 from yeast.notes: Vitamin K2 from yeast fermentation. A food-based source with naturally co-occurring cofactors. Less clinical data than natto-derived MK-7 but a viable option for people avoiding soy.

menadione (K3).notes: A synthetic vitamin K precursor (menadione) that is NOT approved for human supplementation due to toxicity — can cause hemolytic anemia, liver damage, and jaundice. Banned in over-the-counter supplements in most countries. Used only in animal feed.

vitamin K analogs (non-functional).notes: Structurally similar molecules that cannot activate vitamin K-dependent proteins. Provide no clotting, bone, or cardiovascular benefit.

oxidized vitamin K.notes: Vitamin K degraded by light or oxygen exposure. Completely inactive — cannot participate in the carboxylation reactions essential for blood clotting and calcium metabolism.

vitamin k (unspecified).notes: The supplement label does not specify K1 or K2, or which K2 subtype. This distinction matters: K1 supports clotting, K2 (especially MK-7) supports bone and cardiovascular health. Without specification, the form and benefits cannot be confirmed.

vitamin_k.rxcui_note: Generic vitamin K entry spans K1 and K2 vitamers; keep RxNorm null unless the record is split to a specific vitamer.

calcium citrate.notes: One of the best-absorbed calcium forms (~42% absorption). Does not require stomach acid, so it can be taken on an empty stomach. Ideal for older adults and people taking acid-blocking medications (PPIs, H2 blockers). The most recommended form by endocrinologists.

calcium malate.notes: Calcium bound to malic acid — well-absorbed and gentle on the digestive system. Malic acid also plays a role in cellular energy production (Krebs cycle). The DimaCal brand (Albion) is a well-studied dicalcium malate chelate.

calcium bis-glycinate.notes: Calcium chelated with the amino acid glycine (Albion TRAACS). Absorbed via amino acid transport pathways, bypassing the competitive mineral absorption channels. Very gentle on the stomach — minimal constipation or bloating. One of the best-tolerated calcium forms.

calcium hydroxyapatite.notes: Calcium in the exact crystalline structure found in human bone (microcrystalline hydroxyapatite, MCHA). Derived from bovine bone, it also contains phosphorus, collagen, and trace minerals. Some studies suggest it may slow bone loss more effectively than calcium carbonate.

calcium from algae.notes: Plant-based calcium from calcified marine red algae (Lithothamnion). Contains naturally co-occurring trace minerals (magnesium, boron, strontium) in a porous, highly surface-area structure. Brands include AlgaeCal and Aquamin. A popular vegan calcium option.

calcium lactate.notes: Calcium bound to lactic acid. Moderate absorption, very gentle on the stomach, and highly soluble — dissolves well in water. Lower elemental calcium content (~13%) means you need to take more to get the same calcium dose as carbonate or citrate.

calcium gluconate.notes: Calcium bound to gluconic acid. Very gentle on the stomach and easy to dissolve, but has the lowest elemental calcium content of common forms (~9%). Often found in chewable tablets and liquid calcium formulations. Used intravenously for acute hypocalcemia.

calcium carbonate.notes: The most widely used and cheapest calcium form (~40% elemental calcium). REQUIRES stomach acid for absorption — must be taken with food. Poorly absorbed in people with low stomach acid (common in adults over 50). Can cause gas, bloating, and constipation. The form in Tums and most budget supplements.

calcium phosphate.notes: Calcium combined with phosphate — provides both minerals. Poor solubility limits absorption (~25%). Commonly used as a tablet filler and binder rather than a primary calcium source. Tricalcium phosphate (TCP) is the most common form.

dicalcium phosphate.notes: A specific calcium phosphate salt (CaHPO4) commonly used as a tablet filler, binder, and flow agent in supplement manufacturing. Low bioavailability as a calcium source. If you see this on a label, the calcium is likely incidental to its tableting function. Also provides phosphorus — see phosphorus entry for that nutrient's scoring.

coral calcium.notes: Calcium from fossilized coral (primarily calcium carbonate with trace minerals). Despite aggressive marketing claims, absorption is similar to standard calcium carbonate. Quality varies — may contain lead or other contaminants. Sango coral is the most common source.

calcium citrate malate.notes: A highly soluble calcium form that combines citrate and malate for excellent bioavailability (~42%). The form most commonly used to fortify orange juice and other beverages. Well-absorbed even without food and very well-tolerated digestively.

calcium orotate.notes: Calcium chelated with orotic acid. Some European research suggests improved cellular uptake, but the evidence base is limited compared to citrate or bisglycinate. Used in specialty supplements, particularly for cardiovascular support. Low elemental calcium content.

calcium oxide.notes: An industrial-grade calcium compound (quickite/burnt lime) that is caustic and poorly bioavailable. Not suitable for human supplementation. If present in a supplement, it indicates a very low-quality product that prioritized cost over efficacy.

calcium analogs (non-functional).notes: Structurally similar molecules that cannot function as bioavailable calcium in the body. Provide no nutritional benefit for bone health or calcium-dependent biological processes.

calcium (unspecified).notes: The supplement label does not specify which calcium form is used. This matters significantly — calcium citrate and bisglycinate are far better absorbed than calcium carbonate, especially without food. Budget supplements overwhelmingly use the cheapest form (carbonate or phosphate).

calcium amino acid chelate.notes: Calcium chelated to unspecified amino acids. Catch-all for 'Calcium AAC' on labels when the specific chelate type (bisglycinate, citrate, malate) is not disclosed. Better absorbed than calcium carbonate (bio=8). Less specific than calcium bis-glycinate (bio=14) or calcium citrate (bio=14).

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|mineral|Calcium | 27 (Number of products:15496)

phosphate salts.notes: Phosphorus delivered via common phosphate salts (mono/di/tricalcium phosphate, sodium phosphate, potassium phosphate). Essential for bone structure, ATP energy production, and DNA synthesis. Well-absorbed at ~40-60% depending on the specific salt and dietary context.

phosphorus (unspecified).notes: The supplement label does not specify the phosphorus source. Most people get adequate phosphorus from diet — supplemental phosphorus is rarely needed except in specific medical conditions (renal phosphorus wasting, refeeding syndrome).

dicalcium phosphate (as phosphorus sourc.notes: Phosphorus from dicalcium phosphate (DCP), a common tablet excipient. Moderate bioavailability (~30-40%) as a phosphorus source. Often present in supplements as a manufacturing aid rather than an intentional phosphorus source.

cross_ref.note: Same chemical substance as calcium/dicalcium phosphate. Scored here for phosphorus bioavailability.

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|Mineral|Phosphorus | 102 (Number of products:3512)

magnesium glycinate.notes: The gold standard for magnesium absorption. Chelated with glycine (Albion TRAACS), it's absorbed via amino acid transport pathways — bypassing the competitive mineral channels used by oxide/carbonate. Very gentle on the stomach (glycine is calming). Best for correcting deficiency, sleep, and anxiety support.

magnesium brown rice chelate.notes: Magnesium chelated to amino acids derived from brown rice protein hydrolysate. The rice protein amino acid matrix provides organic chelate bonding similar to generic amino acid chelates. Absorption estimated 60–70% — meaningfully better than inorganic forms (oxide, sulfate) but below specific bisglycinate and picolinate chelates. Commonly used in hypoallergenic multivitamin formulations (e.g., Garden of Life RAW line) as an alternative to soy or whey protein-derived chelates. Positioned within the amino acid chelate class per Anderson (1995) chelation framework.

absorption_structured.notes: 60-70% estimated; organic rice-amino acid chelate class

magnesium threonate.notes: The only magnesium form clinically proven to cross the blood-brain barrier (patented as Magtein). Specifically designed for cognitive support — studies show it can improve learning, memory, and synaptic density. Lower total Mg delivery than glycinate but uniquely effective for brain health.

magnesium taurate.notes: Magnesium chelated with the amino acid taurine — both nutrients independently support cardiovascular health. The combination provides synergistic heart and blood pressure benefits. Well-absorbed and calming. A top choice for people focused on heart health. Also provides taurine — see taurine entry for that nutrient's scoring.

magnesium acetyl-taurate.notes: Magnesium chelated with N-acetyltaurine. UNII TPU6QLA66F, CAS 75350-40-2. Distinct from magnesium taurate (RCM1N3D968). Marketed primarily as a nootropic magnesium form with enhanced brain penetration via the acetylated taurine moiety. Preclinical data suggests good BBB permeability; human pharmacokinetic data is limited. Sometimes referred to as MgAT or Magtein-adjacent (though Magtein is L-threonate, not acetyl-taurate). Note the raw DSLD label may contain a typo: 'Acetyl-Tuarate'.

absorption_structured.notes: Preclinical BBB permeability data only; no published human PK

magnesium malate.notes: Magnesium bound to malic acid, a key molecule in cellular energy production (Krebs cycle). Well-absorbed and often recommended for fatigue, fibromyalgia, and muscle pain. The malic acid component supports ATP synthesis. The Albion DiMagnesium Malate is a well-studied form.

magnesium citrate.notes: One of the most popular and well-studied magnesium forms. Good absorption (~30-40%) and highly soluble in water. Has a mild osmotic laxative effect at higher doses — which can be a benefit for constipation or a drawback for sensitive stomachs. The go-to affordable quality form.

magnesium orotate.notes: Magnesium chelated with orotic acid, which may support cellular energy production and cardiovascular function. Some European clinical studies show heart health benefits. Well-absorbed but lower elemental magnesium content than simpler forms. A premium specialty option.

magnesium chloride.notes: A natural magnesium salt from seawater (brand: Ancient Minerals). Well-absorbed orally. Also popular as 'magnesium oil' for transdermal application (topical absorption is debated but widely used for muscle cramps and relaxation). One of the few forms available for both oral and topical use.

magnesium lactate.notes: Magnesium bound to lactic acid. Moderate absorption and very easy on the digestive system — rarely causes the laxative effect seen with citrate or oxide. A good choice for people who need gentle, long-term magnesium supplementation.

magnesium gluconate.notes: Magnesium bound to gluconic acid. Similar profile to lactate — gentle on the stomach with moderate absorption. Lower elemental magnesium content. Well-tolerated for daily supplementation without GI side effects.

magnesium aspartate.notes: Magnesium chelated with the amino acid aspartate. Decent absorption via amino acid transport. Aspartate plays a role in the urea cycle and energy metabolism. Less commonly used than glycinate or citrate in modern supplements.

magnesium carbonate.notes: A natural mineral form of magnesium that requires stomach acid to convert to absorbable magnesium chloride. Low bioavailability (~15-25%). Commonly found in combination products and antacid formulations. Has mild antacid properties.

magnesium oxide.notes: The cheapest and most common magnesium form in budget supplements — but one of the worst absorbed (only ~4% bioavailability). Its high elemental magnesium content (60%) is misleading because so little is actually absorbed. Primarily acts as an osmotic laxative. Avoid if you need to raise magnesium levels.

magnesium hydroxide.notes: Milk of Magnesia — primarily an antacid and osmotic laxative. Minimal systemic magnesium absorption. Not a meaningful source of supplemental magnesium. Used therapeutically for constipation and heartburn relief.

magnesium sulfate.notes: Epsom salt (MgSO4) — used primarily for baths, soaking, and topical muscle relaxation. Very poor oral bioavailability and causes strong laxative effects when taken by mouth. Not recommended as an oral magnesium supplement. Transdermal absorption from baths is unproven but widely practiced.

magnesium (unspecified).notes: The supplement label does not specify which magnesium form is used. This is a major red flag — cheap supplements overwhelmingly use magnesium oxide, which has only ~4% absorption. Quality brands specify the form (glycinate, citrate, threonate, etc.). Always check the form.

magnesium ascorbate (as magnesium source.notes: The magnesium salt of vitamin C (ascorbic acid). Primarily a buffered vitamin C delivery form — the magnesium content is incidental (~6-7% by weight). Scored here for the small magnesium contribution, but this form is chosen for its vitamin C benefits.

absorption_structured.notes: 30% - magnesium content is incidental to vitamin C delivery

cross_ref.note: Same chemical substance as vitamin_c/magnesium ascorbate. Scored here for magnesium bioavailability.

magnesium amino acid chelate.notes: Magnesium chelated to unspecified amino acids. Catch-all for 'Magnesium AAC' or 'chelated magnesium' when the specific amino acid carrier (glycinate, malate, etc.) is not named. Better absorbed than magnesium oxide (bio=4) or carbonate (bio=6). Less specific than magnesium glycinate (bio=14) or malate (bio=12).

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|Mineral|Magnesium | 82 (Number of products:11063)

iron bisglycinate.notes: The gold standard chelated iron (Ferrochel/TRAACS by Albion). Absorbed 2-4x better than ferrous sulfate via amino acid transport pathways, completely independent of stomach acid. Dramatically fewer GI side effects (no nausea, constipation, or metallic taste). Not blocked by food, tannins, or calcium.

iron brown rice chelate.notes: Iron chelated to amino acids derived from brown rice protein hydrolysate. The rice protein amino acid matrix provides organic chelate bonding similar to generic amino acid chelates. Absorption estimated 60–70% — meaningfully better than inorganic forms (oxide, sulfate) but below specific bisglycinate and picolinate chelates. Commonly used in hypoallergenic multivitamin formulations (e.g., Garden of Life RAW line) as an alternative to soy or whey protein-derived chelates. Positioned within the amino acid chelate class per Anderson (1995) chelation framework.

absorption_structured.notes: 60-70% estimated; organic rice-amino acid chelate class

iron protein succinylate.notes: Iron bound to a modified protein carrier (casein succinylate). Releases iron only at intestinal pH, protecting the stomach lining. Excellent GI tolerance — comparable to bisglycinate. Widely used in Europe (brand: Ferplex/Legofer). A premium option for iron-sensitive individuals.

heme iron polypeptide.notes: Iron derived from animal hemoglobin (brand: Proferrin). Heme iron is absorbed via the HCP-1 receptor — a completely different pathway than non-heme iron. Absorbs at 15-35% (vs 2-20% for non-heme). Not blocked by phytates, tannins, calcium, or fiber. The most efficiently absorbed iron form available.

ferrous fumarate.notes: A well-absorbed iron salt with 33% elemental iron content — the highest of the common ferrous salts. Commonly used in prenatal vitamins and iron-deficiency treatment. Can cause GI side effects (constipation, nausea) but less than ferrous sulfate at equivalent doses.

ferrous gluconate.notes: A gentler iron salt with moderate absorption and only 12% elemental iron. Better tolerated than sulfate or fumarate. The lower iron concentration means larger doses needed, but with fewer GI side effects per milligram of elemental iron.

ferrous sulfate.notes: The cheapest and most clinically studied iron form — the reference standard in iron research. However, it causes the most GI side effects (constipation, nausea, black stools, metallic taste). Absorption is significantly blocked by food, calcium, tannins (tea/coffee), and phytates (grains). Best taken on an empty stomach, but many people can't tolerate that.

carbonyl iron.notes: Highly purified elemental iron powder (98% pure). Absorbed slowly via a surface-dissolution mechanism, which means better GI tolerance than ferrous salts. Also much safer in accidental overdose (important for households with children). The Ferronyl brand is the most common.

ferric iron.notes: The oxidized form of iron (Fe3+). Must be reduced to ferrous iron (Fe2+) by stomach acid and vitamin C before absorption, making it inherently less bioavailable than ferrous (Fe2+) forms. Ferric pyrophosphate is the most common ferric supplement salt.

iron oxide.notes: Essentially rust (Fe2O3). Biologically inert and virtually unabsorbable as a nutritional iron source. Used as a red/brown colorant in supplement coatings and food products, NOT as an iron source. If this is the only iron form listed, the product provides no meaningful iron.

iron (unspecified).notes: The supplement label does not specify which iron form is used. This matters enormously — forms range from excellent (bisglycinate, heme) to terrible (oxide, ferric salts). Budget supplements often use ferrous sulfate, which causes the most GI side effects.

ferric citrate.notes: Iron (Fe3+) chelated with citric acid. Better absorbed than other ferric salts due to the solubilizing effect of citrate. Used pharmaceutically (brand: Auryxia) as a phosphate binder in kidney disease, which also provides iron absorption as a secondary benefit.

ferric sulfate.notes: An oxidized iron (Fe3+) sulfate salt with poor bioavailability compared to ferrous (Fe2+) salts. Rarely chosen for oral supplementation due to inferior absorption. Sometimes found in very cheap iron products.

iron amino acid chelate.notes: Iron bound to amino acids (generic chelate, not specifying which amino acid). Absorbed via amino acid transport pathways with reduced GI irritation compared to ferrous salts. The specific amino acid matters — glycine (bisglycinate) is the best-studied.

polysaccharide-iron complex.notes: Iron bound to a polysaccharide (sugar) carrier (brand: Niferex/Neo-Plex). Designed for slow, gentle iron release with minimal GI side effects. Well-tolerated but absorption may be lower than ferrous bisglycinate. A good option for people who cannot tolerate standard iron salts.

microencapsulated iron.notes: Ferric pyrophosphate particles coated in a lipid bilayer (brand: SunActive Fe by Taiyo). The micronized particles are absorbed via endocytosis rather than standard iron transport. About 4x the absorption of conventional ferric pyrophosphate. Virtually no metallic taste or GI irritation. Increasingly used in fortified foods.

absorption_structured.notes: Micronized particles with lipid bilayer coating; absorbed via endocytosis. 40% iron transport vs 10% for conventional ferric pyrophosphate.

ferrous ascorbate.notes: Iron combined with vitamin C (ascorbic acid), which significantly enhances non-heme iron absorption by reducing Fe3+ to Fe2+ and forming a soluble chelate. Studies show 2-3x improved absorption vs ferrous sulfate when taken with vitamin C. A well-tolerated form with clinical evidence for effective iron repletion.

absorption_structured.notes: Ascorbic acid chelation enhances Fe2+ absorption 2-3x vs sulfate alone

liposomal iron.notes: Iron encapsulated in phospholipid liposomes (e.g., sucrosomial iron / Sideral). Bypasses the standard DMT-1 iron transport pathway, absorbing via endocytosis in the M-cells of the intestinal epithelium. Clinical trials show equivalent efficacy to IV iron for correcting deficiency, with dramatically better GI tolerance than any oral ferrous salt.

absorption_structured.notes: Absorbed via M-cell endocytosis, bypassing DMT-1. Clinical trials show non-inferiority to IV iron.

iron picolinate.absorption: ~25-30% elemental iron (high; comparable to ferrous ascorbate)
iron picolinate.notes: Iron(II) chelated with picolinic acid. Picolinate chelation employs the same intestinal mucosal carrier mechanism as zinc picolinate, reducing free iron exposure and associated GI irritation vs. inorganic ferrous salts. Bidentate coordination stabilizes Fe(II) through gastric transit. Limited specific RCT data vs. iron bisglycinate; absorption estimated 25–30% elemental iron — comparable to ferrous ascorbate and above ferrous sulfate (10–15%). Relevant for iron-deficiency supplementation where GI tolerance is key (Tolkien 2015 systematic review framework for iron form GI tolerability).

absorption_structured.notes: ~25-30% elemental iron estimated; picolinate chelate class

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|Mineral|Iron | 254 (Number of products:6714)

zinc carnosine.notes: A patented zinc-carnosine complex (PepZin GI/Polaprezinc). Unique among zinc forms — it adheres to the gastric lining and slowly releases zinc directly where it's needed. Clinically proven to support stomach lining integrity, reduce H. pylori damage, and heal GI mucosal injuries. The top-tier zinc form for gut health.

zinc picolinate.notes: Zinc chelated with picolinic acid, a natural metabolite of tryptophan. The classic Barrie 1987 study showed significantly higher absorption vs citrate and gluconate. One of the most bioavailable zinc forms. Well-tolerated at standard doses.

zinc bisglycinate.notes: Zinc chelated with glycine (Albion TRAACS). Absorbed via amino acid transport pathways, bypassing competitive mineral channels. Very gentle on the stomach. One of the best all-around zinc forms — combines excellent absorption with superior GI tolerance.
aliases: Albion Z-life Zinc Bisglycinate Chelate Max

zinc acetate.notes: A highly soluble zinc salt that releases ionic zinc rapidly. The preferred form in clinical trials for reducing cold duration (Cochrane meta-analysis). Particularly effective in lozenge form where direct contact with throat tissue matters. Well-absorbed orally.

zinc citrate.notes: Zinc bound to citric acid. Good absorption (~61% in studies) at a reasonable cost — a solid middle-ground option. Well-tolerated and widely available. Not as premium as picolinate or bisglycinate, but significantly better than sulfate or oxide.

zinc gluconate.notes: A common, affordable zinc form with moderate absorption. Widely used in cold lozenges (along with acetate). Well-tolerated but not as bioavailable as picolinate or bisglycinate. A reasonable choice for general supplementation.

zinc monomethionine.notes: Zinc chelated with the amino acid L-methionine (patented as OptiZinc). Good absorption and cellular retention. Methionine is a sulfur-containing amino acid that may provide additional antioxidant benefits. A well-studied branded form.

zinc orotate.notes: Zinc chelated with orotic acid. Some European research suggests good cellular uptake, but the evidence base is limited compared to picolinate or bisglycinate. A specialty form used primarily in European and integrative medicine.

zinc sulfate.notes: The cheapest and most basic zinc salt. Commonly causes nausea, metallic taste, and stomach upset — especially on an empty stomach. Moderate absorption, but the GI side effects make it difficult to tolerate for many people. The form you'll find in most budget supplements.

zinc oxide.notes: The cheapest zinc form with the worst absorption. Despite containing 80% elemental zinc by weight, very little is actually bioavailable due to poor solubility. If your supplement uses zinc oxide, you're getting far less zinc than the label suggests. Avoid for zinc supplementation — it's chosen for cost, not efficacy.

absorption_structured.notes: Consistently the lowest-bioavailable common zinc form in comparative studies. High elemental zinc content (80%) is misleading — poorly soluble in the GI tract.

zinc (unspecified).notes: The supplement label does not specify which zinc form is used. This is a red flag — budget products overwhelmingly use zinc oxide (poorest absorption) or zinc sulfate (most GI side effects). Quality brands specify the form.

zinc aspartate.notes: Zinc chelated with the amino acid L-aspartate. A double-blind crossover trial (PMID 37111104) showed higher fractional zinc absorption than gluconate or sulfate. Well-tolerated amino acid chelate with growing clinical support.

absorption_structured.notes: Higher FZA than zinc gluconate and zinc sulfate in double-blind crossover trial (PMID 37111104). Amino acid chelation mechanism.

zinc brown rice chelate.notes: Zinc chelated to amino acids derived from brown rice protein hydrolysate. The rice protein amino acid matrix provides organic chelate bonding similar to generic amino acid chelates. Absorption estimated 60–70% — meaningfully better than inorganic forms (oxide, sulfate) but below specific picolinate and bisglycinate chelates. Often marketed as a hypoallergenic zinc alternative suitable for those avoiding soy or whey-derived chelates. Positioned within the amino acid chelate class per Anderson (1995) chelation framework.

absorption_structured.notes: 60-70% estimated; organic rice-amino acid chelate class

zinc amino acid chelate.notes: Zinc chelated to unspecified amino acids. The catch-all when a label says 'Zinc AAC' or 'chelated zinc' without specifying glycinate, picolinate, or another named form. Absorbs better than zinc oxide (bio=5) or zinc sulfate (bio=8); less documented than zinc bisglycinate (bio=14). Amino acid chelation enhances intestinal absorption via amino acid transport pathways.

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|Mineral|Zinc | 137 (Number of products:9660)

inner bark powder.notes: The highest-quality slippery elm preparation, made from the inner bark which contains the highest concentration of mucilage — a gel-forming fiber that coats and soothes irritated mucous membranes. Traditionally used for sore throat, cough, acid reflux, and inflammatory bowel conditions. The demulcent coating protects tissue from irritation.

standardized extract (mucilage).notes: A concentrated slippery elm extract standardized for high mucilage content. More potent per milligram than raw bark powder, providing a consistent therapeutic dose. Preferred when precise dosing matters. May lose some of the whole-bark synergistic compounds during extraction.
aliases: slippery elm bark extract standardized supplement

bark powder (unspecified).notes: Slippery elm bark without specification of inner vs outer bark. Quality varies — inner bark is far richer in mucilage (the active compound) than outer bark. When the label doesn't specify, the product may contain a mix of both, resulting in inconsistent potency.

outer bark powder.notes: The inferior part of the slippery elm bark with significantly less mucilage than the inner bark. Far less effective as a demulcent (soothing agent). Sometimes used as a cheap filler or in lower-quality products. Look for 'inner bark' on the label for the real therapeutic form.
aliases: outer bark of slippery elm supplement

slippery_elm.rxcui_note: No RxNorm concept for supplement ingredient

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|Botanical|Slippery Elm | 601 (Number of products:528)

selenomethionine.notes: The premium organic selenium form — an amino acid (methionine) with selenium replacing sulfur. Your body incorporates it directly into proteins, creating a selenium reserve that's released as needed. ~90% absorbed. The form used in the landmark SELECT and NPC cancer prevention trials.

selenium-methyl L-selenocysteine.notes: A potent organic selenium compound that's a direct precursor to methylselenol — the selenium metabolite most studied for anti-cancer activity. Unlike selenomethionine (which gets stored in proteins), this form is immediately metabolized to active selenium species. Found naturally in selenium-enriched broccoli and garlic.

selenized yeast.notes: Selenium grown into yeast cells during fermentation, creating a matrix of multiple organic selenium species (primarily selenomethionine, plus selenocysteine and methylselenocysteine). Mimics the selenium profile found in selenium-rich foods. The SelenoExcell brand is the most clinically studied. ~85% absorbed.

selenocysteine.notes: The 'twenty-first amino acid' — the form of selenium actually built into your body's selenoproteins (glutathione peroxidase, thioredoxin reductase, etc.). Extremely rare in supplement form. When available, it's the most biologically direct selenium species, requiring no conversion.

sodium selenite.notes: The most common inorganic selenium form in supplements. An inorganic salt (Na2SeO3) that must be reduced and then converted to organic selenium species in your body. ~50% absorbed, but poorly retained compared to organic forms. Can generate oxidative stress during metabolism at high doses.

sodium selenate.notes: An inorganic selenium salt (Na2SeO4) similar to selenite but even less efficiently retained. Must undergo multiple conversion steps to become biologically active. Higher toxicity risk at elevated doses than organic forms. Used in some water treatment and food fortification applications.

selenium dioxide.notes: An inorganic selenium compound primarily used in animal feed and industrial applications. Low retention in human tissue. Not a preferred supplement form — organic selenium species (selenomethionine, selenized yeast) are far more effective for human supplementation.

selenium-enriched broccoli extract.notes: Selenium accumulated naturally in broccoli sprouts grown in selenium-rich conditions. Contains primarily Se-methylselenocysteine — the form most studied for anti-cancer properties. Provides selenium within a whole-food matrix of sulforaphane and other cruciferous phytonutrients.

selenium-enriched garlic extract.notes: Selenium accumulated naturally in garlic grown in selenium-rich soil. Contains multiple organic selenium species including Se-methylselenocysteine and gamma-glutamyl-Se-methylselenocysteine. Combines selenium's antioxidant benefits with garlic's cardiovascular and antimicrobial properties.

selenobetaine.notes: A synthetic selenium compound used primarily in experimental cancer research. Acts as a precursor to methylselenol. Limited availability and clinical data for general supplementation — mainly of interest in research settings.

methylseleninic acid.notes: A synthetic organoselenium compound (MSA) studied extensively in cancer research. Generates methylselenol directly without enzymatic conversion. Potent in laboratory studies but limited human supplementation data. A research-grade selenium form.

selenium glycinate.absorption: Excellent, chelated form for enhanced bioavailability
selenium glycinate.notes: Selenium chelated with the amino acid glycine (Albion patented form). Absorbed via amino acid transport pathways like other Albion chelates, providing excellent bioavailability with minimal GI irritation. A premium chelated selenium option.

absorption_structured.notes: excellent, chelated form for enhanced bioavailability

selenium brown rice chelate.notes: Selenium chelated to amino acids derived from brown rice protein hydrolysate. The rice protein amino acid matrix provides organic chelate bonding similar to generic amino acid chelates. Absorption estimated 60–70% — meaningfully better than inorganic forms (oxide, sulfate) but below specific bisglycinate and picolinate chelates. Commonly used in hypoallergenic multivitamin formulations (e.g., Garden of Life RAW line) as an alternative to soy or whey protein-derived chelates. Positioned within the amino acid chelate class per Anderson (1995) chelation framework.

absorption_structured.notes: 60-70% estimated; organic rice-amino acid chelate class

selenium analogs (non-functional).notes: Structurally similar molecules that cannot function as bioavailable selenium. Provide no antioxidant or selenoprotein activity.

oxidized selenium.notes: Selenium that has been degraded by oxidation. Completely inactive and provides no biological selenium activity. Indicates poor manufacturing or storage conditions.

selenium (unspecified).notes: The supplement label does not specify which selenium form is used. This matters — organic forms (selenomethionine, selenized yeast) are ~90% absorbed and well-retained, while inorganic forms (selenite, selenate) are only ~50% absorbed with poor retention.
aliases: yeast free selenium amino acid chelate

selenium citrate.notes: Marketed citrate-complexed selenium form. Pure Encapsulations states its Selenium (Citrate) is derived from sodium selenite, so this form is scored conservatively alongside inorganic selenium rather than organic forms. Form-specific human PK data remain limited.

selenium picolinate.notes: Selenium complexed with picolinic acid. Picolinate chelation likely improves transport versus unspecified inorganic selenium forms, but direct human PK data are limited compared with selenomethionine and selenized yeast. Scored conservatively above unspecified selenium and below premium organic forms.

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|Mineral|Selenium | 147 (Number of products:6361)

natural astaxanthin (haematococcus pluvi.absorption: High (fat-soluble; best absorbed with fats or oils)
natural astaxanthin (haematococcus pluvi.notes: The clinically preferred form — extracted from Haematococcus pluvialis microalgae. One of nature's most powerful antioxidants (~6000x stronger than vitamin C in singlet oxygen quenching). Supported by 50+ clinical trials for skin UV protection, eye health (macular support), exercise recovery, and cognitive function. Fat-soluble — best absorbed with a meal containing fat or oils.
aliases: organic astapure arava whole algae astaxanthin powder
astapure arava whole algae astaxanthin powder

absorption_structured.notes: high (fat-soluble; best absorbed with fats or oils)

synthetic astaxanthin.notes: Petroleum-derived synthetic astaxanthin, primarily used in aquaculture (salmon farming) to color fish flesh pink. Different stereoisomer profile than natural astaxanthin. Far less clinical evidence for human health benefits. Significantly cheaper but not recommended for human supplementation.

unspecified astaxanthin.notes: The supplement label says astaxanthin but does not specify natural (algae-derived) or synthetic. This distinction matters — natural astaxanthin from Haematococcus pluvialis has far more clinical support. If the source isn't specified, it may be the cheaper synthetic version.

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|Chemical|Astaxanthin | 12 (Number of products:746)

citrus bioflavonoids complex.notes: A natural blend of flavonoid compounds from citrus fruits — typically including hesperidin, naringin, rutin, and quercetin. These compounds work synergistically with vitamin C, enhancing its absorption and extending its antioxidant activity. Traditionally known as 'Vitamin P.' Supports capillary strength, circulation, and anti-inflammatory pathways.
aliases: seville orange citrus aurantium extract bioflavonoids

hesperidin.notes: The dominant flavanone in oranges and other citrus fruits. Clinically studied for cardiovascular benefits — supports healthy blood pressure, reduces inflammation, and improves endothelial function. Also available as hesperidin methyl chalcone (HMC), which has improved water solubility. Its metabolite hesperetin is the active form.

rutin.notes: A flavonol glycoside (quercetin bound to the sugar rutinose) found in citrus, buckwheat, and Sophora japonica. Traditionally used for capillary fragility and varicose veins. Acts as a natural quercetin delivery vehicle — gut bacteria cleave the sugar to release quercetin. Absorption of intact rutin is limited (~20%).
aliases: rutin sophora japonica flower bud extract

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|Botanical|Citrus Bioflavonoids | 21 (Number of products:165)

creatine monohydrate.notes: The gold standard of creatine with over 500 peer-reviewed studies. Nearly 100% absorbed in the gut, saturates muscle creatine stores to boost short-burst power, strength, and lean mass. Creapure is the highest-purity branded version. No other creatine form has more evidence.

buffered creatine monohydrate.notes: Buffered with alkaline compounds to resist stomach acid breakdown. Marketed as Kre-Alkalyn. Head-to-head trials (Jagim 2012) found no advantage over standard monohydrate in strength, body composition, or muscle creatine levels. Scores lower because the added cost brings no proven benefit.

creatine nitrate.notes: Creatine bonded to a nitrate group. Theoretically combines creatine's ATP-regeneration benefits with nitrate-driven nitric oxide vasodilation. Limited independent research; most evidence comes from multi-ingredient pre-workouts, making it hard to isolate creatine nitrate's specific contribution.

creatine hydrochloride.notes: Creatine bonded to hydrochloric acid, making it extremely water-soluble — roughly 40x more soluble than monohydrate. Marketed as Con-Cret. This means smaller doses dissolve fully, reducing bloating. However, no published trial shows it loads muscles faster or produces greater strength gains than monohydrate at equivalent creatine doses.

creatine ethyl ester.notes: An esterified creatine designed to cross cell membranes without a transporter. In practice, studies (Spillane 2009) show it rapidly converts to creatinine (an inactive waste product) in the stomach, delivering far less creatine to muscles than standard monohydrate. Not recommended — low score reflects poor real-world effectiveness.

creatine citrate.notes: Creatine bonded to citric acid for improved water solubility. Dissolves more easily than monohydrate, which can help with mixing. However, the citrate salt adds molecular weight, meaning you get less actual creatine per gram (~40% creatine by weight vs ~88% for monohydrate). No trials show superior efficacy to monohydrate.

creatine magnesium chelate.notes: Creatine chelated with magnesium — marketed as MagnaPower. The magnesium may help creatine convert to phosphocreatine more efficiently, and one study (Brilla 2003) showed comparable strength gains to monohydrate at a lower dose. Provides both creatine and magnesium in one compound. Limited research compared to monohydrate keeps the score moderate.

dicreatine malate.notes: Di-creatine malate — 2 creatine molecules bound to 1 malic acid molecule. Theoretically combines creatine benefit with malic acid's role in Krebs cycle. Limited human PK data specific to this form; scored conservatively relative to creatine monohydrate. Found in GNC AMP branded products.

absorption_structured.notes: No human PK data for dicreatine malate specifically; assumed similar to creatine monohydrate

creatine monohydrate ((unspecified)).notes: Generic creatine listing without a specified form. Could be monohydrate, HCl, or another salt. Since creatine monohydrate is the gold standard with the most research, products that don't specify the form may be using a less-studied variant. Conservative score applied.

hmb calcium salt (hmb-ca).notes: HMB is a natural metabolite of leucine that signals muscles to slow protein breakdown. The calcium salt (HMB-Ca) is the most studied form — research shows it preserves lean mass during calorie restriction and accelerates recovery from intense training. MyHMB is the leading branded version. Absorbs in 1–2 hours with 70–85% bioavailability. Best taken 3 g/day split across meals.

hmb free acid (hmb-fa).notes: The free acid (liquid gel) form of HMB — absorbs roughly twice as fast as the calcium salt, reaching peak blood levels in 30–60 minutes vs 2–3 hours. Bioavailability is 90–95%, the highest of any HMB form. Ideal for pre-workout timing when rapid anti-catabolic protection is needed. Marketed as MyHMB Clear. Higher score reflects the superior pharmacokinetics.

hmb.cui_note: Corrected from unrelated HMB-45 code to beta-hydroxy-beta-methylbutyrate ingredient concept.
hmb.rxcui_note: No RxNorm concept for supplement ingredient

d-ribose powder.notes: A naturally occurring 5-carbon sugar that is the backbone of ATP, the cell's energy molecule. Your body makes ribose, but production is slow in heart and skeletal muscle after exertion. Supplementing 5 g/day replenishes ATP stores faster — clinical trials show improved cardiac output in heart failure patients and reduced post-exercise fatigue. BioEnergy Ribose is the leading branded source. High score reflects strong absorption (95%+) and well-documented clinical benefits.

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|Other|D-Ribose | 712 (Number of products:2)

atp disodium salt.notes: Oral ATP as a disodium salt — branded as Peak ATP. While intact ATP is mostly degraded in the gut, the breakdown products (adenosine, AMP) still activate purinergic receptors that dilate blood vessels and increase blood flow. A 400 mg/day dose showed improved strength, power output, and lean body mass in resistance-trained athletes (Wilson 2013, Rathmacher 2012). Score reflects moderate but real ergogenic benefit despite limited intact-molecule absorption.

atp (adenosine triphosphate) (unspecifie.notes: Generic ATP listing without a specified salt form. Cannot determine bioavailability or purity. Conservative score applied — look for Peak ATP (disodium salt) for the clinically studied version.

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|chemical|Adenosine triphosphate | 3264 (Number of products:65)

lysozyme enzyme.notes: A naturally occurring antimicrobial enzyme abundant in tears, saliva, breast milk, and egg whites. It breaks down bacterial cell walls (peptidoglycan) on contact, providing broad-spectrum defense. Oral supplements are used for immune support and gut health, though as a protein it is partially degraded by stomach acid — limiting systemic bioavailability. Score reflects genuine antimicrobial activity tempered by oral delivery challenges.

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|Enzyme|Lysozyme | 1525 (Number of products:23)

dim crystalline.notes: DIM (3,3'-diindolylmethane) is a compound naturally formed when you digest cruciferous vegetables like broccoli and cabbage. It shifts estrogen metabolism toward the protective 2-hydroxy pathway and away from the potentially harmful 16-hydroxy and 4-hydroxy pathways. BioResponse DIM is the leading absorption-enhanced formulation. Used by both men and women for hormone balance, acne, and estrogen-dominant conditions. The crystalline form with absorption-enhancing carriers delivers 60–70% bioavailability.

diindolylmethane ((unspecified)).notes: Generic DIM or indole-3-carbinol listing without a specified formulation. I3C is a precursor that converts to DIM in the stomach — they target the same estrogen-metabolism pathway but are distinct molecules with different dosing. Conservative score applied.

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|Chemical|Diindolylmethane | 1346 (Number of products:303)

trans-resveratrol.notes: Trans-resveratrol is the bioactive isomer found in red grape skins, Japanese knotweed (Polygonum cuspidatum), and red wine. It activates SIRT1 and AMPK pathways linked to longevity, cardiovascular protection, and anti-inflammatory effects. Oral bioavailability is limited by rapid Phase II metabolism (glucuronidation/sulfation), but plasma metabolites retain partial activity. Resvida and Resvinol-25 are branded high-purity sources. Typical research dose: 150–500 mg/day.
aliases: Japanese Knotweed (root) and whole Red Grape extracts
Whole grape extract (Vitis vinifera) and Polygonum cuspidatum (root) extract

absorption_structured.notes: Rapidly metabolized via Phase II conjugation; ~20-30% reaches systemic circulation as parent compound

resveratrol (unspecified).notes: Generic resveratrol listing without specifying the isomer or source. Could be trans- or cis-resveratrol, or a mix. Only trans-resveratrol has established bioactivity. Conservative score applied — look for products specifying 'trans-resveratrol' content for a meaningful dose.

stilbenes (resveratrol-family).notes: Stilbenes include trans-resveratrol (SIRT1/AMPK, F ~20%), pterostilbene (CB2 agonist, F ~80% due to methoxy groups), and polydatin (resveratrol glucoside). When labeled as 'stilbenes' likely a mixture. Pterostilbene's superior metabolic stability drives class average above resveratrol alone (PMID 21116625, PMID 29322567).

absorption_structured.notes: Trans-resveratrol F ~20%, pterostilbene F ~80% (PMID 21116625); polydatin hydrolyzed in gut

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|Chemical|Resveratrol | 438 (Number of products:1077)

liposomal saw palmetto.notes: Saw palmetto's active fatty acids (lauric, oleic, myristic) wrapped in phospholipid liposomes for enhanced gut absorption — claimed 2–3x greater bioavailability than conventional extracts. Liposomal delivery protects the lipophilic compounds from bile salt disruption. Limited head-to-head trials vs CO2 extract specifically, but the delivery technology is well-established for fat-soluble botanicals.

supercritical CO\u2082 saw palmetto extr.notes: The gold standard extraction method for saw palmetto. Supercritical CO2 acts as a non-toxic solvent that pulls out the full spectrum of fatty acids and phytosterols without leaving chemical residues. Brands like USPlus and SabalSelect use this process.
