Produces the purest liposterolic concentrate — free from hexane, ethanol, or heat damage. Research dose: 320 mg/day standardized to 85–95% fatty acids.
aliases: supercritical CO2 saw palmetto extract supplement
supercritical fluid extracted saw palmetto supplement
supercritical co2 saw palmetto berry extract
usplus supercritical co2 saw palmetto extract
saw palmetto serenoa repens supercritical extract
saw palmetto serenoa repens fruit extract 12:1
Saw Palmetto (Serenoa repens) berry supercritical CO2 extract

liposterolic extract (85\u201395% fatty.notes: The most clinically studied saw palmetto form — standardized to 85–95% fatty acids and sterols (the active compounds for 5-alpha reductase inhibition). This is the form used in the major European prostate trials (PERMIXON). Extraction may use ethanol or hexane, which is less clean than CO2 but still produces an effective concentrate. Supports urinary flow and prostate comfort at 320 mg/day.

saw palmetto berry powder.notes: Dried and ground whole saw palmetto berries — contains all the plant's compounds but at much lower concentrations than standardized extracts. You'd need roughly 3–5x the dose to match the fatty acid content of a 320 mg extract. Some prefer it as a 'whole food' approach, but clinical trials overwhelmingly used standardized extracts, not raw powder. Low score reflects poor bioavailability of active compounds.

saw palmetto (unspecified).notes: Generic saw palmetto listing without specifying the extraction method or standardization level. Could be a potent CO2 extract or a basic powder — impossible to determine quality. Look for products standardized to 85–95% fatty acids for clinically relevant potency. Conservative score applied.
aliases: saw palmetto fatty acids and sterols
mytosterone(r) saw palmetto berries (serenoa repens) and astaxanthin

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|Botanical|Saw Palmetto | 121 (Number of products:1164)

stinging nettle root extract.notes: The root of stinging nettle (Urtica dioica) contains lignans and polysaccharides that bind sex hormone-binding globulin (SHBG), helping maintain free testosterone levels. Widely used in Europe for benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH) — often combined with saw palmetto. Studies show improvement in urinary flow and nighttime frequency. This is the prostate-specific part of the plant.
aliases: nettle urtica dioica root aqueous extract
stinging nettle urtica dioica root extract 10:1

stinging nettle leaf extract.notes: Nettle leaf (not root) is rich in quercetin, flavonoids, and minerals. Traditionally used for seasonal allergy relief — it may inhibit histamine release and inflammatory prostaglandins. Also used for joint comfort and as a natural source of iron, calcium, and silica. Different clinical applications than the root extract, which targets prostate health.

stinging nettle (unspecified).notes: Generic nettle listing without specifying root or leaf. This matters because the root and leaf have different active compounds and clinical uses — root for prostate/BPH, leaf for allergies and minerals. Conservative score applied since the specific plant part cannot be determined.

stinging_nettle.rxcui_note: No RxNorm concept for supplement ingredient

pygeum bark extract.notes: Bark extract from the African cherry tree (Prunus africana), used for decades in Europe for BPH symptoms. Contains three classes of active compounds: phytosterols (beta-sitosterol) that inhibit prostaglandin production, pentacyclic triterpenes that reduce prostate edema, and ferulic acid esters that lower prolactin and block growth factor stimulation. Typical dose: 100–200 mg/day standardized to 14% triterpenes. Often combined with saw palmetto and nettle root.

pygeum (unspecified).notes: Generic pygeum listing without specifying standardization or extraction method. Look for products standardized to 14% triterpenes and 0.5% n-docosanol for clinical-grade potency. Conservative score applied.

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|Botanical|Pygeum | 148 (Number of products:306)

vanadyl sulfate (VOSO4).notes: The most common vanadium supplement form. Vanadium mimics insulin by activating the insulin receptor's downstream signaling (phosphotyrosine phosphatase inhibition), which can modestly improve glucose uptake. However, oral bioavailability is extremely low — only 1–5% is absorbed. Animal studies are promising but human evidence for blood sugar benefits remains limited. Score reflects genuine mechanism tempered by very poor absorption.

bis(maltolato)oxovanadium (BMOV).notes: An organovanadium chelate designed for better oral absorption — 3–5x more bioavailable than vanadyl sulfate and with a wider safety margin. BMOV showed stronger insulin-mimetic activity in diabetic animal models (Thompson 2009). Still primarily research-grade and rarely found in consumer supplements. Higher score reflects the improved pharmacokinetics over inorganic vanadium.

bis(picolinato)oxovanadium (BPOV).notes: Experimental organovanadium compound using picolinic acid as the chelating ligand. Similar insulin-mimetic mechanism to BMOV but with a different pharmacokinetic profile. Very limited human data — mostly preclinical research. Not commercially available in mainstream supplements. Score reflects theoretical promise but lack of clinical validation.

sodium vanadate.notes: A basic inorganic vanadium salt (NaVO3) with very low oral bioavailability — roughly 0.5%. Used in early diabetes research but largely superseded by chelated forms like BMOV that absorb 10–20x more efficiently. Not recommended when better options exist.

vanadyl sulfate (unspecified).notes: Generic vanadium listing without specifying the salt form. Most supplements use vanadyl sulfate, but the form matters significantly for bioavailability. Conservative score applied.

vanadium aspartate.absorption: ~3-8% (estimated; organic aspartate chelation improves over inorganic salts)
vanadium aspartate.notes: Vanadium(IV) chelated with L-aspartic acid. Aspartate chelation provides organic ligand coordination that protects vanadyl cation through gastric transit, improving absorption relative to inorganic vanadyl sulfate (~1–5%). The aspartate carrier mirrors insulin-mimetic V4+ mechanism: downstream insulin receptor phosphotyrosine phosphatase inhibition supports glucose uptake (Thompson 2009). Estimated absorption 3–8% — modest improvement over VOSO4 but well below organovanadium chelates like BMOV (~10–15%). Limited direct human RCT data for aspartate form specifically.

vanadium citrate.absorption: ~2-5% (similar to vanadyl sulfate; citrate ligand provides modest stability)
vanadium citrate.notes: Vanadium(IV/V) chelated with citric acid. Citrate is a weaker chelation ligand than amino acids or maltolate for transition metals — it confers modest gastric stability but does not markedly enhance mucosal transport vs. vanadyl sulfate. Oral absorption estimated 2–5%, comparable to VOSO4 baseline. Occasionally appears in multi-mineral products as a citrate chelate complex. Insulin-mimetic V4+ mechanism is preserved. Limited direct comparative pharmacokinetic data; score equals vanadyl sulfate given similar expected absorption class.

absorption_structured.notes: ~2-5% estimated; citrate ligand provides modest stability

myo-inositol.notes: The most abundant naturally occurring inositol isomer — your body makes it and it's found in fruits, beans, and grains. Myo-inositol is a second messenger in insulin signaling and plays a critical role in ovarian follicle maturation. Strong clinical evidence for PCOS (improves ovulation and insulin sensitivity at 2–4 g/day), anxiety, and OCD (up to 18 g/day). Often combined with D-chiro-inositol in a 40:1 ratio to mimic the body's natural balance.

d-chiro-inositol.notes: The less common inositol isomer, found naturally in buckwheat and carob. D-chiro-inositol (DCI) is the downstream metabolite your body converts from myo-inositol via an epimerase enzyme. It directly enhances insulin-stimulated glycogen synthesis in muscle tissue. Used at 50–600 mg/day for insulin resistance and PCOS, typically combined with myo-inositol in a 40:1 ratio. Higher score reflects stronger insulin-sensitizing action per milligram.

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|Vitamin|Inositol | 85 (Number of products:3724)

copper bisglycinate.notes: Copper chelated to two glycine molecules — the Albion TRAACS form. The amino acid shield protects copper from binding to dietary inhibitors (phytates, fiber) and allows it to slip through intestinal transporters intact, yielding 75–90% absorption. Extremely gentle on the stomach. Highest-quality copper form available in supplements.

copper brown rice chelate.notes: Copper chelated to amino acids derived from brown rice protein hydrolysate. The rice protein amino acid matrix provides organic chelate bonding similar to generic amino acid chelates. Absorption estimated 60–70% — meaningfully better than inorganic forms (oxide, sulfate) but below specific bisglycinate and picolinate chelates. Commonly used in hypoallergenic multivitamin formulations (e.g., Garden of Life RAW line) as an alternative to soy or whey protein-derived chelates. Positioned within the amino acid chelate class per Anderson (1995) chelation framework.

absorption_structured.notes: 60-70% estimated; organic rice-amino acid chelate class

copper gluconate.notes: Copper bonded to gluconic acid — one of the most common forms in multivitamins and fortified foods. Moderate absorption (30–40%) and generally well-tolerated. A decent mid-range choice that's more affordable than chelated forms but absorbs better than copper sulfate or oxide.

copper sulfate.notes: An inorganic copper salt — cheap to manufacture but harsh on the stomach. Absorption ranges 20–35% and varies with food interactions. Can cause nausea and GI irritation at higher doses. Commonly used in budget supplements and water treatment. Lower score reflects both the poor tolerability and inconsistent absorption.

cupric oxide.notes: Copper oxide is nearly insoluble in stomach acid — Baker (1999) showed it delivers almost no bioavailable copper. Despite this, it appears in many cheap multivitamins because it's shelf-stable and inexpensive. Essentially useless as a copper source. If your supplement lists copper oxide, the copper on the label is not meaningfully absorbed.

copper citrate.notes: Copper bonded to citric acid — well-tolerated and moderately absorbed. Citrate is a natural chelator that keeps copper soluble through the digestive tract. A solid mid-tier option, more bioavailable than sulfate or oxide but not as well-absorbed as bisglycinate chelate.

copper amino acid chelate.notes: Copper bound to an unspecified amino acid or protein hydrolysate. 'Chelated copper' on a label usually means this form. Absorption is generally better than inorganic salts (sulfate, oxide) because the amino acid carrier protects copper through the gut. However, without knowing the specific amino acid, quality can vary. Bisglycinate is the premium chelate — this generic form scores slightly lower.

copper (unspecified).notes: Generic copper listing without specifying the salt form. This matters significantly — copper bisglycinate absorbs 75–90% while copper oxide absorbs less than 10%. Without knowing the form, quality cannot be assessed. Conservative score applied.

copper picolinate.absorption: ~40-60% (estimated; between bisglycinate and amino acid chelate)
copper picolinate.notes: Copper(II) chelated with picolinic acid. The picolinate carrier employs the same intestinal mucosal transport enhancement as zinc picolinate and chromium picolinate, with bidentate coordination protecting copper(II) through gastric transit. Animal model and in vitro data (Ashmead, Albion Research Notes series) indicate picolinate chelates improve mineral mucosal uptake vs. inorganic salts. Human copper picolinate RCT data are limited; estimated absorption 40–60% places it between bisglycinate (top) and generic amino acid chelate.

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|Mineral|Copper | 45 (Number of products:5510)

chromium picolinate.notes: The most-studied chromium form for blood sugar support. The picolinic acid carrier helps shuttle chromium across your gut lining, achieving about 2.8% absorption — which is actually high for chromium. Backed by strong clinical evidence for insulin sensitivity (Cefalu 2004). Found in popular brands like Chromax.

chromium brown rice chelate.notes: Chromium chelated to amino acids derived from brown rice protein hydrolysate. The rice protein amino acid matrix provides organic chelate bonding similar to generic amino acid chelates. Absorption estimated 60–70% — meaningfully better than inorganic forms (oxide, sulfate) but below specific bisglycinate and picolinate chelates. Commonly used in hypoallergenic multivitamin formulations (e.g., Garden of Life RAW line) as an alternative to soy or whey protein-derived chelates. Positioned within the amino acid chelate class per Anderson (1995) chelation framework.

absorption_structured.notes: 60-70% estimated; organic rice-amino acid chelate class

chromium polynicotinate.notes: Chromium bound to niacin (vitamin B3). The niacin component may independently support healthy cholesterol levels. Slightly lower absorption than picolinate (~2.4%), but still a quality chelated form. Sold under the ChromeMate brand.

chromium GTF.notes: Glucose Tolerance Factor — a complex of chromium, niacin, and amino acids that mimics the naturally occurring chromium compound found in brewer's yeast. Moderate evidence for blood sugar support. Less standardized than picolinate.

chromium histidinate.notes: Chromium chelated with the amino acid histidine. A head-to-head human trial (Anderson 2004) showed 76% higher urinary chromium excretion than picolinate, suggesting superior absorption (~4.3%). Less commercially available and with a thinner evidence base than picolinate, which is why it scores the same rather than higher.

chromium chloride.notes: Inorganic salt with very low oral absorption (only 0.4-0.5%). Primarily used in IV nutrition, not ideal for oral supplements. Much less effective than chelated forms like picolinate — you'd absorb roughly 5-7x less chromium from the same dose.

chromium nicotinate glycinate.notes: A dual-chelate that combines niacin and glycine carriers for enhanced mineral transport. The TRAACS/Albion branded version is found in many premium formulas. Manufacturer claims 2.5-3% absorption (not independently verified in peer-reviewed studies), which would make it one of the better-absorbed chromium forms.

chromium (unspecified).notes: The label doesn't specify which chromium form is used. This gets a conservative score because it could be anything from cheap chromium chloride (very poorly absorbed) to premium picolinate. Look for the specific form name on your label for a more accurate score.

crominex 3+ chromium complex.notes: Patented complex (Natreon Inc.) combining trivalent chromium with Phyllanthus emblica (amla) extract and PrimaVie shilajit. RCT evidence shows comparable glucose and lipid effects to chromium picolinate with a potentially improved safety profile (reduced Cr(VI) conversion risk per Natreon in vitro data, 2010). Absorption estimated 2–4%, consistent with other organic chromium complexes. Used in branded products like Crominex 3+.

absorption_structured.notes: ~2-4% estimated; comparable to organic chromium chelates

chromium chelidamate arginate.notes: Chromium(III) chelated with chelidamic acid (2,6-pyridinedicarboxylic acid) and L-arginine. Chelidamic acid is structurally related to picolinic acid and provides strong bidentate coordination to chromium(III); the arginine component adds biological compatibility. A specialty Albion-class chelate; human clinical RCT data sparse but chelation chemistry predicts solid oral absorption (~2–3%), comparable to chromium nicotinate glycinate in mechanism and expected bioavailability.

potassium iodide.notes: The most widely used iodine supplement form and the WHO standard for iodine fortification. Nearly 100% absorbed in your gut (96-98%). Well-studied, inexpensive, and the active ingredient in iodized table salt. The gold standard for thyroid support and deficiency prevention.

kelp iodine.notes: Natural iodine from kelp seaweed (Laminaria, Ascophyllum, or bladderwrack species). Provides iodine within a whole-food matrix alongside trace minerals. The downside: iodine content varies wildly between batches (50-500%), making precise dosing difficult. A good natural option but less predictable than potassium iodide.

absorption_structured.notes: Iodine content varies 50-500% between batches depending on kelp species and harvest

sodium iodide.notes: A well-absorbed iodine salt, functionally equivalent to potassium iodide. Less commonly used in supplements but equally effective. Sometimes preferred in liquid formulations because of its higher solubility.

molecular iodine.notes: Elemental iodine (I₂) — a distinct form from iodide salts like KI. Used in Lugol's solution (a mix of I₂ + KI) and studied for breast tissue health (Ghent 1993). Absorption depends on the formulation. Nascent iodine is a marketing term for atomic iodine (I⁻) in an 'energized' state — no peer-reviewed evidence supports claims of superior absorption. Dosing can be trickier than standard KI.

absorption_structured.notes: Depends on formulation; I2 requires reduction to iodide for thyroid uptake

iodine (unspecified).notes: The label doesn't specify which iodine form is used. This gets a conservative score. Most iodine forms absorb well, but without knowing the source we can't confirm quality. Look for 'potassium iodide' or 'kelp' on your label for a more accurate score.

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|Mineral|Iodine | 184 (Number of products:5290)

manganese bisglycinate.notes: The premium manganese form — two glycine molecules wrap around the mineral, protecting it through your stomach and delivering it efficiently to your intestinal wall. The TRAACS/Albion branded version is found in most high-end formulas. Excellent absorption with minimal GI upset.

manganese amino acid chelate.notes: Manganese bound to amino acids, but the label doesn't say which ones. This catch-all chelate form absorbs well — better than inorganic salts, though not as well-documented as bisglycinate specifically. Includes protein-hydrolysate (proteinate) chelates, which use the same absorption mechanism.

manganese citrate.notes: An organic salt where citric acid acts as the carrier. Good solubility means decent absorption, and it's gentle on the stomach. A common mid-range choice found in many multivitamins.

manganese gluconate.notes: An organic salt bound to gluconic acid. Better tolerated than sulfate with moderate absorption. A reliable mid-tier choice commonly found in daily multivitamins.

manganese picolinate.notes: Picolinic acid chelate designed to improve mineral transport across gut membranes — same carrier principle as the popular chromium picolinate. Less studied than bisglycinate for manganese specifically, but generally well absorbed.

manganese ascorbate.notes: Manganese bound to ascorbic acid (vitamin C). The vitamin C carrier may enhance absorption and provides additional antioxidant support. A solid mid-to-upper tier form.

manganese aspartate.notes: Manganese chelated with aspartic acid, an amino acid involved in the urea cycle and energy production. Good bioavailability, above inorganic salts. Occasionally found in standalone mineral and joint-support formulas.

manganese malate.notes: Malic acid chelate — malic acid is a Krebs cycle intermediate involved in energy production. Good absorption and sometimes paired with magnesium malate in energy-focused formulas. Growing in popularity.

manganese sulfate.notes: An inorganic salt form with lower absorption than organic chelates. More likely to cause GI upset at higher doses. Inexpensive, so it's common in budget multivitamins. If your label says sulfate, you're getting a basic form.

manganese chloride.notes: An inorganic chloride salt — slightly better absorbed than sulfate, but still below organic chelates. Mostly seen in clinical/IV settings. Not the best choice for oral supplementation.

manganese oxide.notes: The cheapest manganese form with very poor absorption. Low solubility means most of it passes through your gut unabsorbed. Found in bargain-bin multivitamins. If you see this on a label, the manufacturer chose cost over quality.

food-based manganese.notes: Whole-food sourced manganese from nuts, grains, or leafy greens. Comes with natural cofactors that may help absorption, but the actual manganese content per serving varies and is harder to standardize. A good choice if you prefer food-based supplements.

manganese yeast-bound.notes: Manganese grown into Saccharomyces cerevisiae (baker's yeast) cells during fermentation. The yeast creates a natural food-matrix around the mineral, mimicking how you'd get it from food. No human data supports superior absorption over standard organic salts, but the whole-food matrix earns a natural bonus. Common in brands like MegaFood and New Chapter.

manganese (unspecified).notes: The label doesn't specify which manganese form is used. This gets a conservative score because it could be anything from cheap oxide (barely absorbed) to premium bisglycinate. Check your label for the specific form name.

manganese brown rice chelate.absorption: moderate-high (estimated; organic rice amino acid chelate class)
manganese brown rice chelate.notes: Manganese chelated to amino acids derived from brown rice protein hydrolysate. Provides organic chelate bonding for improved mucosal uptake vs. inorganic manganese sulfate or oxide. Absorption estimated moderate-high; limited direct comparative clinical data vs. bisglycinate or amino acid chelate. Commonly used in hypoallergenic multivitamin formulations as an alternative to soy or whey protein-derived chelates. The rice amino acid matrix offers a gentle gastric profile analogous to zinc brown rice chelate.

absorption_structured.notes: moderate-high estimated; organic rice amino acid chelate class

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|Mineral|Manganese | 252 (Number of products:6106)

molybdenum glycinate.notes: Premium glycine-chelated form (TRAACS/Albion branded). Estimated 88-93% absorption — extrapolated from Turnlund 1995 dietary molybdenum data, as no form-specific human study exists for glycinate. The chelation may help in the presence of dietary inhibitors. Still the top-tier choice for molybdenum supplementation.

molybdenum brown rice chelate.notes: Molybdenum chelated to amino acids derived from brown rice protein hydrolysate. The rice protein amino acid matrix provides organic chelate bonding similar to generic amino acid chelates. Absorption estimated 60–70% — meaningfully better than inorganic forms (oxide, sulfate) but below specific bisglycinate and picolinate chelates. Commonly used in hypoallergenic multivitamin formulations (e.g., Garden of Life RAW line) as an alternative to soy or whey protein-derived chelates. Positioned within the amino acid chelate class per Anderson (1995) chelation framework.

absorption_structured.notes: 60-70% estimated; organic rice-amino acid chelate class

sodium molybdate.notes: The standard inorganic salt form and the most common molybdenum in multivitamins. Turnlund 1995 (the only published human isotope study) showed 88-93% absorption across all intake levels — making it highly bioavailable. Affordable and effective. The actual peer-reviewed absorption data comes from this form, not chelates.

molybdenum amino acid chelate.notes: Molybdenum bound to amino acids, but the specific carrier isn't disclosed. Absorbs better than inorganic salts. This is the catch-all for chelated molybdenum when the label doesn't name the exact amino acid.

molybdenum chloride.notes: An inorganic chloride salt with moderate absorption. Rarely used in consumer supplements — sodium molybdate is far more common. Included because it occasionally appears on labels from niche manufacturers.

molybdenum picolinate.notes: Picolinic acid chelate — uses the same carrier principle as the popular chromium picolinate. Very good absorption. Less commonly used than glycinate or sodium molybdate, but a quality form when available.

molybdenum (unspecified).notes: The label doesn't specify which molybdenum form is used. Conservative score. Most molybdenum forms actually absorb reasonably well, but without knowing the source we can't confirm. Look for 'glycinate' or 'sodium molybdate' on your label.

molybdenum aspartate.absorption: 88-93% (estimated; high bioavailability class for soluble Mo chelates)
molybdenum aspartate.notes: Molybdenum chelated to L-aspartic acid. Aspartate chelates provide adequate organic carrier for gastrointestinal protection and stability. Molybdenum absorption is characteristically high (88–93%) across soluble forms regardless of carrier ligand; aspartate chelation primarily ensures gastric tolerance. Scores slightly below glycinate where glycinate's lower molecular weight provides marginally more stable bidentate coordination. Used in specialty multi-mineral formulas requiring aspartate chelate systems.

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|Mineral|Molybdenum | 86 (Number of products:3920)

calcium fructoborate.notes: A natural boron-sugar complex found in fruits and vegetables. Sold as FruiteX-B and OsteoBoron, it has the strongest clinical evidence of any boron form — RCTs show benefits for joint comfort (Pietrzkowski 2014) and cardiovascular markers (Militaru 2013). Like all boron forms, absorption is >98%, but the plant-derived matrix and clinical data put it at the top.

absorption_structured.notes: >98% (NIH ODS: applies to all boron forms)

boron glycinate.notes: Glycine-chelated boron sold under the Bororganic brand by Albion. Well-tolerated and gentle on the stomach. Despite marketing claims, NIH data shows all boron forms absorb at >98%, so the chelation doesn't provide a proven absorption advantage. Still a quality form due to good tolerability and the glycine carrier.

absorption_structured.notes: >98% (NIH ODS: applies to all boron forms)

boron brown rice chelate.notes: Boron chelated to amino acids derived from brown rice protein hydrolysate. The rice protein amino acid matrix provides organic chelate bonding similar to generic amino acid chelates. Absorption estimated 60–70% — meaningfully better than inorganic forms (oxide, sulfate) but below specific bisglycinate and picolinate chelates. Commonly used in hypoallergenic multivitamin formulations (e.g., Garden of Life RAW line) as an alternative to soy or whey protein-derived chelates. Positioned within the amino acid chelate class per Anderson (1995) chelation framework.

absorption_structured.notes: 60-70% estimated; organic rice-amino acid chelate class

boron citrate.notes: An organic citrate salt of boron. Well-absorbed (>98%, like all boron forms) and gentle on the stomach. A solid mid-tier choice commonly found in boron standalone supplements. No RCT data specific to this salt, but boron's benefits are well-established regardless of form.

absorption_structured.notes: >98% (NIH ODS: applies to all boron forms)

boron (unspecified).notes: The label doesn't specify which boron form is used. All boron forms absorb at >98%, so the conservative score here reflects uncertainty about tolerability and clinical evidence rather than absorption. Check your label for FruiteX-B or glycinate for a more specific score.

absorption_structured.notes: >98% (NIH ODS: applies to all boron forms)

boron picolinate.absorption: >95% (boron is highly absorbed from most organic forms)
boron picolinate.notes: Boron chelated to picolinic acid. Boron is inherently well-absorbed from nearly all dietary forms (>90–98%), so the picolinate carrier provides minimal absorption advantage over glycinate or citrate. Tissue distribution follows similar patterns to other organic boron forms. Limited independent RCTs comparing picolinate vs. glycinate/citrate; quality tier matches boron glycinate and citrate. Preferred where picolinate-class chelation is formulatorically convenient.

absorption_structured.notes: >95% estimated; boron bioavailability class-high across organic forms

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|Mineral|Boron | 24 (Number of products:3278)

potassium citrate.notes: The top-tier potassium form — not because it absorbs better (all K salts absorb at ~90%), but because citrate is the gentlest on your stomach and has an alkalinizing effect that benefits kidney stone prevention and bone health. The preferred form recommended by nephrologists.

potassium chloride.notes: The most clinically studied potassium form — used in prescription K supplements (Klor-Con, K-Dur) and as a salt substitute (Nu-Salt, NoSalt). Absorbs at ~90%, equivalent to citrate. Can cause more GI upset than citrate at higher doses due to the chloride ion, which is why it scores 1 point lower despite identical absorption.

potassium gluconate.notes: A gentle organic salt absorbed at ~90%, like all potassium salts. Very common in OTC potassium supplements and electrolyte blends. Well-tolerated with minimal GI upset. Low elemental potassium per gram (about 16.7%), so capsules tend to be larger.

potassium bicarbonate.notes: An alkalinizing potassium salt absorbed at ~90%. The bicarbonate component helps buffer acid, making it useful for metabolic acidosis and pH balance. Jehle et al. RCT showed bone health benefits. May cause bloating in sensitive individuals due to CO₂ release in the stomach.

potassium aspartate.notes: Potassium bound to aspartic acid. Absorbed at ~90% like other K salts. The aspartate carrier is an amino acid involved in the urea cycle. Less common than citrate or gluconate. Sometimes combined with magnesium aspartate in electrolyte formulas.

potassium phosphate.notes: A dual-mineral salt providing both potassium and phosphorus. Used in IV repletion protocols and oral electrolyte formulas. Well-absorbed. Comes as monopotassium phosphate (acidic) or dipotassium phosphate (alkaline) — both absorb similarly.

potassium orotate.notes: Orotic acid chelate — proponents claim enhanced intracellular delivery via the 'Nieper hypothesis,' but this has never been validated in controlled human studies. Absorbed at ~90% like other K salts, but no peer-reviewed evidence supports any advantage over citrate or chloride. Niche European form with limited clinical data. Scores low due to unsubstantiated claims.

absorption_structured.notes: ~90% (absorbed like all K salts, despite marketing claims)

potassium (unspecified).notes: The label doesn't specify which potassium salt is used. Most potassium forms absorb at ~90%, so the conservative score reflects uncertainty about tolerability rather than absorption. Look for 'citrate' or 'gluconate' on your label for a more specific score.

absorption_structured.notes: Amino acid chelated potassium shows enhanced absorption similar to other glycinate minerals. Chelation protects the mineral through GI transit and facilitates uptake via amino acid transporters.

potassium brown rice chelate.notes: Potassium chelated to amino acids derived from brown rice protein hydrolysate. The rice protein amino acid matrix provides organic chelate bonding similar to generic amino acid chelates. Absorption estimated 60–70% — meaningfully better than inorganic forms (oxide, sulfate) but below specific bisglycinate and picolinate chelates. Commonly used in hypoallergenic multivitamin formulations (e.g., Garden of Life RAW line) as an alternative to soy or whey protein-derived chelates. Positioned within the amino acid chelate class per Anderson (1995) chelation framework.

absorption_structured.notes: 60-70% estimated; organic rice-amino acid chelate class

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|Mineral|Potassium | 106 (Number of products:9399)

omega-3 triglyceride (rTG).notes: Re-esterified triglyceride form — concentrated fish oil that's been converted back to the natural triglyceride structure after purification. Absorbs about 70% better than ethyl ester (EE) form (Dyerberg 2010). The premium fish oil form used by brands like Nordic Naturals. Combines high EPA/DHA concentration with excellent absorption.

algal omega-3 DHA.notes: Omega-3 from microalgae (Schizochytrium or similar). The only vegan source of preformed DHA. High purity with no ocean contaminants. Traditionally DHA-dominant with limited EPA, though newer strains produce both. Sold as Life's DHA, OPTI-3, and Elantria. A great choice for vegetarians and vegans.

absorption_structured.notes: high; comparable to fish-derived TG forms

omega-3 emulsified.notes: Fish oil processed with emulsification or micellization technology (VESIsorb, AquaCelle) to dramatically improve absorption, especially on an empty stomach. Qin 2017 showed SMEDS formulations significantly boosted fasting omega-3 absorption. A premium delivery technology, not a different chemical form. Best for people who can't take fish oil with meals.

omega-3 natural triglyceride.notes: Standard fish oil in its natural triglyceride form — the way omega-3s exist in actual fish. Better absorbed than ethyl ester (EE) concentrates but less concentrated in EPA/DHA. Includes whole salmon oil, sardine oil, and other single-species oils. A solid natural choice, especially from wild-caught sources.

absorption_structured.notes: moderate-high; natural TG form absorbs better than EE

omega-3 ethyl ester (EE).notes: The concentrated pharmaceutical form used in prescription drugs like Lovaza and Vascepa. Created by chemically converting fish oil triglycerides into ethyl esters for purification. Absorbs about 30% less than natural TG form (Dyerberg 2010), especially on an empty stomach. Cheaper to produce, which is why most budget fish oil supplements use this form.

absorption_structured.notes: ~30% less absorbed than TG form; worse on empty stomach

flaxseed oil (ALA).absorption: ALA well-absorbed (~96%), but poor conversion to EPA/DHA
flaxseed oil (ALA).notes: Plant-based omega-3 from flaxseed, providing alpha-linolenic acid (ALA). ALA itself absorbs well (~96%), but your body converts only 5-10% to EPA and less than 5% to DHA (Burdge 2006). Not equivalent to fish oil for EPA/DHA benefits. Best used as a general omega-3 source alongside a direct EPA/DHA supplement if needed.

absorption_structured.notes: ALA absorption ~96%, but EPA/DHA conversion only 5-10% (Burdge 2006)

omega-3 (unspecified).notes: The label doesn't specify which omega-3 form is used (triglyceride, ethyl ester, krill, or plant-based). This gets a conservative score because many budget fish oils use cheaper ethyl ester (EE) form. Look for 'triglyceride,' 'rTG,' 'krill oil,' or 'ubiquinol' on your label for a more accurate score.
aliases: Fish Oil Omega III fatty acids
Omega 3 Fatty Acids - Fish Oil Concentrate

eicosatetraenoic acid (ETA, 20:4n-3).notes: ETA is a lesser-used marine omega-3 fatty acid identity. It is treated as a distinct omega-3 form for cleaner label recognition; direct comparative human PK data versus EPA/DHA are limited.

absorption_structured.notes: Identity recognized as an omega-3 fatty acid; direct human comparative PK data are limited.

eicosatrienoic acid (20:3n-3).notes: Minor marine omega-3 fatty acid (C20:3n-3) found in wild salmon and cold-water fish oils. UNII PQB8MJD4RB. Structural precursor in the omega-3 elongation pathway; present in trace-to-moderate amounts in whole-fish oils like Dutch Harbor Omega and similar products. Limited direct human supplementation trials; activity inferred from its role in the EPA/DHA biosynthetic cascade.

absorption_structured.notes: Absorbed as a fatty acid; no direct comparative PK data versus EPA/DHA.

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|fatty acid|Omega-3 fatty acids | 2983 (Number of products:10)

ubiquinol crystal-free.notes: The top-tier CoQ10 form — Kaneka's crystal-free ubiquinol (CoQH-CF). Ubiquinol is the reduced (active) form your body actually uses, and the crystal-free dispersion technology prevents the molecule from re-crystallizing, which is what normally tanks absorption. About 2-4x better absorbed than standard crystalline CoQ10 depending on the comparator (Lopez-Lluch 2019). The gold standard.

ubiquinol.notes: The reduced (active) form of CoQ10 — this is what your mitochondria actually use. Your body normally converts ubiquinone to ubiquinol after absorption, but this conversion slows with age. Langsjoen 2014 showed ~1.7x higher plasma levels vs ubiquinone in older adults. Especially important for people over 40, those on statins, or anyone with heart conditions.
aliases: qh active coenzyme q10 ubiquinol form

absorption_structured.notes: ~1.7x higher plasma levels vs ubiquinone in older adults (Langsjoen 2014)

ubiquinone crystal-dispersed.notes: Ubiquinone (oxidized CoQ10) processed with thermal crystal dispersion or solubilization technology to break apart the crystals that normally limit absorption. Products like Q-Gel, VESIsorb CoQ10, and MegaSorb use this approach. Absorption rivals ubiquinol in younger adults (whose bodies efficiently reduce ubiquinone after absorption). A strong choice if ubiquinol is unavailable.

absorption_structured.notes: high; crystal dispersion ~2.3x better than standard crystalline (Lopez-Lluch 2019)

ubiquinone softgel.notes: Standard ubiquinone dissolved in oil inside a softgel capsule. The oil base helps dissolve the CoQ10 crystals, improving absorption ~2.3x over dry powder (Lopez-Lluch 2019). A reliable mid-tier form and the most common quality CoQ10 delivery. Always take with a fat-containing meal for best results.

absorption_structured.notes: ~2.3x better than dry powder; take with fat-containing meal

ubiquinone standard.notes: The basic oxidized form of CoQ10 in a standard capsule or tablet. Your body converts it to active ubiquinol after absorption. Well-studied and affordable, but the crystalline structure limits how much dissolves in your gut. Taking it with a fat-containing meal significantly improves absorption. Produced by Kaneka (the world's largest CoQ10 manufacturer).

ubiquinone powder/capsule.notes: Dry powder CoQ10 in a hard capsule — the cheapest and least effective delivery. Without an oil carrier, the crystalline CoQ10 barely dissolves in your gut. Lopez-Lluch 2019 measured only ~1.3% absorption for powder vs ~3% for oil-based. If this is what you have, always take it with a fatty meal to improve absorption.

coq10 (unspecified).notes: The label doesn't specify whether this is ubiquinol (active) or ubiquinone (oxidized), or what delivery format is used. Conservative score since it could be anything from premium crystal-free ubiquinol to cheap dry powder. Look for 'ubiquinol' or 'Kaneka QH' for a more accurate score.

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|Vitamin|Coenzyme Q-10 | 44 (Number of products:2720)

disodium PQQ.notes: The standard supplemental form of PQQ, sold as BioPQQ by Mitsubishi Gas Chemical. A stable water-soluble disodium salt that supports mitochondrial biogenesis (the creation of new mitochondria) and cognitive function. The '85-90%' absorption sometimes claimed has no human PK data behind it — the best available data is ~62% from a mouse study (Smidt 1991). Still the most well-studied and widely available PQQ form. Typical dose is 10-20mg.

absorption_structured.notes: ~62% (Smidt 1991, mouse data; no human PK study exists)

microactive PQQ.notes: Sustained-release micro-encapsulated PQQ using beta-cyclodextrin complexation, manufactured by BioActives/Maypro. Claims 2.2x better bioavailability with 9+ hour sustained release, but this is based on a manufacturer case study, not a peer-reviewed RCT. Found in brands like Pure Encapsulations. A premium option, though independent replication of the 2.2x claim is lacking.

absorption_structured.notes: Manufacturer claims 2.2x vs standard; not independently verified

pqq (pyrroloquinoline quinone) (unspecif.notes: The label doesn't specify which PQQ form is used. Most likely disodium PQQ (BioPQQ), which is by far the most common commercial form. Conservative score. Look for 'BioPQQ' or 'disodium' on your label.

lifepqq.notes: Stabilized PQQ form with enhanced stability and bioavailability. Premium branded pyrroloquinoline quinone for mitochondrial support.

pqq.cui_note: No confirmed ingredient-level UMLS concept for generic PQQ; current UMLS hits skew to salts, cofactors, or dosage forms.
pqq.rxcui_note: No RxNorm concept found via GSRS lookup

longvida curcumin.notes: UCLA-patented SLCP (Solid Lipid Curcumin Particle) technology. Originally claimed 65-100x better free curcumin bioavailability (Gota 2010). However, Verhoeven 2025 (PMID:40487425) — a rigorous head-to-head crossover PK study — found Longvida total AUC (271 nM×hr) was NOT significantly different from unformulated curcumin (289 nM×hr), and free curcumin remained below 2 nM. The original Gota study is now considered methodologically unreliable. Clinical benefits reported in Longvida trials may be mediated by non-curcumin components or non-systemic mechanisms.

absorption_structured.notes: Verhoeven 2025 (PMID:40487425): AUC not different from unformulated; free curcumin <2 nM

novasol curcumin.notes: Micellar curcumin using Tween-80 (polysorbate 80) synthetic surfactant to form tiny micelles. Schiborr 2014 showed 185x total curcuminoid AUC — but that includes conjugated metabolites (glucuronides/sulfates) with limited biological activity. Free curcumin levels are much lower. Very high total absorption numbers but the clinical relevance of conjugated metabolites is debated.

absorption_structured.notes: 185x total curcuminoids AUC (Schiborr 2014); mostly conjugated metabolites

hydrocurc.notes: Gencor's LipiSperse technology makes curcumin water-dispersible using food-grade lipid carriers. Sports nutrition research shows positive results for exercise recovery (PMID:32858046). However, no head-to-head human PK study vs standard curcumin has been published with quantified fold-improvement. Good clinical evidence base but lacks rigorous comparative PK data. Uses food-grade ingredients (no synthetic surfactants).

curcuwin.notes: OmniActive's UltraSOL technology using polyvinylpyrrolidone, cellulose, and turmeric oil to create a colloidal dispersion. Schiborr 2014 (PMID:28204880) showed 136x total curcuminoid AUC vs unformulated. However, like most enhanced forms, the majority of circulating curcuminoids are conjugated metabolites (glucuronides/sulfates), not free bioactive curcumin. Uses natural carrier components. Good total absorption but clinical relevance of conjugated metabolites is debated.

meriva curcumin.notes: Indena's phosphatidylcholine phytosome — the most clinically studied enhanced curcumin formulation with 35+ published human trials covering joint health, inflammation, skin, and diabetic microangiopathy. About 29x better absorption than standard curcumin. Uses sunflower lecithin (food-grade, no synthetic surfactants). The go-to form for evidence-based joint and inflammation support.
aliases: Curcumin Phytosome (Curcuma longa extract (root) / Phosphatidylcholine complex)
Meriva Turmeric Phytosome Complex Sunflower Phospholipid
Meriva Turmeric Phytosome Sunflower Phospholipid Complex
Meriva Turmeric Phytosome Complex Curcuma longa extract
Meriva Turmeric Phytosome Complex Soy Phospholipid
Meriva Turmeric Phytosome Curcuma longa extract
Meriva Turmeric Phytosome Curcuma longa Root Extract
Curcuma longa Root Extract, Phospholipid Complex

theracurmin.notes: Japanese colloidal submicron particle technology — ~27x better absorption than standard curcumin. About 17 published studies, fewer than Meriva but with solid evidence for cognitive function (Small 2018 PMID: 30265004) and inflammation. The tiny particle size helps bypass the usual absorption barriers.

bcm-95 curcumin.notes: Curcumin combined with turmeric essential oils (ar-turmerone) for natural synergy — about 6.9x better absorption than plain curcumin. Sold as CurcuGreen (formerly BCM-95) by DolCas Biotech. A good mid-tier option that keeps the whole-plant synergy without synthetic delivery systems.
aliases: Curcuma longa rhizome extract, Curcuminoid Complex, Turmeric essential Oil
BCM-95 Bio-Curcumin Turmeric 25:1 extract Total Curcuminoids Complex

curcumin c3 complex with bioperine.notes: Sabinsa's C3 Complex (95% curcuminoids) plus piperine (BioPerine). The original Shoba 1998 study claimed 2000% (20x) increase (PMID:9619120), later reassessed to '5-10x'. However, Verhoeven 2025 (PMID:40487425) found piperine addition produced NO improvement in either free or total curcumin AUC in a controlled crossover study. The Shoba study is now considered methodologically flawed. Still widely available and affordable, but the piperine enhancement claim is not supported by modern PK evidence.

absorption_structured.notes: Piperine enhancement debunked — no AUC improvement (PMID:40487425)

curcumin c3 complex.notes: Sabinsa's standardized 95% curcuminoids extract — the most widely sold curcumin ingredient. High purity but notoriously poor native absorption (only 1-3% reaches your bloodstream). Works much better when paired with piperine/BioPerine or when using an enhanced delivery form. On its own, most of it passes through unabsorbed.

turmeric powder (unstandardized).notes: Raw turmeric root powder with only 2-5% curcumin content. Very poor absorption without any enhancement technology. You'd need to eat tablespoons to get a meaningful curcumin dose. Fine as a cooking spice but not an effective supplement form for therapeutic curcumin levels.

curcumin (unspecified).notes: The label doesn't specify which curcumin technology is used. Without an enhanced delivery form (Longvida, Meriva, BCM-95, etc.), standard curcumin absorbs very poorly (1-3%). Conservative score. Check your label for branded enhancement technologies.

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|chemical|Curcumin | 2377 (Number of products:310)

quercetin phytosome.notes: Indena's Quercefit — quercetin bound to sunflower lecithin (phospholipid phytosome). About 20x better absorption than standard quercetin (Riva 2019). The best-studied enhanced quercetin form with clinical data for respiratory health and antioxidant support. If you want quercetin that actually gets absorbed, this is the top choice.
aliases: Phospholipid Complex, Sophora japonica Flower Extract
Sophora japonica Flower Extract, Sunflower Phospholipids

isoquercetin (EMIQ).notes: Enzymatically Modified Isoquercetin — quercetin with a glucose molecule attached, then further modified with enzymes to dramatically boost absorption (17-40x over standard quercetin). Your gut enzymes cleave the sugar moiety, releasing free quercetin for absorption. Used in several clinical trials. Not natural since it's enzymatically manufactured.

quercetin dihydrate.notes: The standard unenhanced quercetin form — a yellow crystalline powder with notoriously poor absorption (~2% per Hollman 1995). Most of it passes through your gut unabsorbed. Still the most widely sold form because it's cheap. Works better when taken with fat or paired with bromelain/vitamin C, but even then absorption is limited compared to enhanced forms.

quercetin (unspecified).notes: The label doesn't specify which quercetin form is used. Standard quercetin absorbs very poorly (~2%), so this gets a conservative score. Look for 'Quercefit,' 'Phytosome,' or 'EMIQ' on your label — those forms absorb 17-40x better.

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|Botanical|Quercetin | 1099 (Number of products:1551)

liposomal glutathione.absorption: significantly enhanced (~6x Cmax vs standard GSH)
liposomal glutathione.notes: Glutathione encapsulated in phospholipid liposomes that protect it from stomach acid and digestive enzymes. About 6x higher peak blood levels than standard GSH (LipoDuo 2025 data). Sinha 2018 showed liposomal GSH elevated blood GSH levels and improved immune markers. The most effective oral glutathione delivery, but expensive. The '90-100% absorption' sometimes claimed has no peer-reviewed PK data behind it.

absorption_structured.notes: ~6x Cmax vs standard GSH (Sinha 2018); no absolute % data

s-acetyl glutathione.absorption: enhanced (higher plasma AUC vs standard GSH)
s-acetyl glutathione.notes: An acetylated prodrug of glutathione — the acetyl group protects the molecule from stomach acid breakdown. Once absorbed, it's rapidly deacetylated to active GSH inside cells. Clinical crossover studies show higher peak plasma GSH levels vs standard reduced GSH. The '80-90% absorption' sometimes claimed has no peer-reviewed source. A good option for people who want better-than-standard oral GSH delivery.

absorption_structured.notes: Enhanced relative to standard GSH; no absolute % data available

standard glutathione.notes: Standard reduced glutathione (GSH) — your body's master antioxidant, but notoriously hard to absorb orally. Digestive enzymes break most of it down before it reaches your bloodstream (<10% absorption). Richie 2015 showed that high doses (250-1000mg/day) can still raise blood GSH over months, so it's not useless — just inefficient. Setria is Kyowa Hakko's premium fermentation-produced GSH (99%+ purity).

absorption_structured.notes: poor (<10%); but Richie 2015 showed blood GSH increases at high doses over months

glutathione (unspecified).notes: The label doesn't specify which glutathione form is used — could be standard reduced GSH (<10% absorbed), liposomal, or S-acetyl. Conservative score. Standard oral GSH is poorly absorbed, so look for 'liposomal,' 'S-acetyl,' or 'Setria' on your label.

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|Amino Acid|L-Gluthathione | 178 (Number of products:20)

glutathione peroxidase enzyme.absorption: negligible — protein enzyme (~84 kDa) degraded by GI proteolysis; no PK data exists
glutathione peroxidase enzyme.notes: Selenium-dependent enzyme. Oral enzyme supplementation has no clinical support — the protein is destroyed by stomach acid and intestinal proteases before absorption. Endogenous GPx production is driven by selenium intake, not oral enzyme delivery. Not commercially available as a standalone supplement.

absorption_structured.notes: Negligible — 84 kDa protein enzyme degraded by GI proteolysis

glutathione_peroxidase.rxcui_note: No RxNorm concept for enzyme supplement ingredient.

enteric coated.notes: Delayed-release capsules (DRCaps, Acryl-Eze) that protect live cultures from stomach acid. 90%+ strain survival to the intestines. The gold standard delivery format for non-spore probiotics.

spore-based.notes: Spore-forming strains (typically Bacillus) with natural resistance to heat, acid, and bile. Excellent shelf stability without refrigeration. Near-100% survival through the stomach.

absorption_structured.notes: Spore coat provides inherent acid/bile resistance; >90% viability

liposomal probiotics.notes: Liposome-encapsulated strains for enhanced acid protection and targeted intestinal release. Premium delivery technology with improved shelf stability.

absorption_structured.notes: 3x higher stomach survival vs uncoated (in vitro)

liquid probiotics.notes: Live strains in liquid suspension — common for infant drops and vials. Rapid delivery but variable shelf stability. Requires cold storage for most formulations.

absorption_structured.notes: No acid protection; variable shelf stability

freeze-dried with prebiotics.notes: Synbiotic format — freeze-dried (lyophilized) strains combined with prebiotic fiber. Freeze-drying is the industry standard manufacturing method (95-99% survival during drying). The prebiotics improve colonization and strain viability in the gut.

absorption_structured.notes: 95-99% survival during drying; prebiotic improves colonization

refrigerated live.notes: Non-lyophilized live cultures requiring unbroken cold chain from manufacturing to consumption. Lower stability than freeze-dried forms — viability declines without refrigeration. Effective when properly stored but survival is highly dependent on handling.

absorption_structured.notes: Cold-chain dependent; no inherent acid protection

probiotic gummies/chewables.notes: Convenient format but lower CFU counts and poor strain stability due to moisture/heat during manufacturing. Most strains don't survive the gummy production process well. Budget option.

absorption_structured.notes: Heat/moisture exposure during manufacturing; no acid protection

non-enteric coated capsules.notes: Standard capsules without acid protection. In vitro studies show 3-13% viable cell survival in simulated gastric fluid. However, most clinical probiotic trials used non-enteric capsules successfully — in vivo conditions are less harsh (food buffers pH, faster transit). Manufacturers compensate with overage (5-10x label CFU).

multi-strain blend (10+ strains).notes: Broad-spectrum blend with 10+ different strains targeting diverse gut regions. Look for formulas that list individual strain CFU counts rather than just a total blend count.

absorption_structured.notes: Variable by strain composition and delivery format

yogurt cultures.notes: Yogurt/cheese starter cultures (S. thermophilus, L. bulgaricus). Some lactose digestion benefits but limited gut survival compared to dedicated probiotic strains. Not intended as therapeutic probiotics.

absorption_structured.notes: Starter cultures with limited GI survival; not therapeutic probiotics

probiotics (unspecified).notes: Strain not specified on label. Conservative score — without knowing the specific strain, delivery format, and CFU count, we can't assess viability or clinical evidence. Look for named strains (e.g., LGG, BB-12, DE111) for an accurate score.
aliases: l acidophilus la-5(r) and bifidobacterium bb-12(r)

absorption_structured.notes: Depends on strain and delivery format

probiotics.rxcui_note: No RxNorm concept found via GSRS lookup

fructooligosaccharides (FOS).notes: Short-chain fructo-oligosaccharides (DP 2-20) that rapidly ferment in the proximal colon, preferentially feeding Bifidobacterium and Lactobacillus.

absorption_structured.notes: Rapid proximal colon fermentation; highly bifidogenic at 3-4g/day

galactooligosaccharides (GOS).notes: Oligosaccharides derived from lactose that selectively promote beneficial bacteria growth, particularly Bifidobacterium. Well-tolerated and effective.

absorption_structured.notes: Bifidogenic at just 1.37g/day; most dose-efficient prebiotic

xylooligosaccharides (XOS).notes: Oligosaccharides derived from xylan that selectively feed beneficial bacteria. Effective at lower doses compared to other prebiotics.

absorption_structured.notes: Effective at 1-3g/day; highest butyrate in long-term studies

human milk oligosaccharides (HMO).notes: Complex oligosaccharides naturally found in breast milk. Only 2'-FL is commercially available. Early clinical evidence is promising (RAMP study, RCT in 100 adults) but primary endpoints not always met. Highly specific for Bifidobacterium and immune modulation.

absorption_structured.notes: Highly specific for Bifidobacterium; only 2'-FL commercially available

resistant starch.notes: Starch that resists digestion and ferments in the colon, producing beneficial short-chain fatty acids, particularly butyrate.

absorption_structured.notes: Strong butyrate production; increases Bifidobacterium and Akkermansia

acacia fiber.notes: Soluble fiber from acacia trees that gently supports beneficial bacteria with minimal digestive side effects.

absorption_structured.notes: Gentle fermentation; minimal digestive side effects

pectin.notes: Soluble fiber from fruits that supports beneficial bacteria and may help with cholesterol management.

mixed prebiotic blend.notes: Combination of multiple prebiotics (typically FOS, GOS, inulin). Quality depends entirely on components. Scored conservatively — a blend should not outscore its individual constituents.

prebiotics (unspecified).notes: Type not specified on label. Conservative score — different prebiotics feed different bacterial populations. Look for specific types (FOS, GOS, inulin, HMO) for a more accurate score.

alpha-glucooligosaccharides (alpha-GOS).notes: Alpha-linked gluco-oligosaccharide prebiotic form. Includes branded alpha-GOS ingredients such as BioEcolians. Kept separate from GOS and XOS for identity accuracy.

prebiotics.rxcui_note: No RxNorm concept for supplement ingredient

alpha-GPC.absorption: good (~40% choline by weight); plasma AUC equivalent to bitartrate (Stoll 2021)
alpha-GPC.notes: Brain-targeted choline form that crosses the blood-brain barrier. 40% choline by weight. Plasma choline AUC is equivalent to bitartrate (Stoll 2021 crossover), but BBB penetration is better. Rx drug in some EU countries for cognitive decline.

CDP-choline (citicoline).absorption: excellent (>95% oral bioavailability); 18% choline by weight
CDP-choline (citicoline).notes: Dual-action nootropic providing choline + cytidine (uridine precursor) for brain phospholipid synthesis. >95% oral bioavailability. Cognizin is the premium branded version. Meta-analyses show similar efficacy to Alpha-GPC for cognition.

phosphatidylcholine.notes: Cell membrane phospholipid — 13% choline by weight. Supports liver health and fat metabolism. Found naturally in lecithin (soy, sunflower, egg). Less brain-targeted than Alpha-GPC or citicoline.

choline bitartrate.absorption: good (~41% choline by weight); plasma AUC equivalent to Alpha-GPC (Stoll 2021)
choline bitartrate.notes: Budget choline salt — 41% choline by weight (highest elemental yield). Plasma choline AUC equivalent to Alpha-GPC and other forms (Stoll 2021 crossover). Limited BBB penetration vs Alpha-GPC/citicoline.

absorption_structured.notes: 80-95% (Stoll et al. 2021 — equivalent AUC to Alpha-GPC)

choline citrate.notes: Citrate salt — 40% choline by weight. Good solubility for liquid formulas. Adequate for daily choline needs but no brain-crossing advantage over bitartrate.

choline chloride.notes: Chloride salt — 52% choline by weight (highest elemental content). Mostly used in animal feed and industrial applications. Uncommon in human supplements.

choline (unspecified).notes: Form not specified on label. Conservative score — big difference between budget bitartrate and premium Alpha-GPC/citicoline. Look for a specific form name for an accurate score.

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|Vitamin|Choline | 39 (Number of products:4095)

sunflower phosphatidylserine.notes: Non-GMO, allergen-free PS from sunflower lecithin. Chemically identical to soy PS at the molecular level. No clinical trials conducted specifically on sunflower-derived PS — all landmark studies used soy or bovine PS. Advantage is allergen/GMO-free sourcing, not bioavailability.

soy-derived phosphatidylserine.notes: Well-studied PS from soy lecithin. Supports memory, focus, and cortisol regulation. Basis of most landmark clinical trials (Cenacchi 1993, Crook 1991). Potential soy allergen or GMO concerns for some consumers.

bovine phosphatidylserine.notes: Original clinical research form (bovine brain cortex). Strongest trial evidence base (Cenacchi, Crook). Now rare due to BSE/prion safety concerns — plant-derived PS preferred. Safety risk is not a bioavailability penalty.

liposomal phosphatidylserine.absorption: good to excellent — no PK study showing liposomal advantage for PS
liposomal phosphatidylserine.notes: Liposome-encapsulated PS. PS already absorbs well orally (itself a phospholipid), so liposomal encapsulation provides limited additional benefit. No published PK data demonstrating superior absorption over standard PS.

Actiserine (enhanced PS blend).absorption: ~3.7x AUC vs standard PS (industry PK study, not yet peer-reviewed)
Actiserine (enhanced PS blend).notes: Proprietary PS blend with catechins and arabinose nanocarrier system (Shankar/SD Head USA). Industry PK study showed Cmax 2.8x and AUC 3.7x vs conventional PS. Peer-reviewed publication pending — preliminary data. NOT the same as Sharp-PS Gold.

absorption_structured.notes: 3.7x AUC vs standard PS (non-peer-reviewed industry PK study 2025)

phosphatidylserine (unspecified).notes: PS form not specified on label. Scored conservatively — soy-derived PS and Sharp-PS forms score higher with better clinical data.

sharp-ps gold (PS-DHA conjugate).absorption: enhanced (PS-DHA conjugate); small pilot study data
sharp-ps gold (PS-DHA conjugate).notes: PS-DHA conjugate by Enzymotec (now IFF). DHA chemically bonded to PS backbone. Pilot study (8 elderly, 6 weeks) showed 42% improvement in delayed word recall. Separate product from Actiserine.

phosphatidylserine.rxcui_note: No RxNorm concept found via GSRS lookup

phosphatidylinositol standard.absorption: moderate (estimated from phospholipid class data; no PI-specific PK study)
phosphatidylinositol standard.notes: Phospholipid involved in cell signaling and membrane structure. Found within lecithin products, rarely sold standalone. No dedicated PK study — absorption estimated from general phospholipid class data (~40-60% over extended period). Phospholipids are hydrolyzed by pancreatic phospholipase A2 before absorption.

absorption_structured.notes: 40-60% estimated from phospholipid class PK data

phosphatidylinositol.rxcui_note: Generic phosphatidylinositol entry spans multiple lipid molecular species; keep RxNorm null unless the record is split to a specific phosphatidylinositol molecule.

taurine (generic).notes: Free-form synthetic taurine with near-complete absorption (~95-100%). Produced via petrochemical ammonolysis of isethionic acid. Chemically identical to endogenous taurine. Supports heart, brain, and metabolic health.

liposomal taurine.notes: Liposome-encapsulated taurine. Taurine already absorbs ~95-100%, so liposomal delivery adds no demonstrated benefit. Products exist commercially but no clinical evidence of improved absorption.

magnesium taurate (as taurine source).notes: Chelated taurine-magnesium complex. Delivers both nutrients synergistically. McCarty 1996 was a hypothesis paper, not a PK study. Cannot exceed free-form taurine's ~95-100% absorption.

cross_ref.note: Same chemical substance as magnesium/magnesium taurate. Scored here for taurine bioavailability.

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|Amino Acid|Taurine | 2060 (Number of products:2643)

l-ergothioneine.absorption: high (active OCTN1-mediated uptake; low urinary excretion; no formal bioavailability measurement)
l-ergothioneine.notes: Rare amino acid antioxidant with dedicated transporter (OCTN1/SLC22A4). Cheah et al. 2017 showed avid absorption and retention (<4% urinary excretion), but no formal oral bioavailability measurement exists. ErgoActive (Blue California) is the main branded form, produced via precision fermentation. 'Longevity vitamin' term coined by Bruce Ames (2018 PNAS). Promising early research on neuroprotection and cardiovascular health but still limited clinical trial base.

absorption_structured.notes: Active OCTN1 uptake, <4% urinary excretion (Cheah et al. 2017); formal bioavailability not measured

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|TBD|Ergothioneine | 4323 (Number of products:1)

liposomal l-carnitine.absorption: low-moderate (no clinical data on liposomal carnitine; base L-carnitine is ~14-18%)
liposomal l-carnitine.notes: Liposome-encapsulated L-carnitine. Commercial products exist but zero published clinical studies on liposomal carnitine bioavailability. Standard oral L-carnitine is only ~14-18% bioavailable (Rebouche 2004). Liposomal may improve this but no evidence.

absorption_structured.notes: No PK study; standard L-carnitine is ~14-18% (Rebouche 2004)

acetyl-l-carnitine (alcar).absorption: low-moderate (likely higher than L-carnitine base ~14-18%, but no published oral bioavailability for ALCAR specifically)
acetyl-l-carnitine (alcar).notes: Acetylated form that crosses the blood-brain barrier. Well-studied for cognitive function, memory, and neuroprotection. Preferred form for brain health. Oral bioavailability not formally measured for ALCAR but likely modestly above L-carnitine base (~14-18%). The BBB-crossing property is the key differentiator.

absorption_structured.notes: Estimated higher than base L-carnitine (~14-18%); no ALCAR-specific PK

l-carnitine tartrate (lclt).absorption: low-moderate (~14-18% overall, but faster initial absorption than free L-carnitine)
l-carnitine tartrate (lclt).notes: Tartrate salt with faster initial absorption kinetics (higher early plasma in first 3.5h) but similar total bioavailability (~14-18%) to L-carnitine base. Popular for athletic performance. Carnipure is the branded version.

absorption_structured.notes: ~14-18% (EFSA 2003; pig study PubMed 15830915 — faster Tmax but same AUC)

propionyl-l-carnitine (plc).notes: Propionyl ester form targeted at cardiovascular and peripheral circulation. GlycoCarn (GPLC) is the branded glycine-propionyl chelate. Used clinically for intermittent claudication. Oral bioavailability presumed similar to other carnitine forms (~14-18%).

l-carnitine fumarate.absorption: low-moderate (no dedicated PK; presumed similar to L-carnitine base ~14-18%)
l-carnitine fumarate.notes: L-carnitine bound to fumaric acid for stability. Less clinical data than tartrate or ALCAR. Moderate absorption presumed from carnitine class data.

l-carnitine base (free form).absorption: low (~14-18% for supplement doses; dietary carnitine from food is ~54-87%)
l-carnitine base (free form).notes: Synthetic free-form L-carnitine (levocarnitine). Oral bioavailability ~14-18% at supplement doses (Rebouche 2004). Manufactured via chemical synthesis (Lonza Carnipure process). Chemically identical to endogenous L-carnitine.

d-carnitine.notes: AVOID: D-carnitine is the biologically inactive isomer. Competes with L-carnitine for OCTN2 transport and can impair carnitine status. Safety sentinel form — not sold intentionally but may appear in racemic (DL) mixtures.

absorption_structured.notes: Similar absorption mechanics to L-carnitine but biologically inactive

l-carnitine (unspecified).notes: L-carnitine form not specified on label. Scored conservatively — ALCAR (brain health) and tartrate (athletic performance) are the most clinically supported forms.

absorption_structured.notes: Depends on form — ranges from ~14-18% (base) to potentially higher (ALCAR)

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|Amino Acid|L-Carnitine | 255 (Number of products:1503)

betaine anhydrous.absorption: excellent (Tmax ~0.9h, t1/2 ~14h; EMA modeled ~100% oral bioavailability)
betaine anhydrous.notes: Pure crystalline trimethylglycine (TMG). The most studied betaine form. Acts as methyl donor supporting homocysteine metabolism. Excellent oral bioavailability — rapid absorption (Tmax 0.9h), long half-life (~14h), large Vd (1.3 L/kg) (Schwahn et al. 2003).

liposomal tmg.absorption: excellent (TMG already has near-complete oral bioavailability; liposomal adds no demonstrated benefit)
liposomal tmg.notes: Liposome-encapsulated TMG. Standard TMG already has excellent oral bioavailability (~100% per EMA), so liposomal delivery provides negligible additional benefit. Commercial products exist but no clinical data on liposomal TMG.

absorption_structured.notes: TMG baseline already ~100%; liposomal adds no measured benefit

betaine hydrochloride.absorption: moderate (primarily used as digestive acid; methyl donor effect is secondary)
betaine hydrochloride.notes: Primarily a digestive acid supplement (provides HCl for stomach acid). Different clinical use than betaine anhydrous (methyl donor). The betaine moiety can theoretically act as a methyl donor, but at typical digestive doses (650mg/meal) this effect is incidental. Keep in TMG parent since molecule contains TMG.

absorption_structured.notes: As TMG source: moderate (HCl dissociates, betaine portion absorbs); as digestive aid: direct action

tmg (trimethylglycine) (unspecified).notes: TMG/betaine form not specified. Scored conservatively — betaine anhydrous is the most studied and effective supplemental form.

tmg_betaine.cui_note: Corrected from unrelated TMG shorthand code to betaine / trimethylglycine concept.

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|Chemical|Betaine Anhydrous | 625 (Number of products:495)

l-glutamine powder.notes: Free-form L-glutamine is nearly completely absorbed at the intestinal wall, but 50-75% undergoes first-pass splanchnic extraction and oxidation by enterocytes (human isotope tracer studies, AJP 2000). Net systemic bioavailability ~25-50%. FDA-approved (Endari) for sickle cell disease. Well-studied for gut lining repair at doses ≥15g.

absorption_structured.notes: Intestinal wall crossing ~85-95%, but 50-75% of enterally delivered glutamine extracted/oxidized by splanchnic bed (human isotope tracers). Net systemic delivery ~25-50%.

l-alanyl-l-glutamine.notes: Dipeptide form (alanine + glutamine) transported by PepT-1 oligopeptide transporter, bypassing enterocyte glutaminase that destroys free glutamine. Harris 2012 RCT showed 2.24× plasma glutamine AUC vs free-form at equimolar doses. Sustamine is the branded version. 10× better aqueous solubility (586 vs 35 g/L). Ideal for liquid sports hydration formulas.

absorption_structured.notes: 2.24× plasma glutamine AUC over free-form (Harris 2012 RCT, 8 men crossover). PepT-1 dipeptide transporter bypasses enterocyte glutaminase.

n-acetyl-l-glutamine.notes: Acetylated glutamine originally developed for IV parenteral nutrition stability, not oral use. Magnusson 1989 showed 20-40% IV dose excreted intact in urine — deacetylation by renal aminoacylase is saturable. No human oral bioavailability RCT exists. Substantially less efficient than free-form or dipeptide glutamine for raising plasma levels.

absorption_structured.notes: Magnusson 1989: 20-40% excreted unchanged in urine even by IV (bypassing gut). Deacetylation by renal aminoacylase is saturable at supplement doses. No human oral RCT confirms meaningful plasma glutamine elevation.

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|Amino Acid|Glutamine | 75 (Number of products:2650)

l-arginine akG.notes: Arginine bound to alpha-ketoglutarate (AKG). No human PK study supports the "95% absorption" marketing claim. Willoughby 2011 RCT (12g/day, 7 days) showed no significant plasma arginine or NO advantage over placebo. AKG moiety is a Krebs cycle intermediate but provides no proven bioavailability boost for arginine itself. Actual systemic availability similar to free arginine (~20-68% dose-dependent).

absorption_structured.notes: No human PK study shows AAKG superiority over free arginine. Willoughby 2011: no significant difference in plasma arginine vs placebo for hemodynamic endpoints. The "95%" claim is unsupported by any published data.

l-arginine hcl.notes: Hydrochloride salt form with better aqueous solubility than free base. Bode-Böger 1998: 68±9% bioavailability at 6g. Tangphao 1999: 21±4% at 10g — dose-dependent arginase saturation drives the wide range. HCl provides faster dissolution, not fundamentally different absorption. Common in capsule/powder supplements for nitric oxide support.

absorption_structured.notes: Bode-Böger 1998: 68±9% at 6g dose. Tangphao 1999: 21±4% at 10g dose. HCl salt improves solubility and dissolution speed but once dissolved, same arginase-limited metabolism as free base.

l-arginine base.notes: Free-form L-arginine base. Bioavailability is strongly dose-dependent due to intestinal arginase: ~40-68% at 3-6g doses (Bode-Böger 1998), dropping to ~20% at 10g+ (Tangphao 1999). Lower aqueous solubility than HCl salt form. Salt forms (HCl, aspartate) offer manufacturing stability but similar systemic delivery once dissolved.

absorption_structured.notes: Same arginase-limited first-pass as HCl. Tangphao 1999: 21% at 10g. Bode-Böger 1998: 68% at 6g. Slightly lower solubility than HCl salt form may slow dissolution.

arginine aspartate.notes: Arginine salt with aspartic acid. Dissociates in GI fluid to free arginine (same arginase-limited bioavailability as other forms) plus aspartate (urea cycle substrate that may aid ammonia clearance during exercise). No direct PK comparison to HCl exists. Functional arginine delivery similar to HCl/base.

absorption_structured.notes: Salt dissociates to free arginine + aspartate in GI fluid. Arginine component subject to same arginase first-pass as other forms. No direct PK comparison to HCl.
PMC4603712: significantly increased plasma arginine at 1, 1.5, 2, 3, and 6 hours post-dose vs. 1 hour only for HCl. Mechanism: arginase inhibition reduces first-pass degradation.

nitrosigine (arginine silicate inositol).notes: Patented inositol-stabilized arginine silicate (Nutrition21). Human RCT (PMC4603712): plasma arginine elevated for 5-6 hours vs 1 hour for HCl, with >70% higher plasma levels. Unique arginase inhibition mechanism directly reduces the first-pass catabolism that limits other arginine forms. Effective at 1500mg (lower than typical arginine doses). Also studied for cognitive function.

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|Amino Acid|L-Arginine | 116 (Number of products:647)

l-lysine hcl.notes: Hydrochloride salt form — the most common supplemental lysine. HCl improves dissolution and manufacturing stability but delivers identical L-lysine once dissolved. Systemic bioavailability ~60-80% after ~20-40% splanchnic first-pass extraction (Yin 2013 isotope tracer). Supports immune function (herpes simplex suppression), collagen synthesis, and calcium absorption.

absorption_structured.notes: Yin 2013 isotope tracer: ~18-40% splanchnic first-pass disappearance in healthy men. HCl vs base provides identical lysine once dissolved — difference is manufacturing stability, not bioavailability.

l-lysine base.notes: Free-form L-lysine base. Delivers identical lysine to HCl once dissolved — no clinically meaningful bioavailability difference between salt forms. Systemic bioavailability ~60-80% (same splanchnic first-pass as HCl). Essential amino acid not made by the body. Slightly less manufacturing stability and dissolution speed than HCl.

absorption_structured.notes: Same systemic bioavailability as HCl. Salt form affects dissolution/stability, not amino acid absorption.

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|Amino Acid|Lysine | 172 (Number of products:1388)

n-acetyl l-tyrosine.absorption: poor (56% excreted intact IV; minimal plasma tyrosine increase)
n-acetyl l-tyrosine.notes: NALT has better water solubility but is inferior to L-tyrosine for raising plasma/brain tyrosine. Magnusson 1989: 56% of IV dose excreted unchanged — deacetylation by renal aminoacylase-1 is saturable. Avraham 2003 (parenteral): elevated plasma NALT but free tyrosine remained subnormal. Oral L-tyrosine produces 130-276% plasma elevation vs. minimal from NALT.

absorption_structured.notes: Magnusson 1989: 56% of IV dose excreted unchanged in urine. Plasma free tyrosine increased only ~25% vs 130-276% from oral L-tyrosine. Deacetylation by renal aminoacylase-1 is saturable at supplement doses.

l-tyrosine powder.notes: Free-form L-tyrosine — the preferred supplemental form. Absorbed via high-capacity LNAA transporter in small intestine. Jongkees 2017: dose-dependent 130-276% plasma tyrosine elevation, sustained up to 8 hours. Precursor to dopamine, norepinephrine, and thyroid hormones. Superior to NALT for raising brain tyrosine levels.

absorption_structured.notes: Absorbed via high-capacity LNAA transporter (SLC7A5/LAT1). Jongkees 2017 (PMC5748730): 130-276% plasma elevation at 100-150 mg/kg doses, sustained 8 hours. Low hepatic first-pass compared to glutamine.

d-tyrosine.notes: AVOID: D-tyrosine is the biologically inactive stereoisomer. Does not serve as a neurotransmitter precursor and may interfere with L-tyrosine uptake.

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|Amino Acid|Tyrosine | 1540 (Number of products:1613)

l-cysteine base.absorption: ~20-40% total cysteine equivalents (rapid GI oxidation to cystine)
l-cysteine base.notes: Free-form cysteine with low and unreliable systemic delivery (~20-40% as cysteine equivalents) due to rapid GI oxidation to cystine (the disulfide dimer). Most circulating cysteine after oral L-cysteine is oxidized cystine, not free thiol. NAC (a separate parent) is the preferred supplemental cysteine source due to greater chemical stability. Provides sulfur for glutathione synthesis when converted back intracellularly.

absorption_structured.notes: Free L-cysteine thiol group oxidizes rapidly in GI tract to cystine (disulfide dimer). Most circulating cysteine arrives as oxidized cystine, not free thiol. NAC (separate parent) is the preferred supplemental cysteine source.

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|amino acid|Cysteine | 2404 (Number of products:441)

l-proline powder.notes: Free-form L-proline. Absorbed via Na+-dependent IMINO and NBB transporters, but approximately 40% of luminal proline is catabolized by the intestinal mucosa (Wu et al., 2017). Net systemic delivery ~55-70%, not the commonly cited 90-95%. Non-essential amino acid synthesized endogenously from glutamate. Key collagen amino acid — supports joint, skin, and connective tissue structure.

absorption_structured.notes: Absorbed via Na+-dependent IMINO and NBB transporters, but ~40% of luminal proline catabolized by intestinal mucosa before reaching portal blood (Wu et al., 2017). Net systemic delivery ~55-70%.

hydroxyproline.absorption: moderate (absorbed but metabolically non-functional for collagen)
hydroxyproline.notes: Post-translationally modified proline found in collagen. Absorbed via intestinal imino transporters but CANNOT be directly reincorporated into new collagen — humans lack a tRNA for hydroxyproline, and hydroxylation occurs only post-translationally on nascent collagen chains. Catabolized via glyoxylate/pyruvate pathway. Collagen-derived peptides (Pro-Hyp, Hyp-Gly) are more useful forms that reach skin fibroblasts intact.

absorption_structured.notes: Absorbed via intestinal imino transporters but cannot be reincorporated into new collagen (no tRNA for hydroxyproline; hydroxylation is post-translational). Catabolized via glyoxylate/pyruvate pathway.

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|Amino Acid|Proline | 503 (Number of products:476)

l-glycine powder.notes: Simplest amino acid with good oral absorption (~60-85%, dose-dependent due to partial hepatic extraction). Precursor to glutathione, creatine, bile salts, and heme. Well-documented sleep quality benefit at 3g before bedtime via NMDA receptor modulation (Yamadera et al., 2007; Bannai et al., 2012). Used at high doses (60 g/day) in schizophrenia adjunctive trials. Supports collagen synthesis.

absorption_structured.notes: Good but variable absorption; dose-dependent due to partial hepatic extraction via serine hydroxymethyltransferase and glycine cleavage system. Higher doses saturate hepatic extraction.

l_glycine.rxcui_note: No RxNorm concept found via GSRS lookup

l-alanine standard.absorption: ~30-31% systemic (69% splanchnic extraction, Van der Schoor 1999)
l-alanine standard.notes: Non-essential amino acid; primary substrate for hepatic gluconeogenesis via the glucose-alanine cycle.
