Despite complete intestinal absorption, ~69% is extracted by the splanchnic bed on first pass (Van der Schoor et al., 1999 isotope tracer), leaving only ~30-31% reaching systemic circulation. Commonly included in amino acid complex supplements. Limited clinical significance as standalone supplement given extensive hepatic metabolism.

absorption_structured.notes: Van der Schoor et al. 1999 (stable isotope tracer): 69±1% of enterally delivered alanine extracted by splanchnic bed on first pass; 83% of extracted alanine oxidized to CO2. Net systemic delivery ~30-31%.

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|amino acid|Alanine | 2407 (Number of products:622)

l-histidine standard.notes: Essential amino acid; precursor to histamine (immune/gastric), carnosine (muscle buffering dipeptide with beta-alanine), and urocanic acid. Oral and IV bioavailability equivalent in isotope tracer studies (est. >85-90%, Thompson & Halliwell 1996). Supports immune function, GI acid production, and carnosine synthesis for exercise performance. Mild anti-inflammatory activity at supplemental doses (1-4g/day).

absorption_structured.notes: Oral and IV bioavailability equivalent in tracer studies (Thompson & Halliwell 1996). Absorbed via SLC7A8/LAT2 and SLC6A19/B0AT1 neutral amino acid transporters.

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|Amino Acid|L-Histidine | 176 (Number of products:100)

l-serine standard.notes: Non-essential amino acid involved in phospholipid synthesis, one-carbon metabolism, and neurotransmitter synthesis (glycine, cysteine, D-serine for NMDA signaling). Oral bioavailability ~60% with substantial hepatic extraction (~75%) per population PK model (Bhattacharya et al., 2021). Clinically studied at high doses (up to 20g/day) for ALS (Phase I safety confirmed, Cox et al. 2016) and GRIN gene mutation-associated neurological disorders.

absorption_structured.notes: Population PK model (Bhattacharya et al., 2021, PMC8156419): oral bioavailability ~60%, hepatic extraction fraction ~75%. Phase I safety confirmed up to 20g/day (Cox et al. 2016).

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|Amino Acid|L-Serine | 168 (Number of products:1)

l-glutamic acid standard.notes: Non-essential excitatory amino acid with very low oral bioavailability: 88-96% of orally ingested glutamic acid is extracted by the splanchnic bed on first pass, leaving only 4-12% reaching systemic circulation (Matthews et al., 1993; Battezzati et al., 1995). The intestinal mucosa uses glutamate as its primary oxidative fuel. Supplemental oral glutamic acid has minimal impact on systemic or CNS glutamate levels. The body tightly regulates plasma glutamate to prevent neurotoxic excess.

absorption_structured.notes: Matthews et al. 1993: 88±2% of enterally delivered glutamate extracted by splanchnic bed on first pass. Battezzati et al. 1995: 78±3% oxidized to CO2. The gut uses glutamate as its primary oxidative fuel.

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|amino acid|Glutamic Acid | 2305 (Number of products:567)

l-ornithine standard.notes: Non-proteinogenic amino acid; key urea cycle intermediate. Oral bioavailability ~82% (LOLA pharmacokinetics in healthy volunteers; peaks 30-60 min). Reduces blood ammonia and elevates urea output during prolonged exercise (Sugino et al., 2008, 2-6g/day). 400mg before sleep improved sleep quality and lowered cortisol/DHEA ratio in 8-week RCT (Torii et al., 2014). Partially converted to citrulline/arginine in liver.

absorption_structured.notes: LOLA pharmacokinetics in healthy volunteers: oral bioavailability 82.2±28% (peaks 30-60 min, returns to baseline by 7h). Functional urea cycle activity confirmed.

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|Amino Acid|Ornithine | 1819 (Number of products:212)

d-aspartic acid powder.absorption: good (~75-85%; no formal human F% study)
d-aspartic acid powder.notes: D-form amino acid absorbed via intestinal EAAT/X-AG transporters. Topo 2009 (PMC2774316) showed +42% testosterone in low-normal men over 12 days, but Willoughby 2013 and Melville 2015 found no effect in resistance-trained men with normal T. Systematic review (Roshanzamir 2017, PMC5340133): results contradictory across 4 RCTs. Benefits limited to hypogonadal or untrained men with low-normal baseline T. Clinical studies used sodium D-aspartate (DADAVIT), which has better solubility but identical post-absorption PK.

absorption_structured.notes: Amino acid transport (~75-85%); no formal oral bioavailability study for D-enantiomer

d_aspartic_acid.rxcui_note: No RxNorm concept for supplement ingredient

l-tryptophan powder.absorption: intestinal ~90%; systemic lower due to hepatic TDO first-pass and BBB LNAA competition
l-tryptophan powder.notes: Essential amino acid, precursor to serotonin and melatonin. Intestinal absorption ~90% via LAT1/ASCT2 transporters, but hepatic tryptophan 2,3-dioxygenase (TDO) causes 50-70% first-pass extraction (Millson 1985, PMC1400876). Brain uptake further limited by competition with 5 other large neutral amino acids (LNAAs) at the BBB LAT1 transporter. Best taken with carbohydrate (drives competing BCAAs into muscle via insulin) and without protein. TryptoPure (Ajinomoto) is a pharmaceutical-grade fermented form — purity advantage is safety-relevant (EMS crisis 1989) but does not change PK.

absorption_structured.notes: Intestinal ~90%; systemic ~30-50% after hepatic TDO first-pass (Millson 1985); brain fraction further reduced by LNAA competition

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|amino acid|Tryptophan | 2783 (Number of products:447)

gaba powder.absorption: gut absorption good; BBB crossing minimal (efflux 17x influx in animal models)
gaba powder.notes: Primary inhibitory neurotransmitter. Absorbed in gut via GAT2/BGT-1 transporters with measurable plasma elevation at 30 min. However, brain efflux rate is 17x higher than influx in animal models, and no human study has directly measured GABA BBB permeability (Boonstra 2015, PMC4594160). Clinical effects at 28-100 mg doses (stress/sleep) are likely mediated via enteric nervous system and vagal nerve signaling rather than direct CNS penetration (Frontiers 2025, PMC12093412). Effective but through indirect gut-brain axis mechanism.

absorption_structured.notes: Good gut absorption; CNS bioavailability minimal — BBB efflux 17x influx (Boonstra 2015); effects likely vagal/peripheral

pharma-gaba.absorption: gut absorption good; BBB crossing unproven (chemically identical to synthetic GABA)
pharma-gaba.notes: Naturally fermented GABA produced by Lactobacillus hilgardii. Abdou 2006 showed increased alpha-wave EEG amplitude and preserved secretory IgA under stress (n=13 crossover RCT). Byun 2018 showed improved PSQI sleep scores with fermented rice germ GABA. However, PharmaGABA is chemically identical to synthetic GABA — no published head-to-head PK data demonstrates superior BBB crossing. Clinical effects are real but likely mediated through gut-brain axis/vagal signaling, same as synthetic GABA. The +2 bio gap vs generic reflects stronger clinical evidence base, not molecular advantage.

absorption_structured.notes: Same molecule as synthetic GABA; gut absorption good; CNS delivery unproven; clinical signal via gut-brain axis (Abdou 2006, Byun 2018)

liposomal gaba.notes: Liposome-encapsulated GABA designed to improve BBB crossing. Theoretical rationale is sound (liposomes may shield from enzymatic degradation and improve lymphatic absorption), but zero oral human clinical trials exist. Only published liposomal GABA data is IV animal studies (Loeb et al. — epileptic activity reduction via intravenous delivery, not oral). Scored above unspecified but well below forms with actual human clinical data.

absorption_structured.notes: Theoretical enhanced delivery; no published oral human pharmacokinetic or clinical data (Bhatt 2017, PMC6415746)

gaba (gamma-aminobutyric acid) (unspecif.notes: GABA form not specified on label. Scored conservatively. Oral GABA has documented gut absorption but limited/unproven direct BBB crossing; clinical effects likely via gut-brain axis (Boonstra 2015).

gaba.cui_note: Corrected from dosage-form CUI to ingredient-level gamma-aminobutyric acid concept.

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|Chemical|GABA | 443 (Number of products:610)

metabolic_relationships.name: GAMMA-AMINOBUTYRIC ACID TYPE B RECEPTOR SUBUNIT 1

carnosyn beta-alanine.notes: Patented, NSF Certified for Sport beta-alanine from Natural Alternatives International. Harris 2006 (PubMed 16554972): <5% urinary loss at 10-40 mg/kg doses, confirming near-complete absorption. Muscle carnosine increased +58.8% at 4 weeks and +80.1% at 10 weeks at 3.2-6.4 g/day. Virtually all human RCT evidence for beta-alanine used CarnoSyn specifically. May cause harmless paresthesia (tingling) at doses >800 mg in a single bolus.

absorption_structured.notes: <5% urinary loss (Harris 2006, PubMed 16554972); near-complete absorption; Cmax 833±43 µM at 40 mg/kg

sr carnosyn beta-alanine.absorption: excellent, extended-release (lower Cmax, same total absorption)
sr carnosyn beta-alanine.notes: Sustained-release CarnoSyn formulation by Natural Alternatives International. Produces similar total muscle carnosine loading to rapid-release CarnoSyn at equivalent doses (Stegen 2011, Hobson 2012), but with lower Cmax and extended Tmax. Specifically designed to reduce or eliminate paresthesia (tingling), improving compliance at effective doses >4 g/day. Preferred for sustained dosing protocols.

absorption_structured.notes: Same total absorption as rapid-release; lower Cmax, extended Tmax reduces paresthesia (Stegen 2011)

beta-alanine powder.notes: Generic beta-alanine (3-aminopropanoic acid). Same molecule as CarnoSyn but without purity certification or direct clinical provenance. Effective for raising muscle carnosine and improving high-intensity exercise endurance (meta-analyses confirm 2-3% performance benefit at 60-240s efforts). May cause harmless paresthesia (tingling) at single doses >800 mg. Inter-individual PK variability ~35% CV (Stegen 2018, PMC6107792).

absorption_structured.notes: Same molecule as CarnoSyn; near-complete absorption inferred from urinary recovery data

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|Amino Acid|Beta-Alanine | 1061 (Number of products:1807)

metabolic_relationships.name: MAS-RELATED G-PROTEIN COUPLED RECEPTOR MEMBER D

lutein (unspecified).absorption: variable, fat-dependent (~10-50% with dietary fat; <5% fasted)
lutein (unspecified).notes: Xanthophyll carotenoid for macular and eye health, extracted from marigold (Tagetes erecta). Absorption is highly fat-dependent: <5% fasted, 10-50% with dietary fat (PMC5306026, PMC6054194). Free lutein and lutein esters show clinically equivalent bioavailability — conflicting 17% AUC difference across studies (Norkus 2010, PMID:21677121 vs Bowen 2002, PMID:12134711). FloraGLO (free lutein, used in AREDS2 NIH trial) and Lutemax 2020 are well-studied branded forms but not chemically distinct. Must be taken with fat-containing meal for meaningful absorption.

absorption_structured.notes: Fat-dependent carotenoid; <5% fasted, 10-50% with fat (PMC5306026, PMC6054194); free and ester forms equivalent (PMID:21677121)

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|Vitamin|Lutein | 166 (Number of products:2293)

zeaxanthin (unspecified).notes: Xanthophyll carotenoid concentrated in the macula, from marigold or paprika oleoresin. (3R,3’R)-zeaxanthin (dietary/trans) and meso-zeaxanthin (3R,3’S) are stereoisomers with equivalent oral absorption but different retinal deposition — meso-zeaxanthin preferentially targets the central fovea (PMC4706936). All three macular carotenoids together (lutein + zeaxanthin + meso-zeaxanthin) produce the strongest MPOD response (PMID:27420092, Ma 2016, n=1764). Absorption is fat-dependent, same pathway as lutein (PMC6770730). Must be taken with fat-containing meal.

absorption_structured.notes: Fat-dependent carotenoid; same micellar absorption pathway as lutein (PMC6770730); meso- and trans-zeaxanthin equivalent oral absorption

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|chemical|Zeaxanthin | 2326 (Number of products:1137)

stabilized sulforaphane.notes: Pre-formed, stabilized sulforaphane that bypasses glucoraphanin→sulforaphane conversion. Egner 2011 (PMC3076202): mean 70% urinary excretion of sulforaphane metabolites from SFR beverage in Qidong trial (n=200). Vermeulen 2008 (PMID:18950181): 37% from raw broccoli with myrosinase. SFX-01 (alpha-cyclodextrin complex) confirmed rapid absorption in Phase 1 (PMC11782309). The 70% figure is urinary metabolite recovery, not true absolute bioavailability — actual systemic F% is likely somewhat lower.

absorption_structured.notes: ~70% urinary metabolite recovery (Egner 2011, PMC3076202); actual systemic F% slightly lower

broccoli sprout extract.absorption: moderate (~30-50%, depends on endogenous myrosinase activity)
broccoli sprout extract.notes: Whole broccoli sprout extract containing glucoraphanin + variable endogenous myrosinase. Conversion to active sulforaphane depends on intact enzyme activity, which degrades with heat/processing. Vermeulen 2008 (PMID:18950181): 37% urinary recovery from raw broccoli. Fresh sprouts have higher myrosinase than processed extracts. Cramer & Jeffery 2011 (PMID:26524341): sprouts are 3-4x more bioavailable than glucoraphanin alone. Variable between products depending on myrosinase preservation.

absorption_structured.notes: ~37% from raw broccoli (Vermeulen 2008); variable with processing; 3-4x better than glucoraphanin alone

glucoraphanin.absorption: low (mean ~5% conversion; range 1-40% depending on gut microbiome)
glucoraphanin.notes: Sulforaphane precursor requiring myrosinase enzyme or gut bacteria (Bacteroides/Lactobacillus) for activation. Egner 2011 (PMC3076202): mean 5% urinary excretion from GRR beverage without myrosinase. Individual variability is enormous — 1-40% depending on gut microbiome composition (PMC11847849). TrueBroc (Brassica Protection Products) is a branded glucoraphanin from broccoli seed. Without co-administered myrosinase, conversion is unreliable and mostly poor.

absorption_structured.notes: Mean ~5% conversion (Egner 2011, PMC3076202); range 1-40% based on gut microbiome (PMC11847849)

glucoraphanin + myrosinase.notes: Optimized formulation pairing glucoraphanin with active myrosinase enzyme (from mustard seed, moringa leaf, or maitake mushroom). Exogenous myrosinase increases sulforaphane bioavailability 3-7x vs glucoraphanin alone (Nature Sci Rep 2026, doi:10.1038/s41598-026-39389-4). If glucoraphanin alone = ~5%, this yields ~15-35% conversion — not the 60-80% previously claimed. Avmacol uses broccoli seed ERS92 (glucoraphanin) + Myrosimax (moringa+maitake myrosinase). BroccoVital is another branded combo.

absorption_structured.notes: 3-7x improvement over glucoraphanin alone; ~20-40% effective conversion (Sci Rep 2026)

sulforaphane (unspecified).notes: Sulforaphane form not specified on label. Scored conservatively. Stabilized/pre-formed sulforaphane has the best bioavailability (~70% urinary recovery, Egner 2011). Glucoraphanin alone converts poorly (~5%) without myrosinase co-administration.
aliases: Broccoli Aerial Parts, Plant, Seed Extract Concentrate

spermidine (free base).absorption: ~15-25% oral bioavailability (rodent PK; human data limited)
spermidine (free base).notes: Endogenous polyamine (1,8-diamino-4-azaoctane). CUI C0037871, UNII U87FK77H25, CAS 124-20-9, PubChem CID 1102. Key autophagy inducer — promotes cellular self-cleaning via mTOR-independent pathways (PMID:19801973, Eisenberg 2009). Found naturally in wheat germ, soybeans, aged cheese, and natto. Emerging longevity research: 0.9 mmol/day dietary spermidine associated with reduced all-cause mortality (Kiechl 2018, PMID:30094136). Rodent studies show improved cardiac function and neuroprotection. Human clinical trials underway (SmartAge, NCT03094546). Typical supplement dose: 1-10 mg/day, often from wheat germ extract.

absorption_structured.notes: Rodent PK only; human oral bioavailability not established. Rapid first-pass metabolism by polyamine oxidase.

spermidine trihydrochloride.absorption: similar to free base (salt dissociates in GI)
spermidine trihydrochloride.notes: Trihydrochloride salt form of spermidine (3 HCl per molecule for 3 nitrogen centers). GSRS parent: SPERMIDINE HYDROCHLORIDE (UNII 1O14BED398). Used in synthetic/purified spermidine supplements. Functionally equivalent to free base after GI dissolution; salt form improves stability and hygroscopic properties.

absorption_structured.notes: Salt dissociates; same polyamine absorption as free base

fresh spirulina paste.absorption: good (~85-90% protein digestibility; higher phycocyanin retention than dried)
fresh spirulina paste.notes: Unprocessed fresh spirulina with highest nutrient integrity. Phycocyanin and heat-sensitive enzymes are fully intact — spray drying causes ~20% phycocyanin loss (PMC10144176). However, no human study has directly compared fresh vs dried spirulina absorption. Protein digestibility of dried spirulina = 85% (PMC6179135, human dual-tracer ileal method); fresh is likely slightly higher due to intact cell structure. Rare in supplement form — most products are dried.

absorption_structured.notes: Protein digestibility ~85% measured on dried (PMC6179135); fresh likely slightly higher; phycocyanin retention ~20% better than spray-dried

organic spirulina.notes: Certified organic spirulina grown without synthetic fertilizers or pesticides. 'Organic' is a cultivation certification, not a bioavailability modifier — protein digestibility is the same as standard spirulina powder (~85%, PMC6179135). Spirulina has a peptidoglycan-based cell wall (not cellulose like chlorella), which is more readily digestible by human enzymes (PMC94402). May have lower heavy metal contamination due to controlled cultivation conditions.

absorption_structured.notes: Same protein digestibility as standard spirulina (~85%, PMC6179135); organic = cultivation practice, not PK modifier

spirulina powder.notes: Standard dried spirulina powder (Arthrospira platensis). Mean indispensable amino acid digestibility = 85.2% in humans via dual-tracer ileal method (PMC6179135). PDCAAS = 84%. Spirulina has a peptidoglycan cell wall (not cellulose), making it more digestible than chlorella without cell-wall cracking (PMC94402). Rich in protein (~60%), phycocyanin, and B-vitamins. Spray drying causes ~20% phycocyanin loss vs fresh (PMC10144176).

absorption_structured.notes: Mean IAA digestibility 85.2% (PMC6179135, human dual-tracer); PDCAAS 84%

spirulina tablets.absorption: good (~75-85% protein digestibility; compression may slow dissolution)
spirulina tablets.notes: Compressed spirulina tablets or capsules. Same base material as powder but compression into tablets may slightly slow dissolution and digestion onset. No direct human study comparing tablet vs powder spirulina bioavailability. Small penalty vs powder reflects theoretical slower disintegration. Convenient dosage form for standardized intake.

absorption_structured.notes: Powder digestibility ~85% (PMC6179135); small compression penalty estimated; no direct tablet PK study

spirulina (unspecified).notes: Spirulina form not specified on label. Scored conservatively. Human protein digestibility of dried spirulina is ~85% (PMC6179135). Spirulina has a peptidoglycan cell wall (more digestible than chlorella's cellulose wall).

phycocyanin extract (spirulina).notes: Purified C-phycocyanin from Arthrospira platensis (spirulina). Anti-inflammatory via PCB chromophore inhibiting NADPH oxidase and acting as bilirubin analog (PMC9185767). Intact protein MW 70-131 kDa limits direct GI absorption; phycocyanobilin is the bioavailable chromophore released upon digestion. Used in 'blue spirulina' products. Spray drying causes ~20% phycocyanin loss (PMC10144176).

absorption_structured.notes: Intact C-phycocyanin (70-131 kDa) poorly absorbed; PCB chromophore is absorbed species; microencapsulation improves delivery

fermented chlorella.absorption: estimated 50-65% (extrapolated from in vitro enzymatic digestion; no human PK study)
fermented chlorella.notes: Enzymatically fermented chlorella with pre-digested cell walls. In vitro enzymatic treatment raised protein bioaccessibility to ~59% (PMC5489447). 2024 human RCT (PMID:39610880) found split cell chlorella amino acid iAUC ~64% of milk protein — fermented form likely similar or slightly better. No direct human PK study on fermented chlorella specifically. Cell wall is sporopollenin/algaenan and chitin-like glucosamine, NOT cellulose (PMC8759652).

absorption_structured.notes: In vitro enzymatic digestion ~59% (PMC5489447); no direct human fermented chlorella PK study

broken cell wall chlorella.absorption: moderate-to-good (~50-65%; 2024 human RCT: ~64% of milk protein)
broken cell wall chlorella.notes: Chlorella with mechanically broken/split cell walls. 2024 human RCT (PMID:39610880, PMC11602285): split cell chlorella amino acid iAUC was 55.7 mmol/L vs milk at 86.6 mmol/L (~64% of milk). Dramatically better than intact cells which showed near-zero postprandial amino acid response. Cell wall is sporopollenin/algaenan-based (NOT cellulose; PMC8759652) — mechanical disruption is essential for human nutrient access.

absorption_structured.notes: Split cell chlorella AA iAUC ~64% of milk protein (PMID:39610880, PMC11602285)

chlorella powder.absorption: very poor in vivo (~5-20% estimated from human data)
chlorella powder.notes: Standard whole-cell chlorella powder with intact cell walls. 2024 human RCT (PMID:39610880): whole cell chlorella showed NEGATIVE postprandial amino acid iAUC (-4.1 mmol/L) — essentially no net amino acid appearance above baseline over 5 hours. In vitro digestibility (~50-60%) dramatically overestimates true human absorption. Cell wall is sporopollenin/algaenan (PMC8759652), resistant to human digestive enzymes. C. vulgaris and C. pyrenoidosa. Broken cell wall form vastly superior.

absorption_structured.notes: 2024 human RCT: negative amino acid iAUC for whole cell (PMID:39610880); in vitro 50-60% grossly overestimates

chlorella tablets.absorption: very poor to variable (depends on cell wall disruption status)
chlorella tablets.notes: Compressed chlorella tablets. Absorption depends entirely on whether cell walls are disrupted before tableting — most tablet products use intact cells unless labeled 'broken cell wall' or 'cracked cell.' If intact, absorption is near-zero for amino acids in humans (PMID:39610880). Tablet compression adds no benefit for cell wall disruption. Check label for cell wall status.

absorption_structured.notes: Assumed intact cell unless labeled otherwise; same poor absorption as whole cell powder

chlorella (unspecified).absorption: unknown (highly dependent on cell wall disruption)
chlorella (unspecified).notes: Chlorella form not specified on label. Scored conservatively. Human data (PMID:39610880): intact cell chlorella has near-zero amino acid absorption; broken/split cell achieves ~64% of milk protein. Cell wall is sporopollenin/algaenan, NOT cellulose (PMC8759652). Cell wall disruption status is the single most important factor.

absorption_structured.notes: Data not available; depends on cell wall disruption status

chlorella.rxcui_note: No RxNorm concept found via GSRS lookup

oligosaccharide HA.absorption: best among oral HA forms; systemic bioavailability still very low
oligosaccharide HA.notes: Ultra-low molecular weight HA (<10 kDa). Best oral HA form — oligosaccharides pass through intestinal epithelium in vitro. However, no human PK study quantifies systemic bioavailability. All oral HA has very low systemic bioavailability (~0.2% intact; PMID:37182970). Clinical skin hydration benefits are demonstrated (PMID:38009035) and likely mediated through degradation products and gut signaling, not intact HA reaching skin. HyaBest is a branded oligosaccharide form.

absorption_structured.notes: Best among HA forms but systemic bioavailability of intact HA still very low (~0.2%; PMID:37182970). Efficacy via degradation products.

liposomal HA.absorption: theoretical enhancement; no human oral PK data
liposomal HA.notes: Liposome-encapsulated HA for theoretical enhanced oral delivery. Zero published human oral PK studies exist for liposomal HA. Liposomal HA literature in PubMed is about injectable drug delivery (PMC7384933), not oral supplements. Claims of 2-3x improved absorption are extrapolated from liposomal delivery of other compounds and have no direct evidence for oral HA.

absorption_structured.notes: No human oral PK data exists for liposomal HA; theoretical enhancement only

acetylated HA.absorption: no human oral PK data; acetylation studied primarily for topical penetration
acetylated HA.notes: Chemically acetylated HA with improved stability. No published human oral PK data for acetylated HA. Acetylation is primarily studied for topical/dermal penetration enhancement, not oral bioavailability. Claims of ~2x improved absorption have zero clinical support for oral delivery. May have improved antioxidant activity vs native HA in vitro.

absorption_structured.notes: No human oral PK data; acetylation studied for topical, not oral delivery

low molecular weight HA.absorption: low systemic bioavailability; better than HMW-HA but far below 50%
low molecular weight HA.notes: Low molecular weight HA (10-100 kDa). Absorbed mainly in cecum after bacterial degradation (PMID:37182970). LMW-HA achieves modestly better tissue distribution than HMW-HA, but systemic bioavailability of intact HA is ~0.2%. Clinical benefits for skin hydration and joint support are demonstrated but likely mediated through degradation products and gut-immune signaling. Injuv is a branded LMW-HA form.

absorption_structured.notes: LMW-HA absorbed mainly in cecum after bacterial degradation (PMID:37182970); intact systemic bioavailability very low

high molecular weight HA.absorption: very low (~0.2% intact; Balogh 2008: >87% fecal excretion)
high molecular weight HA.notes: High molecular weight HA (>1000 kDa). Balogh 2008 (PMID:18959406): 86.7-95.6% of radiolabeled HMW-HA excreted in feces. The ~16% 'bioavailability' from that study measured radioactivity from degradation fragments, not intact HMW-HA. Systemic bioavailability of intact molecules is near zero. However, clinical trials show oral HMW-HA improves joint pain (PMC4281855) and skin hydration — effects likely via gut-immune axis signaling and bacterial degradation products.

absorption_structured.notes: ~0.2% intact bioavailability; Balogh 2008 (PMID:18959406): >87% fecal excretion; effects via degradation products

hyaluronic acid (unspecified).absorption: unknown (all oral HA has very low systemic bioavailability)
hyaluronic acid (unspecified).notes: HA molecular weight not specified. All oral HA has very low systemic bioavailability (~0.2% intact; PMID:37182970). Smaller MW forms have modestly better absorption. Clinical skin/joint benefits are real but mediated through degradation products and gut-immune signaling, not intact HA reaching target tissues. Sodium hyaluronate is the sodium salt form. Commercial HA is produced by bacterial fermentation.

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|Other|Hyaluronic Acid | 467 (Number of products:627)

alpha-carotene (unspecified).absorption: variable, fat-dependent (~5-20% from food, likely 20-50% from oil-based supplements)
alpha-carotene (unspecified).notes: Provitamin A carotenoid with half the vitamin A activity of beta-carotene (24 mcg = 1 mcg RAE vs 12 mcg for beta-carotene; NIH ODS). Same fat-dependent micellar absorption pathway as lutein and zeaxanthin. Recent data suggests greater apparent bioavailability from foods than beta-carotene (PMC7551262, Burri 2020). No human PK study specifically for isolated alpha-carotene supplements. Found naturally in carrots, pumpkin, and winter squash.

absorption_structured.notes: Fat-dependent carotenoid; same pathway as lutein; greater apparent bioavailability from foods than beta-carotene (PMC7551262)

alpha_carotene.rxcui_note: No RxNorm concept for supplement ingredient

R-alpha-lipoic acid.absorption: moderate (~38% absolute bioavailability from aqueous solution; Breithaupt-Grogler 1999)
R-alpha-lipoic acid.notes: The biologically active R-enantiomer of ALA. Breithaupt-Grogler 1999: 38% absolute bioavailability for R-enantiomer (vs 28% for S-enantiomer) from aqueous solution. Free R-ALA is prone to polymerization and poor aqueous solubility, reducing real-world bioavailability from solid dosage forms below the 38% figure. Keith 2012 (PMC4084596): in young adults, racemic ALA actually showed higher AUC than pure R-ALA, complicating the 'R is always better' narrative. R is the endogenous enantiomer but supplement R-ALA is synthetically produced.

absorption_structured.notes: 38% absolute bioavailability from aqueous solution (Breithaupt-Grogler 1999); lower from solid dosage due to polymerization

sodium r-lipoate.absorption: excellent (significantly higher Cmax and AUC vs free R-ALA; Carlson 2007)
sodium r-lipoate.notes: Sodium salt of R-alpha-lipoic acid (Na-RALA). Solves R-ALA's polymerization and aqueous solubility problems. Carlson 2007 (PMID:18069903): significantly higher Cmax (10x+) and AUC, with decreased Tmax and T1/2 vs both free R-ALA and racemic ALA. The most bioavailable oral ALA form with published human PK data. BioEnhanced Na-RALA is a branded stabilized form.

absorption_structured.notes: 10x+ higher Cmax and significantly higher AUC vs free R-ALA (Carlson 2007, PMID:18069903)

liposomal alpha-lipoic acid.absorption: theoretical enhancement; no human oral PK data
liposomal alpha-lipoic acid.notes: Liposome-encapsulated ALA for theoretical enhanced oral delivery. No published human oral PK study exists for liposomal ALA. Claims of 2-3x improved absorption are extrapolated from liposomal delivery principles applied to other compounds. Scored above racemic but below forms with actual human PK data (Na-RALA, free R-ALA).

absorption_structured.notes: No human oral PK data; theoretical enhancement from liposomal delivery

granulated alpha-lipoic acid.absorption: theoretical gastric protection; no published human PK comparison
granulated alpha-lipoic acid.notes: Polymer-coated ALA granules designed to resist stomach acid degradation. No published human PK study comparing granulated vs standard ALA exists. The 1.5x improvement claim appears to be manufacturer marketing without peer-reviewed clinical support. May modestly improve stability of acid-sensitive ALA, but bioavailability advantage is unproven.

absorption_structured.notes: No published human PK comparison vs standard ALA; theoretical gastric protection

racemic alpha-lipoic acid.notes: Racemic (50:50 R+S) alpha-lipoic acid, the most common and cheapest form. Breithaupt-Grogler 1999: absolute bioavailability ~30% overall (R-component 38%, S-component 28%). Linus Pauling Institute review: 30-40%. Short half-life ~0.3-0.4 hours. Keith 2012 (PMC4084596): in young adults, racemic ALA actually showed higher AUC (1,772 ng-h/mL) than pure R-ALA (1,070 ng-h/mL). Thioctic acid is the pharmaceutical name.

alpha lipoic acid (unspecified).notes: ALA form/enantiomer not specified. Scored conservatively. Na-RALA (sodium R-lipoate) has the best human PK data with 10x+ Cmax vs free R-ALA (Carlson 2007, PMID:18069903). R-ALA has 38% absolute bioavailability (Breithaupt-Grogler 1999). Racemic ALA is the most common form at ~30% overall F%.

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|Chemical|Alpha Lipoic Acid | 8 (Number of products:2523)

MSM (methylsulfonylmethane).absorption: high (extrapolated from rat ~86% urinary recovery; no human F% published)
MSM (methylsulfonylmethane).notes: Standard MSM (dimethyl sulfone, MW 94.13) — small water-soluble organic sulfur compound. Rat PK showed 86% urinary recovery over 120h (PMID:17263509, Magnuson 2007). Human steady-state plasma: 470-700 µM at 1g/day (Lin 2020 LC-MS/MS). No formal human oral bioavailability calculated (no IV reference). OptiMSM is distillation-purified (99.9%) but no PK comparison vs crystallized MSM exists.

absorption_structured.notes: Rat urinary recovery 85.8% (PMID:17263509); no human IV reference arm

msm (methylsulfonylmethane) (unspecified.notes: MSM form not specified. Small water-soluble molecule (MW 94.13) with apparent high oral absorption based on rat data (PMID:17263509). OptiMSM is the gold-standard distillation-purified form. Scored conservatively until exact form is identified.

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|chemical|Methylsulfonylmethane | 651 (Number of products:1171)

KSM-66 ashwagandha.absorption: moderate (low plasma withanolides measured in human PK)
KSM-66 ashwagandha.notes: Patented full-spectrum root-only extract with ~5% withanolides (Ixoreal Biomed). Clinically validated for cortisol, stress, testosterone in 20+ RCTs. However, human PK (PMID:34254920) showed very low plasma withanolide A (Cmax 0.09 ng/mL, t1/2 0.74h). Clinical efficacy may be driven by gut-level or metabolite effects rather than systemic withanolide exposure.

absorption_structured.notes: Withanolide A Cmax 0.09 ng/mL from 30mg withanolides (PMID:34254920)

Shoden ashwagandha extract.absorption: excellent (5.6x Cmax, 18x AUC vs standard 2.5% extract in human PK)
Shoden ashwagandha extract.notes: Shoden (Arjuna Natural) ashwagandha extract standardized to ≥35% withanolide glycosides. Human crossover PK (PMID:38144272, n=16): total withanolide Cmax 60.66 ng/mL (5.6x vs WS-2.5), AUC 815 ng·h/mL (18.2x), t1/2 9.76h (5.2x). Withanolide glycosides identified as the key driver of superior oral bioavailability. Best PK data of any ashwagandha extract.
aliases: Shoden ashwagandha root and leaf extract
shoden ashwagandha (withania somnifera) root and leaf extract
Shoden Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) Root and Leaf
Shoden Ashwagandha Withania somnifera Root and Leaf extract
Shoden ashwagandha roots and leaves extract
ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) roots and leaves 35% withanolide glycosides
ashwagandha root & leaf extract 35% withanolide glycosides
shoden withania somnifera root and leaf
ashwagandha shoden brand root and leaf extract

liposomal ashwagandha.notes: Lipid-encapsulated ashwagandha. NooGandha (Specnova) is a branded liposomal form with clinical outcome data (PMID:38931168, 225mg/day improved cognition and cortisol in 59 subjects) but NO plasma withanolide PK comparison vs conventional extracts. Claims of enhanced bioavailability lack pharmacokinetic evidence.

absorption_structured.notes: No plasma withanolide PK comparison published

sensoril ashwagandha.absorption: moderate (no direct human PK data; 10% withanolide glycosides)
sensoril ashwagandha.notes: Patented leaf+root extract (Natreon) standardized to ≥10% withanolide glycosides and ≥32% total withanolides. Strong RCT evidence for cortisol reduction and stress relief. Higher withanolide glycoside content than KSM-66 may improve absorption (PMID:38144272 showed glycosides drive PK), but no direct PK study for Sensoril exists.
aliases: sensoril trim ashwagandha withania somnifera extract
ashwagandha sensoril brand root and leaf extract
sensoril ashwagandha root and leaf extract
sensoril ashwagandha withania somnifera root and leaf extract
sensoril ashwagandha (withania somnifera) root & leaf extract
ashwagandha (sensoril brand) root and leaf extract

absorption_structured.notes: No direct PK; extrapolated from withanolide glycoside content vs Shoden data

standard ashwagandha extract.notes: Non-branded ashwagandha extract with variable withanolide content (typically 1.5-5%). Bioavailability depends on extraction method and standardization. Withanolide glycoside content is the key driver of oral absorption (PMID:38144272). Less predictable than patented forms like Shoden, KSM-66, or Sensoril.

ashwagandha powder.notes: Whole dried root powder used in traditional Ayurvedic medicine. Lower withanolide concentration than extracts, requiring higher doses (3-6 g/day). Withanolide glycoside content drives absorption (PMID:38144272); raw powder has minimal glycoside enrichment. Provides the full root matrix but with less predictable potency.

ashwagandha (unspecified).notes: Form not specified on the label. Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) products range from raw root powder to highly concentrated extracts like KSM-66 and Sensoril. Withanolide content and bioavailability vary widely. Scored conservatively until the exact form is identified.

ashwagandha.rxcui_note: No RxNorm concept found via GSRS lookup

rice protein isolate.notes: Concentrated rice protein (≥80% protein). DIAAS 0.42-0.50 (lysine-limited) vs whey at 1.09 (PMID:28382889). At 48g dose, comparable to whey for lean mass gains in trained males (PMID:23782948, Joy 2013) — but this is dose-compensated. Hypoallergenic and vegan-friendly. Low lysine is the primary limitation.

absorption_structured.notes: DIAAS 0.42-0.50 lysine-limited (PMID:28382889); comparable to whey at high dose (PMID:23782948)

rice protein.notes: General brown rice protein (60-70% protein). DIAAS 0.37 for rice protein concentrate (PMID:28382889, Mathai 2017). Lower protein concentration than isolate with more fiber and anti-nutrients. Lysine is first-limiting amino acid. Hypoallergenic and suitable for dairy/soy sensitivities.

rice_protein.cui_note: No UMLS entry for botanical/novel compound
rice_protein.rxcui_note: No RxNorm concept for supplement ingredient

rhodiola rosea extract (3% rosavins).absorption: variable (salidroside 75-90%, rosavin only 20-26% in rat IV reference)
rhodiola rosea extract (3% rosavins).notes: Standardized to 3% rosavins and 1% salidroside (clinical gold-standard ratio, SHR-5). Salidroside well-absorbed (75-90% rat F, Panossian 2010; human plasma ~587 ng/mL at 1h). Rosavin poorly absorbed (20-26% rat F, 3x lower AUC than salidroside). Strong evidence for fatigue reduction and stress resilience. EGb-761-level standardization ensures consistent bioactive content.

absorption_structured.notes: Salidroside 75-90% F (rat), rosavin 20-26% F (rat); no human IV reference (Panossian 2010, PMC5976749)

rhodiola root powder.notes: Dried ground rhodiola root without standardization. Rosavin and salidroside content varies by harvest and growing conditions. Salidroside is well-absorbed (75-90% rat F) but rosavin is poorly absorbed (20-26% rat F, Panossian 2010; cited in PMC5976749). Non-standardized means unpredictable bioactive levels.

rhodiola (unspecified).notes: Form not specified on the label. Rhodiola rosea is valued for rosavins and salidroside, but potency varies widely between raw root powder and standardized extracts. Scored conservatively until the exact form is identified.

rhodiola.rxcui_note: No RxNorm concept for supplement ingredient

liposomal ginkgo.notes: Liposome-encapsulated ginkgo (Ginkgosome). Human crossover PK (PMID:35922794, Ried 2022, n=13): ginkgolide B AUC 1.9x higher, ginkgolide C AUC 2.2x higher, Cmax 1.7x higher vs standard GBE. One of few liposomal forms with real human PK support. Sustained-release profile.

absorption_structured.notes: Ginkgolide B AUC 1.9x, C AUC 2.2x vs standard GBE (PMID:35922794)

ginkgo biloba extract (24% flavone glyco.notes: Gold-standard ginkgo extract standardized to 24% flavone glycosides and 6% terpene lactones. Absolute oral bioavailability: ginkgolide A 80%, ginkgolide B 88%, bilobalide 79% (PMID:7631288, Fourtillan 1995 IV reference). Flavone glycosides extensively conjugated (low free form). EGb 761 is the most-studied branded version with strong evidence for cerebral blood flow and cognitive function.

absorption_structured.notes: Terpene lactone F: ginkgolide A 80%, B 88%, bilobalide 79% (PMID:7631288)

ginkgo leaf powder.notes: Dried ground ginkgo leaf without standardization. Substantially lower plasma terpene levels than EGb 761 — tincture showed 7.6x lower bilobalide Cmax (PMID:20041430, Woelkart 2010). Variable flavone glycoside and terpene lactone content. May contain higher ginkgolic acids (allergen) than refined extracts.

ginkgo (unspecified).notes: Form not specified. Ginkgo terpene lactones have 79-88% oral F (PMID:7631288) in standardized extracts, but crude preparations vary enormously (PMID:20041430). Scored conservatively until form is confirmed.

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|Botanical|Ginkgo | 71 (Number of products:1552)

silymarin phytosome.notes: Silybin-phosphatidylcholine complex (Siliphos/IdB 1016). Human crossover PK (PMID:2088770, Barzaghi 1990, n=9): 3.4x relative bioavailability vs standard silymarin at equivalent 360mg silibinin. The '4-10x' claim is inflated — 3.4x is the peer-reviewed figure. Still the best-studied enhanced silymarin form for hepatoprotection.
aliases: milk thistle silybum marianum fruit phytosome

absorption_structured.notes: 3.4x relative F vs standard silymarin (PMID:2088770, Barzaghi 1990)

micellar silymarin.notes: Micelle-encapsulated silymarin (LipoMicel). Human crossover PK (PMID:40733088, 2025, n=16, 130mg): Cmax 18.9x higher, AUC0-24 11.4x higher vs standard silymarin. Tmax 0.5h vs 2.5h for standard. Superior to phytosome (3.4x) by a wide margin. Limited long-term clinical data vs phytosome.

standard silymarin.notes: Conventional milk thistle extract standardized to ~80% silymarin. GI absorption 23-47%, but systemic free silybin only ~0.73% due to extensive glucuronidation (PMID:1572758, Weyhenmeyer 1992). Conjugated metabolites 4-30x higher than free fractions (PMID:17913795, Wen 2008). Most common and affordable form.

absorption_structured.notes: GI absorption 23-47% but free silybin F ~0.73% (PMID:1572758)

milk thistle seed powder.notes: Ground whole milk thistle seeds without extraction or standardization. Lower and more variable silymarin content than 80% extracts. Standard silymarin itself has very low free silybin bioavailability (~0.73%, PMID:1572758); crude seed powder would be even lower. Traditional use in herbal teas.
aliases: milk thistle fruit and seed extract

absorption_structured.notes: Lower and more variable than standardized 80% extract; free silybin F ~0.73% (PMID:1572758)

milk thistle (unspecified).notes: Form not specified on the label. Milk thistle (Silybum marianum) products range from crude seed powder to phytosome-complexed silybin. Silymarin content and bioavailability vary dramatically between forms. Scored conservatively until the exact form is identified.

silybin (isolated).notes: Silybin (silibinin) is the most abundant flavonolignan in silymarin (50-70%). Absolute oral F ~0.95% as free compound (PMID 14692709); GI absorption 20-50% but extensive glucuronidation (55%) and sulfation (28%) (PMID 1572758). Hepatoprotective: evidence for alcoholic liver disease, NAFLD, chemo-hepatotoxicity. Phytosome (IdB 1016) improves F 3.4x.

absorption_structured.notes: Absolute oral F ~0.95%; GI absorption 20-50% but 83% conjugated (PMID 14692709, PMID 1572758)

silychristin (minor flavonolignan).notes: Silychristin comprises ~20% of silymarin. Lowest systemic exposure of major silymarin flavonolignans (PMC3876803). Most potent MCT8 thyroid transporter inhibitor in silymarin complex (PMID 26910310). No standalone clinical data.

absorption_structured.notes: Lowest plasma exposure among silymarin flavonolignans (PMC3876803)

silydianin (minor flavonolignan).notes: Silydianin comprises ~10% of silymarin. Barely detectable in plasma at standard milk thistle doses (PMC3876803). No standalone clinical trials; minor constituent marker only.

absorption_structured.notes: Minimal systemic exposure; lowest AUC of all silymarin flavonolignans (PMC3876803)

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|Botanical|Milk Thistle | 652 (Number of products:1563)

suntheanine (Taiyo).notes: Patented enzymatically-produced pure L-isomer theanine by Taiyo International (40+ patents). Fermentation-based process using gamma-glutamyltranspeptidase yields 99%+ pure L-(S) enantiomer with no D-theanine contamination. Oral bioavailability ~70% (65-74% across doses in mouse PK; human PK consistent). Na+-coupled co-transporter in intestinal brush border; Tmax ~50 min, t1/2 ~65 min. Crosses blood-brain barrier. 200+ studies, 40+ clinical trials. Single 200 mg dose significantly increases frontal alpha brain wave power (relaxation) and reduces salivary cortisol under acute stress (PMID:34562208). 400 mg/day for 28 days decreased perceived stress and improved cognitive attention in triple-blind RCT (PMID:38758503).

absorption_structured.notes: ~70% oral F in animal PK (65-74% across doses); human PK consistent with rapid, efficient absorption; Tmax ~50 min

l-theanine powder.absorption: good (~70% when pure L-isomer; variable purity in generic)
l-theanine powder.notes: Generic free-form L-theanine, typically produced by microbial fermentation or chemical synthesis (not tea extraction). Same molecule as Suntheanine when pure L-isomer — identical chemical structure and optical activity with no measurable difference in absorption or metabolism. However, lower-grade extracts may contain D-theanine isomer which can hinder L-form absorption. Variable purity and no branded QC guarantee. Na+-coupled intestinal transport; Tmax ~50 min. Promotes alpha brain waves and calm focus at 200-400 mg/day (PMID:34562208, PMID:38758503).

absorption_structured.notes: ~70% when pure L-isomer; variable if racemic mixture present

l-theanine (unspecified).notes: Form not specified on the label. L-Theanine is an amino acid found naturally in tea leaves (Camellia sinensis) but most supplements use enzymatic fermentation. Suntheanine is a patented pure L-isomer form; generic products vary in purity. Well-absorbed orally (~70% when pure L-isomer) with good blood-brain barrier penetration. Scored conservatively until form details confirmed.

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|Amino Acid|Theanine | 607 (Number of products:983)

collagen tripeptides.absorption: high (absorbed largely intact; mouse GI absorption ~95%, PMID:10498764)
collagen tripeptides.notes: Ultra-small collagen fragments (~500 Da, e.g., Gly-Pro-Hyp). Enzymatically stable and absorbed nearly intact across intestinal epithelium (PMID:27573716, Sato 2016; PMID:26155906, Sugihara 2015). Higher plasma Pro-Hyp levels than standard hydrolyzed collagen. The '95%' claim derives from mouse GI absorption (PMID:10498764, Oesser 1999), not human systemic bioavailability.

absorption_structured.notes: ~500 Da tripeptides absorbed largely intact (PMID:27573716, PMID:26155906)

hydrolyzed collagen peptides.absorption: good (mouse GI ~95% but most digested to amino acids; PMID:16076145)
hydrolyzed collagen peptides.notes: Enzymatically broken collagen (2-5 kDa). Mouse GI absorption ~95% (PMID:10498764, Oesser 1999) but most is digested to amino acids; only a fraction reaches blood as intact peptides. Pro-Hyp peaks at 20-60 nmol/mL plasma at 1-2h after 9-23g oral dose (PMID:16076145, Iwai 2005). Verisol (skin), Fortigel (joints), and BioCell are well-studied branded versions.
aliases: biocell collagen proprietary sternal cartilage extract

absorption_structured.notes: GI absorption high but intact peptide delivery lower (PMID:10498764, PMID:16076145)

undenatured collagen.absorption: oral tolerance mechanism (gut immune modulation, not systemic absorption)
undenatured collagen.notes: Native (non-hydrolyzed) type II collagen that works via oral tolerance — activating Treg cells in Peyer's patches to suppress immune attack on joint cartilage (PMID:12837047, Bagchi 2002). UC-II is the leading brand. Effective at just 40 mg/day: WOMAC 33% reduction vs 14% for glucosamine+chondroitin (PMID:19847319, Crowley 2009; PMID:26822714, Lugo 2016). Absorption % is irrelevant — works via gut immune modulation, not systemic delivery.

absorption_structured.notes: Oral tolerance mechanism via GALT; systemic absorption not the mechanism (PMID:12837047)

gelatin.notes: Partially hydrolyzed collagen (cooked form, higher MW than peptides). Shaw 2017 (PMID:27852613) showed gelatin increases blood glycine, proline, and hydroxyproline and enhances collagen synthesis markers. Slower absorption and lower bioactive peptide delivery than fully hydrolyzed forms. Same amino acid profile but requires more digestive processing.

collagen (unspecified).notes: Form not specified on the label. Collagen products range from intact gelatin to highly hydrolyzed peptides and tripeptides, with dramatically different absorption profiles. Type (I, II, III) and source (bovine, marine, chicken) also vary. Scored conservatively until the exact form is identified.

collagen.rxcui_note: No RxNorm concept found via GSRS lookup

liposomal melatonin.absorption: unknown (no comparative PK data; clinical outcomes only)
liposomal melatonin.notes: Lipid-encapsulated melatonin (Melatosome). Bruni 2022 (PMID:36612875) showed reduced sleep latency in 100 pediatric patients vs placebo, but measured NO plasma melatonin levels. Zero published human PK study comparing liposomal vs standard oral melatonin Cmax/AUC. Claims of 90-95% absorption are unsupported.

absorption_structured.notes: No plasma melatonin PK comparison published (PMID:36612875 is outcomes only)

oral spray melatonin.absorption: enhanced (~1.9x vs oral PR; bypasses first-pass)
oral spray melatonin.notes: Absorbed through oral mucosa, partially bypassing first-pass hepatic CYP1A2 metabolism. Human crossover PK (PMID:37438493, Luthringer 2023, n=14): sublingual F was 1.89x higher than prolonged-release oral. If oral F ~15% (PMID:10883420), sublingual F ~28%. Fastest onset (10-15 min). Best for sleep initiation.

absorption_structured.notes: 1.89x vs oral PR (PMID:37438493); if oral F ~15%, sublingual ~28%

immediate-release melatonin.absorption: low (~15% F due to extensive first-pass metabolism)
immediate-release melatonin.notes: Standard oral melatonin. Absolute F = 14.3-15.9% at 2-4mg (PMID:10883420, DeMuro 2000), range 9-33% across studies (PMID:26008214, Harpsoe 2015 review). Extensive first-pass CYP1A2 6-hydroxylation. Tmax ~50 min, t1/2 ~45 min. Despite low F, clinically effective — melatonin receptors respond to very low plasma levels.

delayed-release melatonin.absorption: low (~15% F, timed to peak at 3-5h)
delayed-release melatonin.notes: Formulated to release melatonin 3-5 hours after ingestion. Same ~15% bioavailability as IR (PMID:10883420, DeMuro 2000) but shifted Tmax. Targets middle-of-night waking (sleep maintenance insomnia). Take before bed to maintain melatonin through the second half of the night.
aliases: melatonin with zinc and lemon balm

absorption_structured.notes: Same F as IR (~15%, PMID:10883420) with delayed release

extended-release melatonin.notes: Gradually releases melatonin over 6-8 hours, mimicking natural nocturnal secretion. Circadin PR: t1/2 3.5-4h (vs ~45 min for IR), Cmax ~1176 pg/mL fasted. Same ~15% overall F (PMID:10883420) but lower peak and longer duration. Best for those who fall asleep fine but wake too early.

melatonin (unspecified).notes: Form not specified. Oral melatonin F = 14.3-15.9% (PMID:10883420, DeMuro 2000) with high inter-individual variability (9-33%, PMID:26008214). Extensive first-pass CYP1A2 metabolism. Despite low F, clinically effective at 0.5-5 mg doses. Scored conservatively until form is confirmed.

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|Hormone|Melatonin | 517 (Number of products:895)

glucosamine hydrochloride.absorption: moderate (~26% systemic F; ~90% GI absorption but extensive first-pass)
glucosamine hydrochloride.notes: Hydrochloride salt with highest glucosamine per gram (~83% vs ~65% for sulfate). The '90-95% absorption' claim is misleading — Setnikar 1993 (PMID:8267678) showed ~90% GI absorption but only ~26% systemic free glucosamine F (IV reference). Equine PK (PMID:18295513) showed lower bioavailability than sulfate (6.1% vs 9.4%). Available in shellfish-free (corn-derived) versions.

absorption_structured.notes: Systemic F ~26% (PMID:8267678); GI absorption ~90% but extensive first-pass

glucosamine sulfate (crystalline).notes: Crystalline glucosamine sulfate (DONA/Rottapharm) — the European gold-standard prescription form. Human PK (PMID:16168682, Persiani 2005): ~10 µM steady-state plasma at 1500mg QD, linear PK. Synovial fluid reaches near 1:1 with plasma (PMID:17353133). Systemic F 26-44% (PMID:8267678). Strongest clinical evidence for slowing knee OA cartilage loss.

n-acetyl glucosamine (NAG).absorption: unknown (absorbed but no formal F% data)
n-acetyl glucosamine (NAG).notes: Acetylated glucosamine for glycosaminoglycan synthesis. Pilot study (PMID:9001835, Talent 1996, n=10) confirmed oral absorption and serum detection, but no formal PK parameters (Cmax, AUC, F%) were reported. Partially converted to glucosamine in vivo. Preferred for GI-focused protocols (gut mucosal health) rather than systemic joint delivery.

absorption_structured.notes: Absorbed but no formal PK; pilot only (PMID:9001835)

glucosamine sulfate (non-crystalline).absorption: low-moderate (likely lower than crystalline; no direct PK comparison)
glucosamine sulfate (non-crystalline).notes: Non-crystalline glucosamine sulfate without stabilizing counter-ion (NaCl/KCl). More hygroscopic and prone to degradation. No direct human PK comparison vs crystalline form exists, but negative GAIT trial (PMID:16495392) used HCl not crystalline sulfate. Likely lower and more variable absorption than crystalline DONA form.

absorption_structured.notes: Estimated lower than crystalline; no direct PK data

glucosamine (unspecified).notes: Form not specified on the label. Glucosamine is available as HCl (highest concentration), crystalline sulfate (most studied), NAG (gut-focused), and non-crystalline sulfate (least stable). Salt form significantly affects both potency and absorption. Scored conservatively until form is confirmed.

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|TBD|Glucosamine (unspecified) | 2165 (Number of products:92)

low molecular weight chondroitin.absorption: low (~10-15% F; theoretical LMW advantage unproven in humans)
low molecular weight chondroitin.notes: Low molecular weight chondroitin sulfate (<10 kDa). In vitro data shows 12.6x higher absorption vs native CS (PMID:24524826), but no human PK comparison of LMW vs standard CS exists. Overall CS oral F is ~12% in humans (PMID:9743814, Ronca 1998). MW affects Tmax but total F remains in the 10-15% range.

absorption_structured.notes: CS F ~12% human (PMID:9743814); LMW advantage theoretical

chondroitin sulfate.notes: Bovine-derived chondroitin sulfate (typically 14-26 kDa). Absolute oral F = 12% in humans (PMID:9743814, Ronca 1998). Plasma CS >200% above baseline at Tmax ~2h for bovine source (PMID:12359162, Volpi 2002). Ichthyic (shark) CS absorbs slower (Tmax 8.7h, PMID:12801483). Scintigraphy shows tropism for joint tissue. Condrosulf is the best-studied brand.

chondroitin (unspecified).notes: Form not specified on the label. Chondroitin sulfate is a large glycosaminoglycan molecule with inherently poor oral absorption (~13–15% for standard form). Low-molecular-weight and phytosome forms improve this significantly. Source (shark, bovine, or avian) and molecular weight affect bioavailability. Scored conservatively.

chondroitin.rxcui_note: No RxNorm concept found via GSRS lookup

green tea phytosome.notes: Greenselect Phytosome (Indena) — phosphatidylcholine-complexed green tea catechins. Human PK (PMID:9861443, Pietta 1998): ~3x higher EGCG plasma AUC vs unformulated extract at equivalent 400mg EGCG dose. Confirmed by Kidd 2009 review (PMID:19803548). Still very low absolute F but best-enhanced green tea form with human PK support.
aliases: Camellia sinensis Leaf Extract, Decaffeinated, Phospholipid Complex

absorption_structured.notes: ~3x vs unformulated (PMID:9861443); absolute F still very low

green tea extract (50% EGCG).notes: Standardized green tea extract with ≥50% EGCG. EGCG Cmax 326 ng/mL after 4.5g green tea solids (PMID:9568793, Yang 1998). t1/2 5.0-5.5h. Chronic dosing (800mg/day x 4wks) increases AUC >60% suggesting conjugation pathway saturation (PMID:12960117, Chow 2003). Standard form for clinical use.

absorption_structured.notes: EGCG F <1%; Cmax 326 ng/mL at 4.5g dose (PMID:9568793)

matcha powder.notes: Whole-leaf green tea powder. Contains ~137x more EGCG per gram vs brewed tea (PMID:14518774, Weiss 2003), but whole-leaf matrix (fiber, protein) may alter absorption kinetics vs isolated extracts. No direct human PK comparison vs standardized extract exists. Variable catechin content depending on grade and preparation.

absorption_structured.notes: No PK comparison vs extract; whole-leaf matrix effects unknown

green tea catechins.absorption: very low (<1% F for EGCG; extensive first-pass conjugation)
green tea catechins.notes: High-purity catechin isolate (up to 98% polyphenols). EGCG oral F estimated <1% due to extensive glucuronidation, sulfation, and efflux (PMID:12960117, Chow 2003). Cmax 130-3392 ng/mL at 50-1600mg pure EGCG (PMID:12760312, Ullmann 2003). EGCG uniquely present >92% in free (unconjugated) form in plasma. Clinically effective despite low F — active at achievable plasma levels.
aliases: green tea (camellia sinensis) leaf std. extract
green tea (camellia sinensis) leaf standard extract
decaffeinated green tea (camellia sinensis) leaf extract
organic green tea camellia sinensis leaf aqueous extract

green tea extract (unspecified).notes: Form not specified. EGCG oral F is <1% even for standardized extracts (PMID:12960117). Greenselect Phytosome provides ~3x enhancement (PMID:9861443). Scored conservatively until form and EGCG content are confirmed.

green_tea_extract.rxcui_note: No RxNorm concept found via GSRS lookup

grape seed phytosome.absorption: enhanced (Leucoselect Phytosome; improved antioxidant delivery but fold-enhancement not quantified)
grape seed phytosome.notes: Leucoselect Phytosome (Indena) — procyanidin-phosphatidylcholine complex. Human study (PMID:9875688, Vinson 1998): increased serum antioxidant capacity after 5 days, but no direct PK fold-enhancement quantified (unlike silymarin/green tea phytosomes). Reviewed in Kidd 2009 (PMID:19803548). Enhancement expected based on phytosome mechanism but magnitude uncertain.
aliases: Leucoselect Phytosome standardized Grape seed extract

absorption_structured.notes: Enhanced antioxidant delivery (PMID:9875688) but PK fold not quantified

grape seed extract (95% proanthocyanidin.absorption: very low for intact OPCs (<5%); monomers ~10-30%
grape seed extract (95% proanthocyanidin.notes: Standardized to ≥95% proanthocyanidins (OPCs). Only monomers (catechin/epicatechin, 5-10% of extract) are well-absorbed (~10-30%). Dimers detectable at trace nmol/L (PMID:12834296, Sano 2003 — first intact dimer B1 in human serum). Oligomers/polymers NOT absorbed intact — undergo colonic microbial degradation to valerolactones and phenolic acids (PMID:15315398). Effective clinical benefits may be mediated by microbial catabolites.
aliases: MegaNatural(R)-BP Grape Seed (Vitis vinifera) Non-GMO Extract

absorption_structured.notes: Monomers 10-30% F; intact OPCs <5%; polymers not absorbed (PMID:12834296)

whole grape extract.notes: Whole grape extract with lower proanthocyanidin concentration than 95% standardized forms. Contains additional grape polyphenols (resveratrol, anthocyanins, quercetin) but OPC content variable. Same absorption limitations as standardized OPCs — only monomers and microbial catabolites reach systemic circulation (PMID:15640486, Manach 2005).
aliases: BioVin Grape Skin and Seed extract

absorption_structured.notes: Lower OPC concentration; same absorption limitations (PMID:15640486)

grape seed extract (unspecified).notes: Form not specified. Grape seed OPCs have very low intact absorption — only monomers (5-10% of extract) are well-absorbed (PMID:12834296). Scored conservatively until OPC standardization level is confirmed.

standardized red wine extract.absorption: very low (<1% F for free resveratrol; polyphenols extensively conjugated)
standardized red wine extract.notes: Red wine polyphenol extract (60-80% proanthocyanidins, <1% resveratrol). Free trans-resveratrol F <1% — absorption is ~70% but rapid sulfation/glucuronidation (PMID:15333514, Walle 2004). At 500mg: free resveratrol Cmax only 71 ng/mL while metabolites are 20-60x higher (PMID:26889234). Proanthocyanidin absorption same limitations as grape seed — only monomers absorbed.

absorption_structured.notes: Free resveratrol F <1% (PMID:15333514); GI absorption ~70% but extensive first-pass

red wine extract (unspecified).notes: Form not specified. Red wine polyphenols have very low bioavailability — free resveratrol F <1% (PMID:15333514). Proanthocyanidins same limitations as grape seed OPCs. Scored conservatively.

red_wine_extract.cui_note: No UMLS entry for botanical/novel compound
red_wine_extract.rxcui_note: No RxNorm concept for supplement ingredient

pycnogenol.absorption: low for procyanidins; only monomers and phenolic acids absorbed
pycnogenol.notes: Patented French maritime pine (Pinus pinaster) bark extract, 70±5% procyanidins. Procyanidin oligomers/polymers (~70% of extract) NOT absorbed intact (PMID:38757126, Bayer 2024 review). Only monomers absorbed: catechin Cmax 107 ng/mL, taxifolin 33 ng/mL, caffeic acid 17 ng/mL after 300mg (PMID:16887024, Grimm 2006). Microbial metabolite M1 (valerolactone) peaks at 8-10h. Ferulic acid preferentially distributes to synovial fluid (PMID:28452960).
aliases: Pycnogenol French Maritime Pine bark extract
pycnogenol dried french maritime pine extract
oligopin french maritime pine (pinus pinaster) extract

absorption_structured.notes: Only monomers absorbed; catechin Cmax 107 ng/mL at 300mg (PMID:16887024)

generic pine bark extract.notes: Non-Pycnogenol pine bark extract from various Pinus species (thunbergii, densiflora, massoniana, radiata). Variable procyanidin content and composition. Pycnogenol research cannot be extrapolated to other species (PMID:38757126). Same absorption limitation: procyanidin oligomers not absorbed intact, only monomers and microbial catabolites reach circulation.
aliases: Enzogenol New Zealand Pine bark (Pinus radiata) extract

absorption_structured.notes: Variable species; same procyanidin absorption limitation as Pycnogenol

pine bark extract (unspecified).notes: Form not specified. Pine bark procyanidins are poorly absorbed intact — only monomers and microbial catabolites are bioavailable (PMID:38757126). Pycnogenol is the gold-standard branded form. Scored conservatively.

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|Botanical|Pycnogenol | 104 (Number of products:77)

fermented red ginseng.absorption: dramatically enhanced (Compound K AUC 115-135x vs standard red ginseng)
fermented red ginseng.notes: Fermented red ginseng pre-converts protopanaxadiol ginsenosides (Rb1) to Compound K. Human PK (PMID:27516803, Choi 2016, n=24): CK Cmax 254 ng/mL, AUC 115x higher than standard RG. Confirmed by Lee 2021 (PMID:33884767): CK-30 showed 135x AUC, 118x Cmax, Tmax 3h (vs 10h for RG). Best PK data of any ginseng form.

absorption_structured.notes: Compound K AUC 115-135x vs standard RG (PMID:27516803, PMID:33884767)

black ginseng.absorption: enhanced (79-824% higher ginsenoside exposure vs red ginseng)
black ginseng.notes: Nine cycles of steaming/drying convert high-MW ginsenosides (Rb1) to low-MW forms (Rg3, Rk1, Rg5). Human PK (PMID:33918329, Kim 2021): Rg3+Rg5+Rk1 AUC 824% higher, total ginsenoside exposure 79% greater, Tmax 1h faster vs red ginseng. Less dramatic than fermentation but still significantly improved PK.

absorption_structured.notes: Total ginsenoside AUC 79% higher vs RG (PMID:33918329)

panax ginseng extract (4\u20137% ginseno.notes: Standard Panax ginseng extract with 4-7% total ginsenosides. Ginsenoside Rb1 F = 1.2-4.3%, Rg1 F ~18.4% (PMID:32148404, Jin 2020). Plasma Rb1 Cmax only 3-4 ng/mL from standard RGE. Compound K (the main bioactive metabolite) forms slowly via gut microbiome, Tmax 8-12h. Well-tolerated after 15 days repeated dosing.
aliases: Asian Ginseng root (Panax Ginseng) standardized extract

american ginseng (panax quinquefolius).absorption: very low (similar ginsenoside F to P. ginseng; no direct PK comparison)
american ginseng (panax quinquefolius).notes: Panax quinquefolius — higher Rb1/Re/Rd content vs P. ginseng, lower Rg1 (PMID:18718179, Qi 2008). No direct head-to-head human PK comparison exists. Same ginsenoside absorption limitations as P. ginseng (Rb1 F 1-4.3%). Different ginsenoside ratio gives calming vs stimulant profile.

absorption_structured.notes: Similar ginsenoside F to P. ginseng; no direct PK data (PMID:18718179)

siberian ginseng (eleuthero).notes: NOT true ginseng — Eleutherococcus senticosus, different genus, contains NO ginsenosides. Active compounds: eleutherosides B (syringin) and E (syringaresinol). Very poor oral bioavailability — eleutheroside K F = 0.30%. Adaptogenic effects well-documented but PK data is limited compared to Panax species (PMID:18718179 for comparison context).

absorption_structured.notes: Eleutheroside K F = 0.30%; NOT true ginseng

ginseng (unspecified).notes: Form not specified on the label. Ginseng products span multiple species (Panax ginseng, P. quinquefolius, Eleutherococcus), processing methods (white, red, black, fermented), and ginsenoside profiles. Bioavailability and effects vary substantially by form. Scored conservatively until species and preparation are confirmed.

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|Botanical|Ginseng | 1585 (Number of products:200)

echinacea purpurea extract (4% phenolics.absorption: mixed (alkamides absorbed rapidly; cichoric acid NOT detected in plasma)
echinacea purpurea extract (4% phenolics.notes: Standardized E. purpurea extract. Alkamides (2,4-dienoic isobutylamides) are well-absorbed — sum Cmax 336 ng eq/mL, Tmax 2.3h (PMID:15919096, Matthias 2005). However, cichoric acid (the major caffeic acid derivative) was NOT detectable in any plasma sample at any time point. Clinically effective for immune support but bioavailability is compound-specific.
aliases: Echinacea purpurea aerial parts and root 4:1 extract
Echinacea (Echinacea purpurea) (aerial and root parts) 4:1 extract
Echinacea purpurea aerial and root parts 4:1 extract
echinacea purpurea aerial and root 4:1 extract
echinacea purpurea aerial parts and root extract

absorption_structured.notes: Alkamides well-absorbed; cichoric acid NOT detected in plasma (PMID:15919096)

echinacea angustifolia.notes: E. angustifolia root extract — higher alkamide content than E. purpurea root. Alkamides rapidly absorbed: dodeca-tetraenoic acid isobutylamides Cmax 10.88 ng/mL at Tmax 30 min (PMID:15901750, Woelkart 2005). Contains echinacoside (caffeic acid derivative) but these are NOT absorbed into systemic circulation. Different alkamide profile (2-monoene) vs E. purpurea (2,4-dienoic).

whole echinacea herb.notes: Dried whole echinacea herb (root, leaf, flower). Lower alkamide concentration than standardized extracts. Same absorption pattern: alkamides are bioavailable but caffeic acid derivatives (cichoric acid, echinacoside) are not absorbed systemically (PMID:15919096). Requires higher doses for equivalent alkamide intake.

echinacea (unspecified).notes: Form not specified on the label. Echinacea products span three species (purpurea, angustifolia, pallida), different plant parts (root, aerial, whole herb), and both standardized extracts and raw powders. Immune-active compounds (alkylamides, cichoric acid) vary significantly between species and preparations. Scored conservatively.

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|Botanical|Echinacea | 60 (Number of products:442)

turmeric extract (95% curcuminoids).notes: Standardized turmeric extract with ~95% curcuminoids. Despite high purity, native oral bioavailability is extremely poor — free (unconjugated) curcumin remains below 2 nM even at 2,400 mg doses (PMID:40487425). Most curcumin undergoes rapid glucuronidation/sulfation; the conjugated metabolites have limited bioactivity. The oft-cited '20-30% absorption' conflates GI dissolution with systemic bioavailability. Without an enhanced delivery system, most curcumin passes through unabsorbed (PMID:17999464).
aliases: meriva phytosome turmeric root extract curcuminoids
meriva phytosome turmeric root extract sunflower-based phosphatidyl choline
organic turmeric blend organic turmeric root extract
curcumin c3 complex turmeric (curcuma longa) extract
curcumin c3 complex turmeric (curcuma longa) extract curcuminoids
turmeric complex curcuma longa rhizome extract
turmeric root and rhizome extract curcuminoids
turmeric root extract (curcuma longa) blend curcuminoids

whole turmeric powder.notes: Whole dried turmeric root containing only 2-5% curcuminoids by weight. Oral bioavailability of curcumin from whole powder is well below 1% — free curcumin is essentially undetectable in plasma at typical supplemental doses (PMID:17999464). Contains turmerones and other non-curcuminoid compounds that may have independent activity, but curcumin delivery is subtherapeutic. Fine as a cooking spice but not a meaningful curcumin source.
aliases: organic turmeric blend organic fermented turmeric

liposomal curcumin.notes: Curcumin encapsulated in lipid vesicles. Despite marketing claims of '50x better' absorption, the only published human PK study of liposomal curcumin used intravenous administration (PMID:25500488, Greil 2014), NOT oral. No rigorous human oral pharmacokinetic data exists to validate absorption enhancement claims. Scored conservatively pending proper oral PK studies.

absorption_structured.notes: No oral human PK data; only IV study published (PMID:25500488)

curcumin with piperine.notes: Curcumin combined with piperine (BioPerine/black pepper extract). The landmark Shoba 1998 claim of 2000% (20x) bioavailability increase (PMID:9619120) has been directly contradicted by Verhoeven 2025 (PMID:40487425), which found piperine addition produced NO improvement in either free or total curcumin AUC in a controlled crossover study. The original study is now considered methodologically flawed. Piperine may still have independent anti-inflammatory value, but it does not meaningfully enhance curcumin absorption.

turmeric (unspecified).notes: Form not specified on the label. Turmeric/curcumin products range from raw powder (~3% curcuminoids, very poor absorption) to advanced delivery systems (liposomal, phytosome, piperine-enhanced) that can increase bioavailability 20–50x. Form choice dramatically affects clinical benefit. Scored conservatively.

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|Botanical|Turmeric | 48 (Number of products:2583)

aged garlic extract.notes: Garlic aged 18+ months producing stable S-allylcysteine (SAC) and S-allylmercaptocysteine (SAMC). SAC has exceptional oral bioavailability — 98.2% in rats, 103% in mice, 87.2% in dogs (PMID:8073085). No formal human absolute F study exists, but human plasma pharmacokinetics are consistent with very high absorption (half-life >10h in humans). Kyolic is the most-studied brand with strong cardiovascular evidence (cholesterol, blood pressure, arterial stiffness). Odorless and well-tolerated.

allicin (from fresh garlic).notes: Fresh garlic concentrates designed to release allicin upon crushing/ingestion. However, allicin has zero systemic bioavailability — it decomposes within seconds in blood into diallyl sulfides (DAS, DADS, DATS) and is never detectable in plasma (PMID:29937536). Lawson & Hunsaker 2018 developed a breath allyl methyl sulfide (AMS) surrogate assay showing 80-111% metabolite bioequivalence for non-enteric tablets, but this measures downstream metabolites, NOT allicin itself. The sulfide metabolites may be bioactive but are pharmacologically distinct from allicin.

absorption_structured.notes: Allicin decomposes instantly in blood; 0% systemic allicin (PMID:29937536)

garlic powder.notes: Dehydrated and ground garlic with variable retention of allicin-generating potential. Breath AMS bioequivalence ranges widely from 26-109% depending on formulation (PMID:29937536). Requires enteric coating to protect alliinase from stomach acid, and high-protein meals reduce metabolite appearance by 40-60%. Budget form commonly found in generic supplements. Note: allicin itself is never systemically available — these products deliver sulfide metabolites.

absorption_structured.notes: AMS bioequivalence 26-109% (PMID:29937536); actual systemic sulfide levels variable

black garlic extract.notes: Garlic aged at high temperature and humidity for 2-4 weeks (Maillard reaction). Rich in melanoidin antioxidants and S-allylcysteine (SAC), but the heat-aging process destroys nearly all allicin-generating capacity — breath AMS bioequivalence is only 5% vs raw garlic (PMID:29937536). Has antioxidant and cardiovascular evidence through SAC and melanoidin pathways, but the sulfur compound bioavailability profile is fundamentally different from fresh garlic or aged garlic extract.
aliases: aged fermented black garlic (allium sativum) extract
black garlic bulb extract, aged, fermented
black garlic bulb extract aged fermented

absorption_structured.notes: 5% allicin bioequivalence vs raw garlic (PMID:29937536); SAC present but at lower levels than AGE

garlic (unspecified).notes: Form not specified on the label. Garlic supplements vary dramatically: aged garlic extract (water-soluble SAC), fresh garlic concentrates (allicin-releasing), dehydrated powders, and black garlic (fermented). Each has a different active compound profile and absorption pattern. Scored conservatively.

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|Botanical|Garlic | 4 (Number of products:1363)

cranberry extract (25% proanthocyanidins.absorption: poor (<1% intact PACs; ~6% as urinary metabolites)
cranberry extract (25% proanthocyanidins.notes: Standardized to 25% A-type proanthocyanidins (PACs). Polymeric PACs are too large to cross intestinal epithelium and have <1% systemic bioavailability as intact molecules. However, colonic microbiota degrade PACs into phenolic acids (hippuric acid, phenylacetic acid, valerolactones) which ARE absorbed — 6.2% urinary recovery of total phenolic metabolites (PMID:25172705). UTI prevention likely works through these urinary metabolites rather than intact PAC anti-adhesion (PMID:12058985). 36 mg PACs/day is the established effective dose. A-type PACs are unique to cranberry.

whole cranberry powder.notes: Ground whole cranberry fruit with lower, non-standardized PAC content. Intact proanthocyanidins are not systemically absorbed (<1%). Contains fiber, organic acids, and non-PAC polyphenols. Insoluble PACs from whole-berry products produce lower urinary anti-adhesion activity than soluble PACs from juice/extract products (PMID:33818241). Requires higher doses for equivalent metabolite generation.

absorption_structured.notes: Intact PACs not absorbed; lower metabolite yield than extracts (PMID:33818241)

cranberry juice concentrate.notes: Concentrated cranberry juice with variable PAC retention. Like all cranberry products, intact PACs are not systemically absorbed. Soluble PACs from juice preparations show higher urinary anti-adhesion activity than insoluble PACs from whole berry (PMID:33818241), suggesting juice concentrate may deliver more UTI-relevant metabolites despite lower total PAC content. Sugar content may be counterproductive for some users.

absorption_structured.notes: PACs not absorbed intact; soluble PACs generate more urinary metabolites (PMID:33818241)

cranberry (unspecified).notes: Form not specified on the label. Cranberry products range from standardized PAC extracts (proanthocyanidins) to juice concentrates and whole fruit powders. PAC type (A-type vs B-type) and concentration determine anti-adhesion activity against urinary tract bacteria. Scored conservatively.

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|Botanical|cranberry | 47 (Number of products:1736)

bilberry extract (25% anthocyanins).absorption: poor (~1-2% intact anthocyanins; ~12% total as metabolites)
bilberry extract (25% anthocyanins).notes: Standardized to 25% anthocyanosides (delphinidin and cyanidin glycosides). Intact anthocyanin bioavailability is only 0.93-1.82% in rat PK studies (PMID:16939312). The gold-standard 13C-labeled human tracer study (PMID:23604435) found 12.4% total recovery — but 98% of that was metabolites (hippuric acid, ferulic acid, protocatechuic acid), not intact anthocyanins. Intact C3G half-life is only 0.4 hours. MirtoSelect is the leading branded extract and may have matrix-enhanced absorption (PMID:27338070, rat data). Retinal and cardiovascular benefits may operate through phenolic metabolites.

whole bilberry powder.notes: Ground whole bilberry fruit with lower, non-standardized anthocyanin content. Intact anthocyanins have <2% systemic bioavailability and a plasma half-life of only 24 minutes (PMID:23604435). Colonic microbiota are essential for generating absorbable metabolites — subjects with intact colon show higher metabolite levels than ileostomists (PMID:28450007). Provides broader nutritional value but requires higher doses.

absorption_structured.notes: Intact anthocyanins <2% F; requires colonic metabolism (PMID:23604435, 28450007)

bilberry juice concentrate.notes: Concentrated bilberry juice with variable anthocyanin retention. Heat and light during processing degrade anthocyanins. Intact anthocyanin bioavailability is <2% with extremely short plasma half-life (PMID:23604435). Retains the full polyphenol spectrum which generates diverse metabolites via colonic microbiota. Less standardized than extracts.

bilberry (unspecified).notes: Form not specified on the label. Bilberry (Vaccinium myrtillus) products range from standardized 25% anthocyanoside extracts to whole fruit powders and juice concentrates. Anthocyanin content and bioavailability vary significantly between forms. Scored conservatively until form is confirmed.

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|botanical|Dwarf Bilberry | 3184 (Number of products:6)

elderberry extract (sambucol).notes: Standardized elderberry (Sambucus nigra) extract rich in anthocyanins (cyanidin-3-glucoside and cyanidin-3-sambubioside). Sambucol is the most clinically studied brand. Anthocyanin oral bioavailability is very low — only 0.3-0.4% recovered in urine (Milbury 2002, PMID:18040528). Like all anthocyanins, intact compounds are poorly absorbed (<5%) but colonic metabolites (protocatechuic acid, phenolic acids) extend biological activity to ~12-20% total recovery. Despite low systemic absorption, strong clinical evidence for reducing cold/flu duration by 2-4 days via local GI immunomodulation and metabolite activity.
aliases: sambucus black elderberry fruit extract supplement

absorption_structured.notes: Intact anthocyanins <5% absorbed; total metabolites ~12-20% (PMID:18040528; PMC7023094)

whole elderberry powder.notes: Ground whole elderberry fruit with lower, non-standardized anthocyanin content. Intact anthocyanins have <5% systemic bioavailability — similar to bilberry and other berry anthocyanins. Contains fiber, vitamins, and natural sugars. Colonic microbiota generate absorbable phenolic acid metabolites but at lower yields than concentrated extracts. Requires higher doses than standardized extracts for equivalent metabolite generation.
