absorption_structured.notes: Intact anthocyanins <3% absorbed; lower metabolite yield than extracts

elderberry syrup.notes: Traditional elderberry preparation (liquid syrup or concentrate). Popular for children and adults who prefer liquid forms. Anthocyanin bioavailability is <5% as intact compounds, with variable metabolite generation depending on preparation method and sugar content. Heat processing during syrup preparation may degrade anthocyanins. High sugar content (honey or sugar-based) adds calories but may improve palatability and compliance.

absorption_structured.notes: Intact anthocyanins <3%; heat processing may reduce further

elderberry (unspecified).notes: Form not specified on the label. Elderberry (Sambucus nigra/canadensis) products range from standardized anthocyanin extracts (Sambucol, ElderCraft) to syrups, powders, and juice concentrates. Anthocyanin content and immune-stimulating potency vary substantially between forms. Scored conservatively.

plant-based enzyme complex.absorption: N/A (enzymes act locally in GI tract)
plant-based enzyme complex.notes: Broad-spectrum blend of enzymes sourced from fungal (Aspergillus) or botanical sources. Includes amylase, protease, lipase, cellulase, and often lactase. Fungal-derived enzymes are generally more acid-stable than animal-derived pancreatic enzymes, allowing activity across a wider pH range in the GI tract. DigeZyme (penta-enzyme complex) showed significant improvement in gastric/intestinal symptoms over 60 days in a clinical study. OTC supplements lack FDA regulation of enzyme potency and concentrations.

absorption_structured.notes: Represents GI enzyme activity survival; fungal enzymes more acid-stable than animal-derived

pancreatic enzymes (animal-derived).notes: Enzymes sourced from animal (typically porcine) pancreases, containing amylase, lipase, and protease. Requires enteric coating to survive stomach acid and function in the small intestine. Prescription pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy (PERT) is FDA-regulated for exocrine pancreatic insufficiency; OTC forms have unregulated potency. Pancreatin 4x/8x/10x refers to concentration multiples of USP activity units. Well-established efficacy in pancreatic insufficiency but limited evidence for use in healthy individuals.

specific enzymes.absorption: N/A (enzymes act locally in GI tract, not absorbed systemically)
specific enzymes.notes: Targeted individual enzymes (protease, lipase, amylase, bromelain, papain, serrapeptase, lactase, cellulase, etc.) designed to break down specific food components in the GI lumen. Enzymes work locally — systemic absorption is NOT the relevant metric; GI stability and enzyme activity delivery matter. Without enteric coating, many enzymes are partially denatured by stomach acid (~30-50% survival). OTC enzyme supplements are not FDA-regulated and enzyme concentrations are not guaranteed (Johns Hopkins Medicine). Well-characterized branded forms (DigeZyme, ProHydrolase) have more reliable potency.

absorption_structured.notes: Represents GI enzyme activity survival, NOT systemic absorption; stomach acid denatures unprotected enzymes

enteric-coated enzymes.notes: Encapsulation technology that protects enzymes from stomach acid, ensuring they are released in the small intestine where they are most effective. Essential for pancreatic enzymes.

oxidized enzymes.notes: Enzymes that have been degraded by exposure to heat, light, or improper storage, rendering them inactive and ineffective. [FLAG: Review for removal due to low efficacy]

digestive enzymes (unspecified).notes: Form not specified; conservative scoring applied. Digestive enzymes work locally in the GI tract and are not absorbed systemically. Enzyme activity, stability, and spectrum vary widely between products. OTC supplements lack FDA regulation of potency. Consider using a specific form for better characterization.

digestive_enzymes.rxcui_note: No RxNorm concept for supplement ingredient

nattokinase standard.absorption: low but functionally significant (enzyme detectable in serum 2-24h post-dose)
nattokinase standard.notes: Fibrinolytic serine protease (27.7 kDa) derived from natto (fermented soybeans). The only human PK study (Ero 2013, PMID:23709455) detected nattokinase in serum via ELISA at 2-24h after a single 2000 FU dose (peak ~13.3h). A single 2000 FU dose significantly elevated D-dimer at 6-8h and fibrin degradation products at 4h (Kurosawa 2015, PMC4479826). Despite functional evidence of systemic fibrinolytic activity, a 27.7 kDa protein is generally too large for efficient oral absorption — actual bioavailability percentage is unknown. NSK-SD is the vitamin K2-free standardized form. Active research into nano-delivery systems to improve GI survival.

absorption_structured.notes: 27.7 kDa protein; detected in serum via ELISA but actual F% unknown (PMID:23709455)

nattokinase nsk-sd.notes: NSK-SD (Nattokinase Standardized by JBSL) — purified, vitamin K2-free standardized nattokinase. Same 27.7 kDa serine protease as standard nattokinase but with controlled purity and removal of vitamin K2 (important for patients on warfarin). Standardized to fibrinolytic activity units (FU). Same GI survival challenges as standard nattokinase. Most clinical trials used NSK-SD or equivalent standardized forms. Fibrinolytic activity 6x more efficient than plasmin for cross-linked fibrin cleavage (PMC4479826).

absorption_structured.notes: Standardized purity but same large-protein absorption challenges; ELISA-detectable in serum

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|Enzyme|Nattokinase | 1232 (Number of products:169)

squalene standard.notes: Triterpene lipid (C30H50) found in shark liver oil (~96% squalene), olive oil, and amaranth. Oral absorption is well-characterized at 60-85% in humans, via lymphatic chylomicron transport — same pathway as dietary cholesterol (Tilvis & Miettinen 1983, PMID:6855483). About 20% of absorbed squalene is cyclized to sterols during intestinal transit. Supports skin health (precursor to skin lipids), immune function (vaccine adjuvant research), and cholesterol metabolism. No evidence that shark-derived squalene is more bioavailable than plant-derived — same molecule, different source concentrations.

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|Chemical|Squalene | 1853 (Number of products:19)

d-limonene standard.notes: Monoterpene (MW 136 Da) from citrus peel oil. Rapidly absorbed after oral administration — perillic acid (primary metabolite) peaks at ~1 hour with t1/2 of 0.82-1.84h (Vigushin 1998). Miller 2010 (PMID:20661827) showed adipose tissue accumulation after 4 weeks of 500-600 mg/day, confirming systemic distribution. Extensively metabolized via CYP enzymes to perillic acid, dihydroperillic acid, and limonene diols. Supports digestive health (heartburn relief) and has antioxidant/potential anticancer properties. Exact oral bioavailability percentage not formally measured but functional absorption is clearly efficient.

absorption_structured.notes: Rapidly absorbed; tissue accumulation confirmed (PMID:20661827); exact F% not formally measured

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|Chemical|Limonene | 1215 (Number of products:28)

nicotinamide riboside (NR).absorption: moderate-good (effectively doubles NAD+; most converted to NA by gut microbiota)
nicotinamide riboside (NR).notes: Patented NAD+ precursor (Niagen/TruNiagen is the leading brand). Oral NR effectively doubles circulating NAD+ at 300-1000 mg/day (PMID:27720903, Trammell 2016). However, most oral NR undergoes gut microbiota-mediated deamidation and conversion to nicotinic acid (NA), then raises NAD+ via the Preiss-Handler pathway — NOT direct NR absorption (Science Advances 2024, Kim et al.). Absolute oral bioavailability of intact NR has never been formally measured; high inter-individual variability (Airhart 2017, PMC5718430). Hydrophilic molecule with low passive permeability.

absorption_structured.notes: Most NR converted to NA by gut microbiota; NAD+ raised via Preiss-Handler pathway (PMID:27720903; Science Advances 2024)

nicotinamide mononucleotide (NMN).absorption: moderate (extensive first-pass metabolism; most converted to NA)
nicotinamide mononucleotide (NMN).notes: Direct NAD+ precursor that effectively raises NAD+ levels when taken orally at 250-500 mg/day (PMC9036060, Irie 2020). However, extensive first-pass hepatic metabolism converts nearly all ingested NMN to nicotinamide (NAM) in the liver (PMC11205942). Most oral NMN undergoes gut microbiota-mediated deamidation to nicotinic acid (NA) before contributing to NAD+ synthesis via Preiss-Handler pathway (Science Advances 2024, Kim et al.). Emerging anti-aging molecule but oral PK is less favorable than once believed.

absorption_structured.notes: Extensive first-pass metabolism; most converted to NA by gut bacteria (PMC11205942; Science Advances 2024)

liposomal nmn / nr.notes: NMN or NR encapsulated in liposomes to bypass gut microbiota degradation and first-pass metabolism via lymphatic absorption. A Feb 2025 RCT (n=15, healthy men) found liposomal NMN at 350 mg/day significantly increased NAD+ vs non-liposomal NMN over 4 weeks — the first human head-to-head comparison. Mechanistic rationale is sound (liposomes bypass enterohepatic degradation) but evidence base is still limited to one small trial. Stability concerns: liposomes can leak during storage, losing the bioavailability advantage.

absorption_structured.notes: One human RCT (Feb 2025, n=15) shows significant NAD+ increase vs non-liposomal; larger trials needed

niacinamide (nicotinamide).notes: Non-flushing form of vitamin B3. Industrially synthesized (not extracted from food sources in supplement form). NAD+ precursor but conversion efficiency is lower than NR and NMN — niacinamide enters the salvage pathway rather than the more efficient Preiss-Handler route. Well-absorbed orally (~60-70%) with established safety at doses up to 3g/day. Does not cause the vasodilatory flush associated with nicotinic acid.

niacin (nicotinic acid).notes: Flushing form of vitamin B3 (nicotinic acid). Industrially synthesized in supplement form. Converts to NAD+ via the Preiss-Handler pathway. Well-absorbed orally (~60-70%) but causes prostaglandin-mediated vasodilatory flush at >50 mg doses. At pharmacological doses (1-3g/day), effectively lowers LDL and raises HDL (PMID:12873710). The flush side effect limits tolerability for many users.

oxidized nad+ precursor.notes: A degraded or inactive NAD+ precursor due to improper storage, heat, or age. It provides no biological activity and indicates a poor-quality product. [FLAG: Low Efficacy]

nad+ precursors (unspecified).notes: Form not specified; conservative scoring applied. Consider using a specific form for better bioavailability.

nad_precursors.rxcui_note: No RxNorm concept for supplement ingredient
nad_precursors.cui_note: Class entry spanning multiple NAD+ precursor compounds (NR, NMN, niacin). No single UMLS concept.

lycopene extract.absorption: low (~24% average from purified dose; highly variable)
lycopene extract.notes: Carotenoid antioxidant standardized from tomato oleoresin. Accelerator mass spectrometry (AMS) study found average ~24% absorption from purified lycopene dose in humans (Ross 2011, PMID:21543537). Absorption is highly fat-dependent (requires 10-15g dietary fat), matrix-dependent (processed > raw tomato), and subject to large inter-individual variability due to genetic polymorphisms in absorption transporters (PMID:25772008). LycoRed/Lyc-O-Mato is the leading standardized brand. Prostate, cardiovascular, and skin health benefits supported by observational and interventional studies.

absorption_structured.notes: ~24% average from AMS study (PMID:21543537); requires fat co-ingestion; high inter-individual variability

synthetic lycopene.absorption: low (~20-30% with fat; possibly higher AUC than tomato-derived)
synthetic lycopene.notes: Synthetic lycopene (e.g., Lycovit by BASF). Same molecule as natural lycopene. Deuterium-labeled study (PMID:15808327) showed ~3x higher AUC than tomato-derived lycopene, possibly due to formulation differences rather than intrinsic molecular superiority. Hoppe 2003 found no significant difference in serum lycopene response between synthetic and natural forms at 15 mg/day for 28 days. Less commonly used in supplements than natural tomato-derived forms.

absorption_structured.notes: ~24% average; deuterium study showed possibly higher AUC vs tomato (PMID:15808327)

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|Botanical|Lycopene | 164 (Number of products:1777)

paba powder.notes: Para-aminobenzoic acid (MW 137 Da), a B-vitamin-like compound. Demonstrates excellent intestinal absorption — effective permeability coefficient indicates ~100% absorption at the intestinal epithelium (J Pharm Sci 2017). So reliably absorbed it is used as a dietary intake completeness marker in nutritional research (the PABA check method). Rapidly N-acetylated by NAT1 in intestinal epithelial cells during absorption. Urinary recovery ~67% within 24h. Traditionally used for skin health, UV protection, and hair pigmentation support. May interfere with sulfonamide antibiotics.

paba (para-aminobenzoic acid) (unspecifi.notes: Form not specified; conservative scoring applied. Consider using a specific form for better bioavailability.

paba.cui_note: Corrected from multivitamin product concept to para-aminobenzoic acid ingredient concept.

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|Chemical|PABA | 97 (Number of products:1491)

l-leucine powder.notes: Essential branched-chain amino acid (BCAA) and the primary trigger for mTOR-mediated muscle protein synthesis. BCAAs are unique in largely bypassing first-pass hepatic metabolism, appearing directly in systemic circulation (PMC5429325). Free-form amino acids are absorbed more rapidly than intact protein with greater postprandial plasma availability (Churchward-Venne 2021, PMID:34642762). Renal reabsorption is 95-99%. The ~90-95% absorption estimate is based on converging evidence (hepatic bypass, rapid absorption, high renal conservation) but no single PK study has formally measured absolute oral bioavailability.

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|amino acid|Leucine | 2315 (Number of products:2347)

l-valine powder.notes: Essential BCAA important for muscle metabolism and energy production during exercise. Like leucine and isoleucine, valine largely bypasses first-pass hepatic metabolism and appears directly in systemic circulation (PMC5429325). Free-form amino acids have greater postprandial plasma availability than intact protein (PMID:34642762). Renal reabsorption is 95-99%. Plays a role in glycogen synthesis and blood sugar regulation. The ~90-95% absorption is an estimate based on converging amino acid PK evidence.

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|Amino Acid|Branched Chain Amino Acids | 173 (Number of products:424)

l-isoleucine powder.notes: Essential BCAA important for muscle recovery, glucose regulation, and hemoglobin synthesis during exercise. Like leucine and valine, isoleucine largely bypasses first-pass hepatic metabolism (PMC5429325). Free-form amino acids are absorbed more rapidly than intact protein (PMID:34642762). Renal reabsorption is 95-99%. Also plays a role in immune function and fatty acid metabolism. The ~90-95% absorption is an estimate based on converging amino acid PK evidence.

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|amino acid|Isoleucine | 2415 (Number of products:1910)

l-threonine powder.absorption: good (~80-90%; significant first-pass gut mucosal utilization)
l-threonine powder.notes: Essential amino acid important for protein synthesis, immune function, and collagen production. Absorbed via active carrier-mediated transport in the small intestine. Oral L-threonine at 6-12 g/day produces dose-dependent increases in plasma threonine and metabolite L-2-aminobutyrate (PMC12106564, 2025 RCT). Notable: the gut mucosa consumes a significant fraction of ingested threonine for mucin glycoprotein synthesis before it reaches systemic circulation, which may lower effective systemic bioavailability relative to other amino acids. The ~85% estimate accounts for this first-pass intestinal utilization.

absorption_structured.notes: Good absorption but significant gut mucosal utilization for mucin synthesis reduces systemic F

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|Amino Acid|L-Threonine | 167 (Number of products:18)

l-citrulline malate.notes: Citrulline bound to malic acid (2:1 ratio typical). Near-complete intestinal absorption; citrulline bypasses hepatic first-pass metabolism unlike L-arginine. Malic acid contributes to the Krebs cycle but does not alter citrulline PK vs free-form (PMC8571142). At 6-8 g/day (providing 3-4 g citrulline) supports NO production, exercise performance, and blood flow. Schwedhelm et al. (PMID 17662090) confirmed oral citrulline raises plasma arginine more effectively than oral arginine.

absorption_structured.notes: Near-complete intestinal absorption; no hepatic first-pass for citrulline

l-citrulline powder.notes: Free-form L-citrulline with near-complete intestinal absorption. Uniquely bypasses hepatic first-pass metabolism unlike L-arginine (only ~20% F). Converts to arginine in kidneys, raising plasma arginine more effectively than arginine itself (Schwedhelm et al., PMID 17662090). At 3-6 g/day supports nitric oxide production and exercise performance. PK data shows no significant difference from citrulline malate for citrulline delivery (PMC8571142).

absorption_structured.notes: Near-complete intestinal absorption; bypasses hepatic first-pass unlike arginine

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|amino acid|Citrulline | 2792 (Number of products:612)

dmae bitartrate.absorption: moderate (~30-60% estimated; limited human PK data)
dmae bitartrate.notes: Small water-soluble amine (MW ~89) marketed for cognitive function and skin health. Human PK data is essentially nonexistent. Rat ADME studies (PMID 31100288, Hyland & Bhatt 2019) show rapid absorption but significant hepatic first-pass metabolism: urinary recovery 16-69%, CO2 exhalation 3-22%. Brain concentrations were low with no accumulation despite crossing BBB. May support acetylcholine production but clinical evidence is limited. The 'Riker' deanol studies from 1970s showed modest cognitive benefits.

absorption_structured.notes: Estimated from rodent ADME; no published human PK data; significant first-pass metabolism

dhea supplement.absorption: very poor (~3% parent compound; extensive first-pass sulfation to DHEA-S)
dhea supplement.notes: Prohormone precursor (dehydroepiandrosterone). Oral bioavailability of parent DHEA is only ~3.1% in primates (Leblanc et al. 2003, PMID 12970301) due to massive first-pass hepatic sulfation to DHEA-S. However, DHEA-S has >20h half-life and serves as circulating reservoir that tissues can back-hydrolyze to free DHEA. At 25-50 mg/day raises serum DHEA-S to youthful levels in postmenopausal women. Use under medical supervision due to sex hormone conversion (testosterone, estradiol). 7-keto-DHEA is pharmacologically distinct (no sex hormone conversion).

absorption_structured.notes: Only ~3% parent F (PMID 12970301); extensive sulfation to DHEA-S reservoir provides prolonged biological exposure

dhea (dehydroepiandrosterone) (unspecifi.notes: Form not specified; conservative scoring applied. Consider using a specific form for better bioavailability.

sod supplement.absorption: very poor (native SOD ~0% oral F; GliSODin gliadin-coated form has indirect efficacy)
sod supplement.notes: Antioxidant metalloenzyme (~32 kDa). Native unprotected SOD is completely degraded by gastric acid and proteases (0% oral bioavailability). GliSODin (melon SOD + wheat gliadin biopolymer coating, Isocell) preserves enzyme activity through GI transit and shows indirect efficacy — likely induces endogenous SOD expression rather than being absorbed intact. Over 30 publications on GliSODin show antioxidant marker improvements. Score reflects mixed product category (native SOD worthless, GliSODin has evidence).

extramel superoxide dismutase french melon concentrate

absorption_structured.notes: Native SOD: 0% oral F (protein degradation). GliSODin: indirect mechanism via endogenous SOD induction

synephrine hcl.absorption: moderate (~20-30% estimated oral bioavailability based on phenylethylamine class)
synephrine hcl.notes: p-Synephrine is a protoalkaloid naturally occurring in Citrus aurantium (bitter orange) peel. The hydrochloride salt is the standard supplemental form. Acts as a beta-3 adrenergic agonist; used for thermogenesis, energy, and weight management. Unlike ephedrine, p-synephrine has minimal alpha-1/2 or beta-1/2 activity, resulting in a more favorable cardiovascular safety profile. Stohs et al. (2012, PMID 22176753) review of 30 studies: no significant adverse cardiovascular effects at standard doses (10-50 mg/day). Not to be confused with m-synephrine (phenylephrine) which has different pharmacology.

absorption_structured.notes: Estimated based on phenylethylamine class pharmacokinetics

synephrine (unspecified).notes: Form not specified; conservative scoring applied.

same supplement.absorption: very poor (~2-5% oral F; extensive first-pass metabolism)
same supplement.notes: Key methyl donor involved in >100 methylation reactions (mood, liver, joints, DNA repair). Oral bioavailability is only 2.6% (men) / 2.1% (women) per IV/oral AUC ratio (Yang et al. 2009, PMID 19302903). Enteric coating is essential to prevent acid degradation but only marginally improves F. Food reduces AUC by 55%. Despite poor PK, clinically effective for depression (400-1600 mg/day) and osteoarthritis, suggesting hepatic/gut effects or sufficient systemic exposure from high doses. Tosylate disulfate salt is most stable form.

absorption_structured.notes: Only ~2-5% oral F (PMID 19302903); enteric coating essential but doesn't fix poor permeability

same (s-adenosyl-l-methionine) (unspecif.notes: Form not specified; conservative scoring applied. Consider using a specific form for better bioavailability.

nucleic acid complex.notes: Oral RNA and DNA are completely degraded by pancreatic nucleases and phosphodiesterases to individual nucleosides and bases, which are absorbed. However, the body synthesizes nucleotides de novo, so exogenous nucleic acids provide no unique molecular entity. Limited evidence of clinical benefit from standalone RNA/DNA supplements. Only positive data is for RNA combined with L-arginine and EPA in immunonutrition formulas for surgical patients (multicomponent intervention, not proof of RNA bioavailability). Typically yeast-derived.

rna/dna (nucleic acids) (unspecified).notes: Form not specified; conservative scoring applied. Consider using a specific form for better bioavailability.

octacosanol supplement.absorption: poor (~5-12% in rats; long-chain fatty alcohol with poor solubility)
octacosanol supplement.notes: Long-chain fatty alcohol (C28) from wheat germ and sugar cane wax. Rat PK studies show only 5-12% oral bioavailability with 1-2 hour half-life. Highly hydrophobic with poor water solubility limits intestinal absorption; mostly excreted in feces unabsorbed. Accumulates primarily in liver and adipose tissue. May support cardiovascular health and exercise performance via cholesterol metabolism modulation, though human evidence is limited. Often found in policosanol mixtures.

absorption_structured.notes: Rat PK: only 5-12% oral F; poor water solubility limits intestinal absorption

sugar cane policosanol.absorption: poor (~5-12%; limited by long-chain alcohol solubility)
sugar cane policosanol.notes: Mixture of long-chain fatty alcohols from sugar cane wax, primarily octacosanol (~67%). Oral bioavailability limited to 5-12% due to poor aqueous solubility. CRITICAL: virtually all positive lipid-lowering data came from a single Cuban research group holding the patent. Of 89 independent studies (>57,000 participants), none found policosanol more effective than placebo for cholesterol (Examine.com review). Cuban trial 100% success rate vs ~14% outside Cuba is a major reproducibility red flag.

absorption_structured.notes: 5-12% oral F; same as octacosanol (primary constituent). Cuban trial data not independently reproduced.

rice bran policosanol.absorption: poor (~5-10%; less studied than sugar cane variety)
rice bran policosanol.notes: Policosanol derived from rice bran wax. Less studied than Cuban sugar cane variety. Same long-chain alcohol absorption limitations (~5-10% oral F). Independent clinical trials have not confirmed cholesterol-lowering benefits for any policosanol source. May contain different fatty alcohol ratios than sugar cane policosanol.

absorption_structured.notes: Poor (~5-10%); even less studied than sugar cane variety

eriocitrin extract.absorption: very poor (<1% parent compound; metabolized by gut microbiota)
eriocitrin extract.notes: Flavanone glycoside (eriodictyol 7-rutinoside) found in citrus fruits, particularly lemon. Rat PK shows <1% parent bioavailability (Ferreira et al. 2021, PMID 33533607). Like most flavanone glycosides, poorly absorbed intact in small intestine. Colonic microbiota cleave the rutinoside sugar releasing eriodictyol aglycone, which is conjugated (glucuronidated/sulfated) and partially absorbed. Antioxidant and cardiovascular benefits may occur via metabolite activity rather than parent compound.

absorption_structured.notes: <1% parent F (PMID 33533607); colonic microbiota-mediated metabolism to eriodictyol metabolites

gamma-linolenic acid (unspecified).notes: GLA (C18:3 n-6) from borage/evening primrose oil. Elongated to DGLA which competitively inhibits arachidonic acid conversion to pro-inflammatory eicosanoids. RA RCTs at 1.4-2.8 g/day (PMID 2181884, PMID 8177983) show anti-inflammatory efficacy. Requires dietary fat for optimal absorption.

beta-cryptoxanthin.notes: Provitamin A carotenoid (xanthophyll) with the highest apparent bioavailability among common carotenoids — blood concentrations 686-725% higher than beta-carotene at comparable intakes (Burri et al. 2010, PMID 20807466). SR-B1 transporter preferentially absorbs xanthophylls over carotenes. Found in orange/red fruits (citrus, papaya, peppers). Still limited by lipophilic micellar/chylomicron absorption pathway. Requires dietary fat for absorption (PMC4892306).

27-deoxyactein (unspecified).notes: Triterpene glycoside from black cohosh (Cimicifuga racemosa) root/rhizome. One of the key standardization markers alongside actein for menopausal support extracts like Remifemin. May modulate serotonin and opioid receptors. 4-hydroxyisoleucine (unspecified).notes: Unique non-proteinogenic amino acid found almost exclusively in fenugreek seeds (Trigonella foenum-graecum). Stimulates glucose-dependent insulin secretion from pancreatic beta cells — only active when blood glucose is elevated, reducing hypoglycemia risk. Key bioactive behind fenugreek's blood sugar benefits. Also found in Testofen and other standardized fenugreek extracts.

akba (unspecified).notes: The most pharmacologically active boswellic acid from Boswellia serrata resin (frankincense). Potent and selective 5-lipoxygenase (5-LOX) inhibitor with IC50 ~1.5 μM. Anti-inflammatory without the GI side effects of NSAIDs. 5-Loxin (PLT Health) is standardized to 30% AKBA; Aflapin uses a different enrichment. Listed on labels as a quantitative marker for boswellia extract potency.

cape (unspecified).notes: Caffeic acid phenethyl ester — the primary bioactive compound in bee propolis responsible for its anti-inflammatory and immunomodulatory effects. Potent inhibitor of NF-κB signaling pathway. Research shows anti-cancer, neuroprotective, and wound healing properties in preclinical studies. Listed on propolis supplement labels as a potency marker. Brazilian green propolis typically has highest CAPE content.

cgf (unspecified).notes: Chlorella Growth Factor — a concentrated nucleotide-peptide complex extracted from the nucleus of Chlorella vulgaris during its rapid growth phase. Contains RNA, DNA, amino acids, vitamins, and minerals. Claimed to support cellular repair and immune function, though clinical evidence is limited. dnj (1-deoxynojirimycin) (unspecified).notes: 1-Deoxynojirimycin (DNJ) — a polyhydroxylated piperidine alkaloid (iminosugar) found primarily in white mulberry (Morus alba) leaves and silkworm. Potent alpha-glucosidase inhibitor that slows carbohydrate digestion and postprandial glucose spikes. Same mechanism as the diabetes drug acarbose (Precose). Japanese research shows ~6% oral bioavailability in rats. Listed on mulberry leaf supplement labels as the key active compound.

egcg.description: Primary catechin in green tea (Camellia sinensis); most studied tea polyphenol for metabolism and antioxidant activity

egcg (unspecified).notes: Epigallocatechin-3-gallate — the most abundant and pharmacologically active catechin in green tea (Camellia sinensis). Accounts for 50-80% of total catechins. Oral bioavailability is low (~2-5%) due to instability at intestinal pH and extensive Phase II conjugation. Multiple RCTs support metabolic benefits (thermogenesis, fat oxidation) and antioxidant activity at 300-800 mg/day. Listed on green tea extract labels as the primary standardization marker.

absorption_structured.notes: 2-5% fasted oral F; food reduces AUC 2.7-3.9x (PMID 32438698); strong inter-subject variability >6-fold in Cmax

l_dopa.description: Dopamine precursor amino acid from Mucuna pruriens (velvet bean); used for mood and neurological support

l-dopa (unspecified).notes: L-3,4-dihydroxyphenylalanine (levodopa) — the direct metabolic precursor to dopamine. Found naturally in Mucuna pruriens seeds (15-20% L-DOPA content). Crosses the blood-brain barrier where it is decarboxylated to dopamine by DOPA decarboxylase. Prescription levodopa is the gold-standard Parkinson's treatment. Supplement-grade L-DOPA from mucuna supports mood, motivation, and reproductive health. Listed on mucuna labels as the key active marker.

opc (unspecified).notes: Oligomeric proanthocyanidins — dimers and trimers of flavan-3-ol units (catechin/epicatechin). Found in grape seed (most common supplement source), pine bark (Pycnogenol), and cranberry. 50-100x stronger antioxidant than vitamins C and E in vitro. Support capillary integrity, venous tone, and skin collagen. Masquelier's OPCs refer to the original French research lineage. Oral bioavailability moderate (~10-30%) for low-MW oligomers.

pac.description: A-type proanthocyanidins from cranberry; anti-adhesion activity against urinary tract pathogens

pac (unspecified).notes: Type-A proanthocyanidins (PACs) — the key bioactive in cranberry responsible for urinary tract health. Unlike common B-type PACs in grape seed, cranberry A-type PACs have a unique double interflavan linkage that prevents E. coli from adhering to bladder epithelium. 36 mg PACs/day is the threshold dose supported by clinical evidence for UTI prevention. Listed on cranberry supplement labels as the primary quality marker.

rb1 (unspecified).notes: Ginsenoside Rb1 — one of the most abundant protopanaxadiol (PPD) type ginsenosides in Panax ginseng root. Research shows neuroprotective, anti-inflammatory, and cardioprotective properties. Metabolized by gut bacteria to compound K (the active metabolite). Very low oral bioavailability (~1-4%) due to large molecular size and poor membrane permeability. Listed on ginseng supplement labels as a standardization marker alongside Rg1.

rg1 (unspecified).notes: Ginsenoside Rg1 — the most abundant protopanaxatriol (PPT) type ginsenoside in Panax ginseng root. Associated with stimulatory and cognitive-enhancing effects of ginseng (versus Rb1's calming properties). Supports neurogenesis, angiogenesis, and immune modulation. Oral bioavailability ~2-6% — better than PPD ginsenosides. Used alongside Rb1 as the primary standardization marker for ginseng extract potency.

Research focuses on anti-tumor, anti-fatigue, and neuroprotective properties. S-form (20S-Rg3) has higher bioactivity than R-form. Shenyi capsule (China) is a Rg3-based pharmaceutical. s_allylcysteine.description: Water-soluble organosulfur compound from aged garlic extract; cardiovascular and antioxidant support

s-allylcysteine (unspecified).notes: S-Allylcysteine (SAC) — the most studied organosulfur compound in aged garlic extract (AGE). Formed during the aging process as gamma-glutamyl-S-allylcysteine is hydrolyzed. Unlike allicin (which is unstable), SAC is water-soluble, stable, and has excellent oral bioavailability (~98% in rats). Cardioprotective via antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and lipid-lowering mechanisms. Kyolic aged garlic extract is standardized to SAC content. Key marker distinguishing aged garlic from fresh garlic supplements.

Research shows neuroprotective, anti-aging, and lipid-lowering properties. IMPORTANT: Fo-Ti/He Shou Wu has documented hepatotoxicity risk — the processed (zhi) form is considered safer than raw. TSG is the standardization marker for extract potency. Oral bioavailability is low (~5-8%) in rat studies.

acemannan (unspecified).notes: Acemannan — a long-chain acetylated polymannose polysaccharide from Aloe barbadensis (aloe vera) inner leaf gel. The primary bioactive responsible for aloe's immune-stimulating properties. Activates macrophages via mannose receptors, stimulates cytokine release, and supports wound healing. FDA-approved veterinary product (Acemannan Immunostimulant) for fibrosarcoma in cats/dogs. Not absorbed systemically — acts via gut-associated immune tissue. Manapol is a branded high-MW acemannan.

In cordyceps products, benefits come from polysaccharide beta-glucans and cordycepin rather than absorbed free adenosine.

alkaloids.description: Broad class of nitrogen-containing plant compounds. When listed generically on labels, refers to total alkaloid fraction of an herbal extract (goldenseal, barberry, cat's claw, etc.).

alkaloids (unspecified).notes: Alkaloids are nitrogen-containing plant compounds found in hundreds of medicinal herbs. Common supplement examples include berberine (goldenseal, barberry), oxindole alkaloids (cat's claw), piperine (black pepper), and caffeine. When listed generically without a specific compound named, this indicates a total alkaloid fraction in an herbal extract. Bioavailability varies dramatically by subtype. Conservative scoring applies to unidentified fractions.

alkamides.description: Lipophilic isobutylamide compounds and the primary bioavailable active constituents of echinacea. CB2 receptor partial agonists with immunomodulatory activity.

alkamides (unspecified).notes: Alkamides (alkylamides) are lipophilic amide compounds and the primary bioavailable actives of echinacea. Unlike caffeic acid derivatives (cichoric acid, echinacoside) which show zero plasma detection, alkamides achieve ~50% oral bioavailability via passive transcellular diffusion (PMID 15901750, 15919096). Proposed mechanism is cannabinoid CB2 receptor partial agonism modulating innate immune cytokine production. Alkamide content is the most reliable quality marker for echinacea extract potency.

absorption_structured.notes: ~50% oral bioavailability for major isobutylamides; detected in plasma within 20-30 min (Woelkart 2005 PMID 15901750; Matthias 2005 PMID 15919096)

aloin.description: Anthraquinone C-glycoside from aloe vera latex, converted by gut bacteria to aloe-emodin. FDA removed aloe laxatives from OTC in 2002; NTP carcinogenicity concern.

aloin (unspecified).notes: Anthraquinone C-glycoside from aloe vera latex (not gel). Gut bacteria cleave the C-glycosidic bond releasing aloe-emodin, the proximal laxative agent. FDA removed aloe-based laxatives from OTC market in 2002; NTP 2-year rat study found clear evidence of carcinogenic activity from whole-leaf aloe extract (IARC Group 2B). Oral absorption ~5-7% as parent compound (PMC2788160). Not present in decolorized aloe gel products.

absorption_structured.notes: ~5-7% absorption (Caco-2 model, PMC2788160); primarily pro-drug converted by colonic bacteria to aloe-emodin

alpha_linolenic_acid.description: Essential plant-sourced omega-3 fatty acid (18:3n-3) found in flaxseed, chia, hemp, and walnuts. Precursor to EPA/DHA with limited conversion efficiency.

alpha-linolenic acid (unspecified).notes: Essential plant omega-3 (18:3n-3) from flaxseed (~57% ALA), chia (~63%), hemp (~17%), and walnuts. Intestinal absorption from oil form is ~90% via pancreatic lipase/bile micellarization, but conversion to active EPA is <8% and DHA <4% in most men, higher in women (PMID 12323090). High ALA intake associated with ~10% reduced coronary heart disease mortality risk. Bioavailability higher from oil or milled seed than whole seed (PMID 18689552).

absorption_structured.notes: ~85-96% intestinal absorption as fatty acid via standard lipid digestion; however conversion to EPA <8% and DHA <4% in men (PMID 12323090)

anthocyanins.description: Flavonoid pigments (glycosides of anthocyanidins) responsible for red/blue/purple colors in berries. Generic catch-all when source berry is not specified on label.

anthocyanins (unspecified).notes: Flavonoid pigments from dark berries (bilberry, elderberry, chokeberry, blueberry, black currant). Intact anthocyanin recovery in plasma is <2%, but C13-tracer human research (PMID 24650097) found ~12% metabolite recovery including colonic catabolites like protocatechuic acid. Supports vascular, cognitive, and anti-inflammatory pathways in RCTs. Source berry and standardization level influence potency.

absorption_structured.notes: Intact parent <2% by HPLC, but C13-tracer study found ~12.4% total metabolite recovery in humans (PMID 24650097); activity partly via colonic catabolites

cyanidin_3_glucoside.description: Most abundant anthocyanin in berries. Primary C13-tracer study compound with ~12% total metabolite bioavailability in humans.

cyanidin-3-glucoside (unspecified).notes: Most abundant and studied anthocyanin, found in elderberry, black rice, chokeberry, and blueberry. C13-tracer human study (PMID 24650097) found 12.4% total metabolite recovery — much higher than the <2% intact parent compound detected by HPLC. Metabolized to protocatechuic acid and phenolic acids via colonic microbiota. Anti-inflammatory, cardioprotective, and neuroprotective activity demonstrated in multiple preclinical models.

absorption_structured.notes: Intact C3G <2% by HPLC; C13-tracer human study showed 12.4% total metabolite recovery (PMID 24650097); active metabolites include protocatechuic acid

anthraquinones.description: Class of aromatic compounds found in senna, rhubarb, cascara, and aloe. Used as standardization markers for laxative herb extracts.

anthraquinones (unspecified).notes: Aromatic compounds from senna, cascara, rhubarb, and aloe used as laxative herb standardization markers. Parent glycosides (sennosides) are pro-drugs converted by colonic bacteria to active aglycones (rhein, emodin). Long-term use associated with melanosis coli and electrolyte imbalance. Total sennosides is the typical standardization metric for senna extracts.

absorption_structured.notes: Pro-drug class; parent glycosides poorly absorbed, converted by colonic bacteria to active aglycones (emodin, rhein, aloe-emodin)

apigenin extract.notes: Flavone from chamomile (Matricaria chamomilla), parsley, and celery. Bioavailability is form-dependent: aglycone powder yields <1% urinary recovery while chamomile extract standardized to apigenin-7-glucoside yields ~34% (PMID 35452808). Inhibits CD38 NAD+ase in vitro (IC50 ~10 µM, Escande 2013 PMID 23093624) and raises NAD+ in mouse models, though human RCT data is lacking. Also modulates GABA-A receptors for mild anxiolytic effect.

absorption_structured.notes: Highly form-dependent: aglycone powder <1% urinary metabolite recovery; chamomile extract (apigenin-7-glucoside) ~34% in human ADME study (PMID 35452808)

apigenin.description: Flavone from chamomile, parsley, and celery. CD38 inhibitor (preclinical), GABA-A modulator, emerging NAD+ research.

arbutin.description: Hydroquinone glycoside from bearberry (Arctostaphylos uva-ursi) leaves. Prodrug converted to antimicrobial hydroquinone in the urinary tract.

arbutin (unspecified).notes: Hydroquinone glycoside from bearberry (Arctostaphylos uva-ursi) leaves. Gut bacteria and liver enzymes hydrolyze it to free hydroquinone, which reaches the urinary tract as an antimicrobial agent. Human PK study (PMID 12162475) recovered ~65% as urinary hydroquinone conjugates. EMA supports short-term use (max 1 week) for mild lower UTI symptoms. Not suitable during pregnancy or long-term due to hydroquinone nephrotoxicity risk at high doses.

absorption_structured.notes: ~65-67% urinary recovery as hydroquinone conjugates in human crossover study (Schindler 2002, PMID 12162475); prodrug fully converted to hydroquinone

arctiin.description: Lignan glycoside from burdock (Arctium lappa) seeds. Prodrug converted by gut bacteria to arctigenin, the active anti-inflammatory aglycone.

arctiin (unspecified).notes: Lignan glycoside from burdock (Arctium lappa) seeds. Gut bacteria rapidly hydrolyze arctiin to arctigenin, the pharmacologically active aglycone with anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties (PMID 22499560). Modeled oral bioavailability ~58-75% for arctigenin in rats; no human PK data. Clinical evidence remains preclinical. Burdock has a long history of traditional use as a root vegetable and liver tonic.

absorption_structured.notes: Prodrug rapidly converted to arctigenin by gut microbiota; arctigenin absolute F ~58% in rats (PMID 25274606). Microbiome-dependent conversion, high individual variability expected in humans.

aspalathin.description: Unique C-glucosyl dihydrochalcone flavonoid found exclusively in rooibos (Aspalathus linearis). Emerging SGLT2 inhibition and insulin-sensitizing research.

aspalathin (unspecified).notes: Unique C-glucosyl dihydrochalcone found only in rooibos (Aspalathus linearis). Unlike most flavonoid glycosides, absorbed intact without deglycosylation (PMID 19083477), then methylated and glucuronidated. Human PK (PMID 19534535, 25212140) shows very low systemic exposure: 0.1-0.22% urinary recovery, plasma Tmax 1.5-3h. Unfermented (green) rooibos has 10x more aspalathin than fermented (red). Preclinical evidence for SGLT2 inhibition relevant to type 2 diabetes.

absorption_structured.notes: ~0.1-0.22% urinary recovery in humans (PMID 19534535, 25212140); absorbed intact as C-glycoside without deglycosylation (PMID 19083477)

Preclinical evidence for anti-inflammatory (TNF-α/NF-κB inhibition, PMID 15326552) and hepatoprotective effects (PMID 35961541). No human RCTs on isolated aucubin; benefits attributed to the full phytocomplex of plantain or vitex extracts.

Urinary recovery <1% of dose in humans (PMC5346430); betanin often undetectable in plasma. Structurally unrelated to anthocyanins — contain nitrogen in the chromophore. Preclinical antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and antihypertensive effects documented; human RCT evidence remains limited.

betulinic acid (unspecified).notes: Pentacyclic lupane-type triterpene primarily from birch bark (Betula spp., up to 34% as betulin precursor) and secondarily from chaga mushroom sclerotium. Poor oral bioavailability estimated <5% due to low aqueous solubility. Preclinical anti-cancer activity (selective apoptosis in tumor cells) and anti-HIV research (blocks viral maturation). No human clinical trials on oral betulinic acid. Birch bark extract is the richer source; chaga accumulates it from its birch host.

absorption_structured.notes: Estimated <5% by structural analogy to ursolic acid and oleanolic acid; no direct human PK data. Poor aqueous solubility (BCS Class IV)

bisabolol (unspecified).notes: Sesquiterpene alcohol from German chamomile (Matricaria chamomilla) and candeia tree (Eremanthus erythropappus). Natural enantiomer is (-)-alpha-bisabolol (levomenol). High GI absorption predicted from ADMET modeling (PMID 35405982); no human oral bioavailability published. Anti-inflammatory via NF-kB and COX inhibition; GI mucoprotective in rodent models (PMID 19663933). Oral supplement use targets GI comfort and systemic anti-inflammatory.

absorption_structured.notes: High GI absorption predicted by ADMET modeling (Lipinski-compliant, no P-gp efflux, PMID 35405982); no published human oral bioavailability. LogP ~4.2.

caffeine.description: Purine alkaloid (1,3,7-trimethylxanthine) and adenosine A1/A2A receptor antagonist. ~99% oral bioavailability. Most consumed psychoactive compound globally.

absorption_structured.notes: ~99-100% absolute oral bioavailability (PMID 6832208, Denaro 1983); no significant first-pass effect; Tmax ~30 min

caffeine anhydrous.notes: Synthetic crystalline caffeine with near-complete oral absorption (~99%, PMID 6832208). Adenosine A1/A2A receptor antagonist — blocks sleep-promoting adenosine, raising dopamine and norepinephrine signaling. Tmax ~30 min, half-life 3-5h (CYP1A2-driven, 2-10h individual range). Improves alertness, reaction time, and endurance at 3-6 mg/kg. FDA GRAS.

absorption_structured.notes: Same ~99% total bioavailability as anhydrous (PMID 6832208); slightly slower Tmax ~40-60 min due to tannin matrix

caffeine (natural source).notes: Botanical caffeine from green tea, guarana, or green coffee — chemically identical to anhydrous but bound to tannins/polyphenols. Same ~99% total oral bioavailability (PMID 6832208); slightly slower Tmax (~40-60 min). Guarana co-delivers catechins; green tea provides L-theanine which attenuates jitteriness. Adenosine receptor antagonist mechanism identical to synthetic.

Used as standardization markers on echinacea, artichoke, and green coffee extracts.

absorption_structured.notes: Highly compound-specific: free caffeic acid ~95% absorbed (PMID 11208940); chlorogenic acid ~29% SI absorption (PMID 20226754); cichoric acid not detected in plasma (PMID 15919096)

catalpol (unspecified).notes: Iridoid glycoside and primary bioactive of Rehmannia glutinosa (Di Huang), a TCM kidney/liver tonic herb. Oral bioavailability ~67% in rats (PMID 26031219); rapid Tmax ~0.5h; short half-life ~1.2h. Crosses the blood-brain barrier (CSF detected, PMID 22677654). Gut microbiota cleave the glucose moiety (PMID 26741989). Preclinical neuroprotection via ERK-CREB-BDNF pathway (PMC7023090). No human RCTs on isolated catalpol.

absorption_structured.notes: ~67% absolute oral bioavailability in rats (PMID 26031219); rapid Tmax ~0.5h; short t1/2 ~1.2h; crosses BBB (PMID 22677654)

chlorogenic acids (unspecified).notes: Chlorogenic acids are the main polyphenols in green coffee bean extract, absorbed ~29% in the small intestine (PMID 20226754). RCTs at 200-400 mg/day show modest reductions in fasting blood glucose and post-meal insulin. Branded extracts Svetol and CoffeeGenic are standardized to 45-50% CGA. Parent compound has short plasma half-life (~1 h) and is extensively metabolized to caffeic acid, ferulic acid, and gut-microbial catabolites.

absorption_structured.notes: ~29% small intestinal absorption in ileostomy study (PMID 20226754); remainder undergoes colonic microbial conversion to ferulic/dihydrocaffeic acid

chlorophyll.description: Green pigment supplement — commercial forms are mostly sodium copper chlorophyllin (semi-synthetic), not natural chlorophyll. Aflatoxin chemoprevention evidence.

absorption_structured.notes: Water-soluble; reaches micromolar serum levels (PMID 19952359) but no absolute oral bioavailability% established

copper chlorophyllin.notes: Sodium copper chlorophyllin (SCC) — the dominant commercial 'liquid chlorophyll' form. Semi-synthetic: Mg replaced by Cu, phytol tail cleaved. Water-soluble with confirmed serum absorption (micromolar range). Reduces aflatoxin B1 absorption by 40-60% in human volunteers (PMID 19952359). Claimed deodorant and detox benefits are mostly low-evidence.

absorption_structured.notes: Fat-soluble; estimated 1-3% absorption in animal studies; requires micellarization with dietary fat

natural chlorophyll.notes: Natural fat-soluble chlorophyll a/b from plant sources. Very poorly absorbed (~1-3% in animal studies); unstable and less commercially prevalent than copper chlorophyllin. Requires dietary fat for micellarization. Most 'chlorophyll' supplements are actually copper chlorophyllin, not natural chlorophyll.

sodium magnesium chlorophyllin.absorption: low (micromolar serum levels, analogous to copper chlorophyllin)
sodium magnesium chlorophyllin.notes: Water-soluble chlorophyllin derivative in which the magnesium center is retained (unlike copper chlorophyllin where Mg is replaced by Cu). Saponification removes the phytol tail to confer water solubility. The Mg-porphyrin complex is less stable in aqueous media than Cu-chlorophyllin and is rarely used commercially. Limited human pharmacokinetic data; absorption is presumed low (micromolar serum levels) analogous to copper chlorophyllin. No distinguishing clinical benefits over other chlorophyllin forms have been demonstrated in peer-reviewed literature.

absorption_structured.notes: low; micromolar serum levels, analogous to copper chlorophyllin

Dose escalation shows saturation absorption — no benefit above ~500 mg (PMID 31552981).

fiber (unspecified).notes: Generic dietary fiber without type specification. Fiber acts via luminal mechanisms (viscosity, fermentation to SCFAs, bile acid sequestration) not systemic absorption. Efficacy is type-dependent: soluble viscous fibers (glucomannan, psyllium) have strongest lipid/glycemic evidence. See konjac glucomannan form for specific clinical data.

absorption_structured.notes: Fiber acts via luminal viscosity mechanism, not systemic absorption

konjac glucomannan.notes: Highly viscous soluble fiber from Amorphophallus konjac root. Meta-analysis of 12 RCTs (n=370, PMID 28356275): LDL-C -10%, non-HDL-C -7% at ~3 g/day. Additional meta-analysis confirms modest weight loss (PMID 24533610). Improves glycemia in T2DM (PMID 10372241). Mechanism: luminal viscosity reduces bile acid reabsorption and slows glucose absorption. Effective dose 3-5 g/day with adequate water before meals.

absorption_structured.notes: Soluble fiber; mechanism is luminal viscosity and SCFA fermentation, not systemic absorption

flavonoids (unspecified).notes: Generic polyphenol term covering flavones, flavonols, flavanones, isoflavones, anthocyanins, flavan-3-ols. Bioavailability ranges from <1% (anthocyanins) to ~43% (isoflavones/gallic acid) (PMID 15640486). No clinical evidence applies to "flavonoids" as a class — efficacy is entirely subclass-specific. See quercetin, rutin, hesperidin, EGCG, citrus_bioflavonoids for specific data.

absorption_structured.notes: 0.3-43% depending on subclass (PMID 15640486); isoflavones highest, anthocyanins lowest

DB-RCT (PMID 25369831, n=40): formononetin-enriched red clover isoflavones reduced LDL 12% vs 2% placebo (P=0.005). ERbeta preferential binding; lower ERalpha affinity vs genistein.

beta-sitosterol.absorption: very poor (~0.41% absolute oral F); acts locally in GI to inhibit cholesterol absorption
beta-sitosterol.notes: Most abundant plant sterol (campesterol and stigmasterol also present). Definitive human PK: absolute oral bioavailability 0.41% (Heinemann 2012, PMID 22826463). Works by competitive inhibition of cholesterol absorption at intestinal brush border — efficacy independent of systemic absorption. 2-3 g/day reduces LDL-C by 6-15%.

absorption_structured.notes: Absolute F 0.41% (PMID 22826463); locally-acting in GI lumen

mixed phytosterols.absorption: very poor systemically (~0.5-2%); locally active in GI tract
mixed phytosterols.notes: Mixture of beta-sitosterol, campesterol, and stigmasterol. Systemic absorption <2%. Competes with dietary and biliary cholesterol for micellar incorporation at the intestinal brush border. 2-3 g/day reduces LDL-C 6-15% in meta-analyses. Efficacy is independent of systemic bioavailability.

absorption_structured.notes: Systemic F <2%; efficacy is via local GI cholesterol absorption inhibition

stigmasterol.notes: Less abundant phytosterol. Even lower systemic absorption than beta-sitosterol. Contributes to cholesterol absorption inhibition in mixed phytosterol preparations. Limited standalone clinical data.

absorption_structured.notes: Very low systemic F; efficacy via GI-local cholesterol competition

campesterol.absorption: very poor (~1.9% systemic, higher than sitosterol); locally active
campesterol.notes: Plant sterol with slightly higher absorption than beta-sitosterol (~1.9% vs 0.41%). Absorbed campesterol is efficiently excreted via ABCG5/G8 transporters. Contributes to cholesterol absorption inhibition in mixed phytosterol preparations.

absorption_structured.notes: ~1.9% systemic F (higher than sitosterol but still very low)

phytosterols (unspecified).notes: Plant-derived sterols (beta-sitosterol, campesterol, stigmasterol) that competitively inhibit intestinal cholesterol absorption. EFSA/FDA-approved health claim for LDL reduction at 1.5-3 g/day. Form unspecified; conservative scoring applied.

phytosterols.rxcui_note: No RxNorm concept for supplement ingredient

piperine.description: Black pepper alkaloid; primary bioavailability enhancer (BioPerine)

piperine (unspecified).notes: Primary pungent alkaloid of black pepper (Piper nigrum). BioPerine (Sabinsa, 95%+ piperine) is the patented standardized form. Inhibits CYP3A4, CYP1A2, P-glycoprotein, and UGTs — increases curcumin AUC ~20-fold (PMID 9619120). Enhances beta-carotene absorption ~2x at 5 mg (PMC7353321). Rapid transcellular passive diffusion; high Caco-2 permeability (PMID 9536651). Standard dose: 5-20 mg as adjuvant.
aliases: Black Pepper fruit (Piper nigrum) supercritical extract
Certified Organic Black Pepper (Piper nigrum) supercritical extract
Black Pepper fruit Supercritical CO2 extract
Black Pepper (Piper nigrum) fruit supercritical CO2 extract
essence of pure black pepper (fruit) oil
essence of pure black pepper oil extract

absorption_structured.notes: High apparent permeability in Caco-2 (PMID 9536651); Tmax ~15 min rats; human t1/2 13-16h (PMC8796742)

polyphenols (unspecified).notes: Largest class of plant secondary metabolites (~8,000+ compounds) including flavonoids, phenolic acids, stilbenes, lignans, and tannins. Bioavailability is highly structure-dependent: anthocyanins ~0.1%, flavonols ~1-10%, isoflavones up to 60%. Gut microbiota metabolizes larger polymers (PMC9324685). Generic 'total polyphenols' label claims do not specify absorbed fractions.

absorption_structured.notes: Highly structure-dependent; anthocyanins ~0.1%, isoflavones ~60%; gut microbiota dominant for polymers (PMC9324685)

Not absorbed intact — act via Dectin-1/TLR receptor engagement in Peyer's patches and prebiotic SCFA production (PMC10902450, PMC8308413). Specific polysaccharides (beta-glucan, fucoidan, arabinogalactan) have dedicated IQM parents with more precise scoring.

proanthocyanidins (unspecified).notes: Condensed tannins from grape seed, pine bark, apple, cranberry, cacao. Oligomers (dimers-tetramers) absorbed in small intestine at ~10-30% (PMC10215713). High polymers reach colon and are metabolized to phenylvalerolactones. Antioxidant potency 50x vitamin E in vitro. Cardiovascular, skin collagen, and venous insufficiency support documented. See 'opc' for grape/pine oligomers; 'pac' for cranberry Type-A.

Human PK confirms rapid absorption, peak at ~1h (PMID 16884345). Cardioprotective, antioxidant, neuroprotective, and estrogenic agonist (PMC9686758). Used as IV puerarin for cerebrovascular disease in China.

Traditional use as digestive bitter/appetite stimulant. No human oral bioavailability data; cytotoxic at pharmacological doses. Supplement use primarily as bitter standardization marker.

rosmarinic acid (unspecified).notes: Hydroxycinnamic acid ester ubiquitous in Lamiaceae (rosemary, lemon balm, sage, perilla). Oral bioavailability 0.9-1.7% in rats; extensively metabolized to caffeic/ferulic acid conjugates which retain activity (PMID 15120569, PMID 33285594). Human peak plasma at 1h. Anti-inflammatory via NF-kB/COX-2, antioxidant, cognitive support. Not rosemary-specific.

ruscogenin.description: Steroidal saponin from Ruscus aculeatus (butcher's broom); venotonic

ruscogenin (unspecified).notes: Steroidal saponin aglycone from Ruscus aculeatus (butcher's broom). Unlike most saponins, demonstrates high GI absorption and good oral bioavailability (PMID 23896689). Vasoconstriction via norepinephrine release. Standardized extract reduced leg volume in 166-patient CVI RCT (PMID 12040966). European Commission monograph supports use for venous insufficiency and hemorrhoids.

Oral bioavailability ~1-2% in animals due to tight junction barrier and biliary excretion (PMC10538146, PMID 21569331). Potent antioxidant; cardioprotective via HO-1 upregulation and arginase inhibition (PMID 22442118). IV puerarin used clinically in China. Oral efficacy limited — phospholipid complexes may enhance absorption.

Lipid nanocapsules improve oral bioavailability.

Oral F ~15.6% for schisandrin A (PMID 29342955); schisandrin B F 19-55% sex-dependent with enterohepatic recirculation (PMID 28833320). Hepatoprotective via CYP modulation and antioxidant defense upregulation. TCM adaptogen with 2000-year use history (PMC11984061). Extract bioavailability exceeds isolated compound.

sesamin.description: Furofuran lignan from sesame seeds; CYP inhibitor and cardiovascular support

sesamin (unspecified).notes: Primary furofuran lignan of sesame (Sesamum indicum). Meta-analysis of 7 RCTs (n=212, 60-200 mg/day) found modest reductions in SBP, triglycerides, and body weight (PMC8931661). CYP1A2/2C9 inhibition elevates gamma-tocopherol; delta-5-desaturase inhibition shifts FA profile (PMID 26151734). SNEDDS formulations improve bioavailability 2-3x (PMID 32650503).

absorption_structured.notes: Rat ADME 37.5% urinary recovery (PMID 27991716); SNEDDS enhances F 2-3x (PMID 32650503)

sesamolin (minor lignan).notes: Minor furofuran lignan in sesame (~25% of sesamin content). Oral bioavailability <25%; acid-labile under gastric conditions (PMC8510241). In vivo converted partially to sesamol (antioxidant). No standalone clinical trials; functions as minor constituent in sesame lignan complexes.

absorption_structured.notes: <25% absorbed; acid-labile in stomach; no urinary metabolites detected (PMC8510241)

silica (unspecified).notes: Dietary silica (SiO2) as silicon source for collagen cross-linking, bone mineralization, and connective tissue integrity. Amorphous dietary silica: 27-44% oral bioavailability by urinary Si excretion (PMID 19356271, PMC2744664). Distinct from silicon dioxide used as excipient flow agent in capsules.

absorption_structured.notes: Amorphous dietary silica: 27-44% urinary Si recovery (PMID 19356271, PMC2744664)

bamboo silica.absorption: 27-44% (plant-matrix organic silica; similar to horsetail-derived forms)
bamboo silica.notes: Silica (SiO2) derived from bamboo (Bambusa vulgaris, Phyllostachys edulis) stem or leaf extract — one of the richest botanical silica sources (~70% SiO2 by dry weight). Plant-matrix organic silica dissolves as orthosilicic acid (OSA) in gut fluid, with absorption estimated 27–44%, comparable to horsetail-derived stabilized orthosilicic acid (Jugdaohsingh 2002, J. Nutr.). Supports collagen synthesis, connective tissue, hair, and nail structural integrity. Natural designation reflects botanical plant origin.

absorption_structured.notes: 27-44%; plant-matrix organic silica dissolves as orthosilicic acid

vicine.description: Pyrimidine glucoside from fava beans; G6PD hemolytic risk — safety marker, not therapeutic

vicine (unspecified).notes: Pyrimidine nucleoside glucoside from fava beans (Vicia faba), up to 0.4% dry seed weight. In G6PD-deficient individuals, gut bacterial beta-glucosidase hydrolyzes vicine to divicine, generating reactive oxygen species causing hemolytic anemia (favism, PMID 23718910). No therapeutic benefit — appears on supplement labels as a contaminant marker in fava bean protein products.

absorption_structured.notes: Moderate absorption (~50%); hydrolyzed by gut beta-glucosidase to divicine aglycone — the toxic moiety in G6PD deficiency

Cytotoxic and cardiotoxic via cell membrane disruption (PMID 10535766). Clinically administered subcutaneously in integrative oncology (Iscador, Helixor) — NOT orally bioavailable, degraded by GI proteases. Appears on labels as quality/safety marker for mistletoe extracts.

absorption_structured.notes: Essentially zero oral bioavailability — degraded by gastrointestinal proteases; only effective via subcutaneous injection

niagen (nicotinamide riboside chloride).notes: Patented, highly pure form of nicotinamide riboside chloride by ChromaDex (TruNiagen). The most clinically studied NR form with multiple human trials showing dose-dependent NAD+ elevation. Trammell 2016 (PMID:27720903) demonstrated 142% NAD+ increase at 1000 mg. However, like all oral NR, most is converted to nicotinic acid by gut microbiota before contributing to NAD+ synthesis via Preiss-Handler pathway (Science Advances 2024). High inter-individual variability in NR blood levels (PMC5718430).

absorption_structured.notes: Most clinically studied NR form; variable inter-individual response (PMC5718430)

nicotinamide riboside (unspecified).notes: Form not specified; conservative scoring applied. Consider using a specific form for better bioavailability.

beta-nicotinamide mononucleotide.notes: Direct NAD+ precursor. Oral NMN effectively raises blood NAD+ at 250-500 mg/day, but most undergoes extensive first-pass hepatic metabolism and gut microbiota-mediated deamidation to nicotinic acid (Science Advances 2024, Kim et al.). Only a small portion is directly absorbed intact from the small intestine (PMC11205942). Rapidly absorbed when taken orally with detectable plasma increases within minutes in animal models, but human systemic bioavailability of intact NMN remains unquantified.

absorption_structured.notes: Extensive first-pass metabolism; most converted to NA (PMC11205942)

nicotinamide mononucleotide (unspecified.notes: Form not specified; conservative scoring applied. Consider using a specific form for better bioavailability.

trans-pterostilbene.notes: Dimethylated analog of resveratrol (from blueberries, grapes). 4x higher oral bioavailability than resveratrol (~80% vs ~20%) due to methyl groups improving lipophilicity and metabolic stability. pTeroPure is the leading branded form. Anti-aging, cardiovascular, and cognitive benefits at 50-250 mg/day.

sarsaparilla root extract.notes: Smilax officinalis root extract. Traditional use for skin conditions, joint inflammation, and as a 'blood purifier.' Contains steroidal saponins (sarsaponin, smilagenin). Limited clinical evidence. Historical use in syphilis treatment. May have mild anti-inflammatory and diuretic effects.

sarsaparilla (unspecified).notes: Form not specified; conservative scoring applied. Consider using a specific form for better bioavailability.

sarsaparilla.rxcui_note: No RxNorm concept for supplement ingredient

enada nadh.absorption: poor (~5% intestinal absorption; enteric coating protects from acid degradation)
enada nadh.notes: Enteric-coated stabilized NADH (Birkmayer patent). Gut sac experiments show ~5% concentration-independent absorption (PMID 18508468). Oral NADH did not increase urinary nicotinamide excretion, suggesting decomposition in digestive organs (PMID 16802695). Enteric coating protects against gastric acid but no published human PK quantifies absolute F. CFS trial (PMID 10071523) showed marginal clinical benefit. Not sublingual — this is an oral enteric-coated tablet.

absorption_structured.notes: ~5% in gut sac models (PMID 18508468); no human PK data; enteric coating provides some protection

panmol nadh.notes: Alternative stabilized NADH formulation. Same fundamental limitations as all oral NADH: extremely acid-labile, ~5% intestinal absorption in gut sac models (PMID 18508468). Less studied than Enada. Score reflects uncertainty and limited clinical evidence.

absorption_structured.notes: Estimated similar to Enada; no specific PK data for Panmol formulation

generic nadh.notes: Uncoated/generic NADH. Rapidly degraded by gastric acid (pH <7). Without enteric coating, even less NADH survives to reach the small intestine. Gut sac absorption ~5% for NADH that does reach the intestine (PMID 18508468). Effective oral F is essentially negligible.

absorption_structured.notes: No acid protection; most degraded in stomach before reaching absorption site

larch arabinogalactan powder.notes: Immune-supporting prebiotic fiber. Increases beneficial gut bacteria. 10g daily shows prebiotic efficacy.

larch_arabinogalactan.cui_note: No confirmed exact UMLS concept for larch arabinogalactan as a supplement ingredient.

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|fiber|Arabinogalactan | 3540 (Number of products:104)

lactobacillus salivarius ha-118.notes: Specific strain with oral and gut health benefits. Produces bacteriocins with antimicrobial properties.

generic lactobacillus salivarius.notes: Non-strain-specific L. salivarius entry. Benefits may vary significantly without strain identification. Some strains (e.g., HA-118, UCC118) have antimicrobial bacteriocin production. Look for strain designation.

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|bacteria|Lactobacillus salivarius | 2577 (Number of products:623)

deer antler velvet extract.absorption: negligible for active peptides (~0% for oral IGF-1; amino acids/minerals absorbed normally)
deer antler velvet extract.notes: Complex mixture of peptides, amino acids, collagen, chondroitin, minerals. Oral IGF-1 (7.6 kDa peptide) is completely degraded by gastric acid and proteases — 0% oral bioavailability for intact growth factors. Controlled human study found no significant change in serum IGF-1, GH, or cortisol vs placebo. OPSS review: 'no scientific evidence supports these claims.' Amino acid and mineral content may provide some nutritional value, but the marketing premise (oral growth factor delivery) is not supported by pharmacology.

absorption_structured.notes: ~0% for peptide hormones (IGF-1 degraded by proteases); only amino acids/minerals absorbed

standardized deer antler extract.absorption: negligible for active peptides; standardization does not overcome oral peptide degradation
standardized deer antler extract.notes: Standardized extract claiming specific IGF-1 content. Standardization cannot overcome the fundamental pharmacological barrier: oral peptide hormones (>1 kDa) are completely degraded by GI proteases. No technology in this product category delivers intact IGF-1 across the gut barrier. Same null clinical results as generic antler velvet. Score reflects amino acid/mineral nutritional value only.

absorption_structured.notes: Peptide hormones cannot survive oral GI transit; standardization irrelevant to bioavailability

deer_antler_velvet.rxcui_note: No RxNorm concept for supplement ingredient

gymnema leaf extract.absorption: poor (~14% oral F in rats; poor Caco-2 permeability, high efflux)
gymnema leaf extract.notes: Gymnema sylvestre standardized leaf extract (typically 25-75% gymnemic acids). Gymnemagenin absolute oral F ~14% in rats with poor Caco-2 permeability (1.31e-6 cm/s) and high efflux ratio. Rapid microsomal metabolism (t1/2 ~7 min). No human PK data. Some activity may be gut-mediated — gymnemic acids block sweet taste receptors and may inhibit intestinal glucose absorption at the brush border. Traditional Ayurvedic 'sugar destroyer' (gurmar).

absorption_structured.notes: ~14% in rats; poor Caco-2 permeability; high efflux; no human PK data

gymnema leaf powder.notes: Ground Gymnema sylvestre leaves ('gurmar' = sugar destroyer). Contains gymnemic acids at lower concentration than standardized extracts. Traditional Ayurvedic herb for glucose control. Gymnemic acids temporarily block sweet taste receptors on the tongue and may inhibit intestinal glucose absorption.

gymnema sylvestre (unspecified).notes: Form not specified; conservative scoring applied. Consider using a specific form for better bioavailability.

gymnema_sylvestre.rxcui_note: No RxNorm concept found via GSRS lookup

pregnenolone.notes: Neurosteroid precursor synthesized from diosgenin (wild yam) via Marker degradation — technically semi-synthetic despite plant starting material. DrugBank characterizes as 'low bioavailability, high metabolism.' Intranasal F ~23%; oral F likely 20-40% (no definitive human IV/oral study). At 175 mg oral dose approximately doubles serum pregnenolone over 4-8h. Chronic dosing (300-500 mg/day) achieves stable elevated plasma levels (PMC11595496). Used for cognitive support, mood, and neurological conditions.

absorption_structured.notes: Estimated 20-40%; no published absolute oral F study; intranasal F ~23% as reference

micronized pregnenolone.absorption: moderate (~25-45% estimated; micronization improves dissolution rate)
micronized pregnenolone.notes: Micronized pregnenolone with reduced particle size for improved dissolution and potentially higher oral bioavailability. Still subject to same first-pass hepatic metabolism as standard form. No published human PK comparing micronized vs standard pregnenolone. Semi-synthetic from diosgenin (same as standard form).

absorption_structured.notes: Estimated slight improvement over standard form from micronization; no comparative human PK data

metabolic_relationships.name: NUCLEAR RECEPTOR SUBFAMILY 1 GROUP I MEMBER 2

berberine hcl.absorption: very poor (~0.37-0.68% absolute F); P-gp efflux substrate
berberine hcl.notes: Plant alkaloid (Berberis, Coptis, Phellodendron). Absolute oral bioavailability ~0.37% in animal models (PMC8964367). P-glycoprotein efflux is major limiting factor; heavily ionized at physiological pH. Despite abysmal systemic F, clinically effective for glucose/lipid metabolism — effects likely gut-mediated (microbiome modulation, intestinal AMPK activation, bile acid pathway). Enhanced formulations (LipoMicel, phytosome) achieve ~6x improvement but still <5% F (PMC10675484). 500-1500 mg/day standard dose.

absorption_structured.notes: Absolute F ~0.37-0.68% (PMC8964367); P-gp efflux substrate; clinical effects are gut-mediated

liposomal berberine.notes: Berberine encapsulated in phospholipid liposomes to enhance absorption. Standard berberine HCl has only ~0.37% oral F; liposomal delivery may improve 3-6x via lymphatic absorption pathway (PMC10675484). Still experimental — limited head-to-head human PK data vs standard berberine. Premium pricing.

berberine sulfate.absorption: very poor (~0.37-0.68% absolute F); P-gp efflux substrate
berberine sulfate.notes: Alternative berberine salt used in the literature and on supplement labels. No validated human PK advantage over standard oral berberine hydrochloride was confirmed, so it is scored equivalently to other standard oral berberine salts.

absorption_structured.notes: Absolute F ~0.37-0.68% (PMC8964367); P-gp efflux substrate; clinical effects are gut-mediated

alpha-amylase enzyme.notes: Digestive enzyme that breaks down starch and carbohydrates into sugars. Essential for carbohydrate digestion. Bioactive functional ingredient that supports digestive health.

alpha_amylase.rxcui_note: No RxNorm concept for supplement ingredient

dicalcium phosphate dihydrate.notes: Calcium and phosphorus supplement providing both essential minerals. Good bioavailability and well-tolerated. Used as both nutritional supplement and tablet excipient.

di-calcium phosphate (unspecified).notes: Dicalcium phosphate (DCP) provides both calcium (~29%) and phosphorus (~23%). Used as tablet filler and calcium source. Absorption comparable to milk calcium at ~30% efficiency.

l-methionine.notes: Essential amino acid required for protein synthesis and methylation processes. Important for liver health, detoxification, and S-adenosylmethionine (SAMe) production.

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|Amino Acid|METHIONINE | 2065 (Number of products:1257)

coenzymated.notes: Active coenzyme forms of B-vitamins for enhanced bioavailability and cellular utilization. Pre-converted forms ready for immediate use.

coenzymated complex (unspecified).notes: Pre-activated B-vitamin complex containing coenzymated forms (P5P, methylcobalamin, MTHF, riboflavin-5-phosphate). Bypasses hepatic activation step; may benefit those with genetic polymorphisms affecting vitamin metabolism.

coenzymated_complex.cui_note: No confirmed ingredient-level UMLS concept for coenzymated B-vitamin complex branding.
coenzymated_complex.rxcui_note: No RxNorm concept for supplement ingredient

phylloquinone.notes: Plant-derived vitamin K1 (phylloquinone). The primary dietary form, essential for blood clotting factor synthesis. Absorbed from the GI tract with dietary fat. Only ~5-25% converts to K2 in the body.

micellized k1.notes: K1 processed into water-soluble micelles for significantly enhanced absorption. Standard K1 is poorly absorbed (~10-20% from food); micellization can dramatically improve this. Valuable for people with fat malabsorption or those taking K1 without a fat-containing meal.

gsrs.dsld_info_raw: Dietary Supplement Label Database|Vitamin|Vitamin K | 208 (Number of products:446)

5-htp extract.notes: 5-Hydroxytryptophan from Griffonia simplicifolia seeds. Mean oral bioavailability 69.2% (range 47-84%) at steady state (Magnussen 1980, PMID 6966118). Absorbed via amino acid transporters in upper intestine (Tmax 1.8-3.3h). Serotonin precursor used for mood support, sleep, and appetite regulation at 100-300 mg/day. With carbidopa co-admin, F~48% but therapeutic exposure increases due to reduced peripheral decarboxylation.

5-htp (unspecified).notes: Direct precursor to serotonin; bypasses rate-limiting tryptophan conversion step. Source/form unspecified.

absorption_structured.notes: moderate — specific extract form scores higher

5_htp.rxcui_note: No RxNorm concept found via GSRS lookup

alcar.absorption: poor (~10-20% oral F; similar to L-carnitine supplements)
alcar.notes: Acetylated L-carnitine with improved BBB penetration over L-carnitine. However, oral bioavailability is only 10-20% (comparable to L-carnitine at 14-18%, Rebouche 2004, PMID 15591001). ALCAR actually showed 7.7x lower delta-AUC than L-carnitine in head-to-head PK study (PMID 19178874). Despite low F, clinically effective for cognitive support and neuropathy at 1500-3000 mg/day, likely due to sufficient carnitine pool loading at high oral doses.

absorption_structured.notes: 10-20% oral F (PMID 15591001, 19178874); ALCAR delta-AUC 7.7x lower than L-carnitine

acetyl-l-carnitine (unspecified).notes: Form not specified; conservative scoring applied. Consider using a specific form for better bioavailability.

nac powder.absorption: very poor (~6-10% oral F; extensive first-pass metabolism)
nac powder.notes: Gold-standard cysteine/glutathione precursor. Oral bioavailability is only 6-10% (Borgstrom 1986, PMID 3360052; StatPearls NBK537183). Rapidly deacetylated to cysteine, which feeds GSH synthesis. Despite poor PK, clinically effective for mucolysis, acetaminophen toxicity (IV), and psychiatric applications (1200-2400 mg/day oral). Efficacy at low F suggests sufficient cysteine delivery even from small absorbed fraction.

absorption_structured.notes: 6-10% oral F (PMID 3360052); extensive first-pass deacetylation to cysteine

nac sustained-release.absorption: poor (~8-15% oral F estimated; sustained release may reduce first-pass peak)
nac sustained-release.notes: Sustained-release NAC formulation designed to reduce GI side effects and potentially improve exposure. Oral bioavailability may be marginally better than immediate-release (6-10%) by avoiding saturation of first-pass metabolism. Limited head-to-head PK data vs IR. Same cysteine/GSH delivery mechanism as standard NAC.

absorption_structured.notes: Estimated slightly better than IR NAC (6-10%) via reduced first-pass saturation; limited data

metabolic_relationships.name: TUMOR NECROSIS FACTOR-.ALPHA. HUMAN (PLASMA DERIVED, SOLUBLE FORM)

ginger root powder.absorption: moderate (matrix-bound gingerols slowly released; same conjugation issue)
ginger root powder.notes: Whole ginger root powder with gingerols embedded in plant matrix. Lower gingerol concentration than extracts but same fundamental PK: any absorbed gingerols are completely conjugated before reaching systemic circulation (PMID 18708382). Slower release from plant matrix may provide more sustained GI exposure. Traditional anti-nausea and digestive use well-supported clinically.

absorption_structured.notes: Matrix-bound release; lower gingerol concentration; same conjugation fate as extracts

ginger extract standardized.absorption: moderate (rapidly absorbed but 100% conjugated; no free gingerols in plasma)
ginger extract standardized.notes: Hydroethanolic standardized ginger extract (typically 5-10% gingerols). Same PK as supercritical: gingerols absorbed rapidly but undergo complete Phase II glucuronidation — no free gingerols in plasma (Zick et al. 2008, PMID 18708382). Clinical anti-emetic and anti-inflammatory effects may be mediated by metabolites or local GI activity. Extraction method does not change the Phase II metabolism bottleneck.
aliases: Ginger Rhizome Extract, Ginger Root Extract

absorption_structured.notes: Rapid GI absorption but complete first-pass conjugation; 0% free gingerol systemically

ginger supercritical extract.absorption: moderate (rapidly absorbed but 100% conjugated; no free gingerols in plasma)
ginger supercritical extract.notes: High-purity CO2 extract preserving volatile gingerols and shogaols. Gingerols are rapidly absorbed from GI tract (Tmax 30-80 min) but undergo complete Phase II conjugation (glucuronidation via intestinal UGT enzymes) — no free 6-,8-,10-gingerol or 6-shogaol detected in plasma at any dose up to 2g (Zick et al. 2008, PMID 18708382; PMC3160151). About 50% of 6-gingerol excreted as glucuronides in bile. Conjugate metabolites may retain partial bioactivity.
aliases: organic Ginger root supercritical CO2 extract

absorption_structured.notes: GI absorption is rapid/near-complete, but 100% first-pass conjugation; 0% free gingerol in systemic circulation (PMID 18708382)

ginger (unspecified).notes: Form not specified; conservative scoring applied. Consider using a specific form for better bioavailability.

ginger essential oil.notes: Steam-distilled essential oil of Zingiber officinale; rich in gingerols, shogaols, and sesquiterpenes. Well-absorbed via mucosal routes.

shogaols (dried ginger concentrate).notes: Shogaols (6-, 8-, 10-shogaol) are dehydration products of gingerols, enriched in dried/heat-processed ginger. More lipophilic than gingerols — higher GI absorption but same Phase II glucuronidation; no free shogaols in systemic circulation (PMID 18708382). Superior anti-inflammatory potency vs gingerols in vitro (COX/LOX/NF-kB). Rat interconversion PK: gingerol <-> shogaol in vivo (PMC11187260).

absorption_structured.notes: High lipophilic GI absorption; detected as glucuronide conjugates (PMID 30546874); no free shogaol systemically
